description: Dialogue between Prince Arjuna and Lord Krishna on duty, self-realization, and devotion.

chapters.name: Arjuna Vishada Yoga
chapters.summary: Arjuna experiences intense grief, despondency, and anxiety on seeing his relatives, uncles, cousins, and brothers on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. He refuses to fight them. Addresses family conflict, disputes with brothers/cousins, feeling unloved or betrayed by relatives, grief over family relations, moral crises, and fear of hurting family members.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “1.1 The King Dhritarashtra asked: "O Sanjaya! What happened on the sacred battlefield of Kurukshetra, when my people gathered against the Pandavas?"” and develops toward “1.20 Then beholding the sons of Dhritarashtra, drawn up on the battle- field, ready to fight, Arjuna, whose flag bore the Hanuman,”

This passage begins with “1.21 Raising his bow, spoke this to the Lord Shri Krishna: O Infallible! Lord of the earth! Please draw up my chariot betwixt the two armies,” and develops toward “1.40 The destruction of our kindred means the destruction of the traditions of our ancient lineage, and when these are lost, irreligion will overrun…”

This passage begins with “1.41 When irreligion spreads, the women of the house begin to stray; when they lose their purity, adulteration of the stock follows.” and develops toward “1.47 Sanjaya said: "Having spoken thus, in the midst of the armies, Arjuna sank on the seat of the chariot, casting away his bow and arrow;…”

chapters.name: Sankhya Yoga
chapters.summary: Krishna teaches the eternity of the Soul, overcoming grief and fear of death, performing selfless action (Karma Yoga), mind control, rising above emotional hurt, and traits of a person of steady wisdom. Addresses overthinking, exam stress, anxiety about results, feeling overwhelmed, bereavement, loss of loved ones, and relationship detachment.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “2.1 Sanjaya then told how the Lord Shri Krishna, seeing Arjuna overwhelmed with compassion, his eyes dimmed with flowing tears and full of despondency, consoled him:” and develops toward “2.20 It was not born; It will never die, nor once having been, can It cease to be. Unborn, Eternal, Ever-enduring, yet Most Ancient, the Spirit dies…”

This passage begins with “2.21 He who knows the Spirit as Indestructible, Immortal, Unborn, Always-the-Same, how should he kill or cause to be killed?” and develops toward “2.40 On this Path, endeavour is never wasted, nor can it ever be repressed. Even a very little of its practice protects one from great danger.”

This passage begins with “2.41 By its means, the straying intellect becomes steadied in the contemplation of one object only; whereas the minds of the irresolute stray into bypaths innumerable.” and develops toward “2.60 O Arjuna! The mind of him, who is trying to conquer it, is forcibly carried away in spite of his efforts, by his tumultuous senses.”

This passage begins with “2.61 Restraining them all, let him meditate steadfastly on Me; for who thus conquers his senses achieves perfection.” and develops toward “2.72 O Arjuna! This is the state of the Self, the Supreme Spirit, to which if a man once attain, it shall never be taken from him. Even at the time…”

chapters.name: Karma Yoga
chapters.summary: The path of selfless action, explaining why work is necessary, how to perform duty without desire for results, and action without expectation. Addresses career stress, lack of motivation, feeling burnt out by work, exam preparation, and finding meaning in everyday tasks.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “3.1 "Arjuna questioned: My Lord! If Wisdom is above action, why dost Thou advise me to engage in this terrible fight?” and develops toward “3.20 King Janaka and others attained perfection through action alone. Even for the sake of enlightening the world, it is thy duty to act;”

This passage begins with “3.21 For whatever a great man does, others imitate. People conform to the standard which he has set.” and develops toward “3.40 It works through the senses, the mind and the reason; and with their help destroys wisdom and confounds the soul.”

This passage begins with “3.41 Therefore, O Arjuna, first control thy senses and then slay desire, for it is full of sin, and is the destroyer of knowledge and of wisdom.” and develops toward “3.43 Thus, O Mighty-in-Arms, knowing Him to be beyond the intellect and, by His help, subduing thy personal egotism, kill thine enemy, Desire,…”

chapters.name: Jnana Karma Sanyasa Yoga
chapters.summary: The Yoga of Knowledge and sacrifice, divine birth of Krishna, renunciation of action, and the fire of knowledge. Addresses spiritual ignorance, searching for truth, and understanding divine cycles.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “4.1 "Lord Shri Krishna said: This imperishable philosophy I taught to Viwaswana, the founder of the Sun dynasty, Viwaswana gave it to Manu the lawgiver, and Manu to King Ikshwaku!” and develops toward “4.20 Having surrendered all claim to the results of his actions, always contented and independent, in reality he does nothing, even though he is…”

This passage begins with “4.21 Expecting nothing, his mind and personality controlled, without greed, doing bodily actions only; though he acts, yet he remains untainted.” and develops toward “4.40 But the ignorant man, and he who has no faith, and the sceptic are lost. Neither in this world nor elsewhere is there any happiness in store for…”

This passage begins with “4.41 But the man who has renounced his action for meditation, who has cleft his doubt in twain by the sword of wisdom, who remains always enthroned in his Self, is not bound by his acts.” and develops toward “4.42 Therefore, cleaving asunder with the sword of wisdom the doubts of the heart, which thine own ignorance has engendered, follow the Path of…”

chapters.name: Karma Sanyasa Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of renunciation, comparing renunciation of action with performance of selfless action, equanimity, and inner peace. Addresses mental peace, desire control, and emotional stability.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “5.1 "Arjuna said: My Lord! At one moment Thou praisest renunciation of action; at another, right action. Tell me truly, I pray, which of these is the more conducive to my highest welfare?” and develops toward “5.20 He who knows and lives in the Absolute remains unmoved and unperturbed; he is not elated by pleasure or depressed by pain.”

This passage begins with “5.21 He finds happiness in his own Self, and enjoys eternal bliss, whose heart does not yearn for the contacts of earth and whose Self is one with the Everlasting.” and develops toward “5.29 Knowing me as Him who gladly receives all offerings of austerity and sacrifice, as the Might Ruler of all the Worlds and the Friend of all…”

chapters.name: Dhyana Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of Meditation, controlling the restless and wandering mind, self-discipline, practice of moderation, and self-realization. Addresses overthinking, restlessness, stress relief, focus issues, loneliness, depression, and mental health.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “6.1 "Lord Shri Krishna said: He who acts because it is his duty, not thinking of the consequences, is really spiritual and a true ascetic; and not he who merely observes rituals or who…” and develops toward “6.20 There, where the whole nature is seen in the light of the Self, where the man abides within his Self and is satisfied there, its functions…”

This passage begins with “6.21 When he enjoys the Bliss which passes sense, and which only the Pure Intellect can grasp, when he comes to rest within his own highest Self, never again will he stray from reality.” and develops toward “6.40 Lord Shri Krishna replied: My beloved child! There is no destruction for him, either in this world or in the next. No evil fate awaits him who…”

This passage begins with “6.41 Having reached the worlds where the righteous dwell, and having remained there for many years, he who has slipped from the path of spirituality will be born again in the family of the…” and develops toward “6.47 I look upon him as the best of mystics who, full of faith, worshippeth Me and abideth in Me."”

chapters.name: Jnana Vijnana Yoga
chapters.summary: Knowledge of the Absolute, divine energy, and the four types of devotees. Addresses seeking God, finding faith, and understanding the universe.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “7.1 "Lord Shri Krishna said: Listen, O Arjuna! And I will tell thee how thou shalt know Me in my Full perfection, practising meditation with thy mind devoted to Me, and having Me for thy…” and develops toward “7.20 They in whom wisdom is obscured by one desire or the other, worship the lesser Powers, practising many rites which vary according to their…”

This passage begins with “7.21 But whatever the form of worship, if the devotee have faith, then upon his faith in that worship do I set My own seal.” and develops toward “7.30 Those who see Me in the life of the world, in the universal sacrifice, and as pure Divinity, keeping their minds steady, they live in Me, even…”

chapters.name: Akshara Brahma Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of the imperishable Brahman, path to liberation, and remembrance of the Divine at the time of death. Addresses fear of death, mortality, afterlife, and spiritual consistency.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Shri Purohit Swami did not comment on this sloka” and develops toward “8.20 In truth, therefore, there is the Eternal Unmanifest, which is beyond and above the Unmanifest Spirit of Creation, which is never destroyed when…”

This passage begins with “8.21 The wise say that the Unmanifest and Indestructible is the highest goal of all; when once That is reached, there is no return. That is My Blessed Home.” and develops toward “8.28 The sage who knows this passes beyond all merit that comes from the study of the scriptures, from sacrifice, from austerities and charity, and…”

chapters.name: Raja Vidya Raja Guhya Yoga
chapters.summary: The sovereign science and secret, divine protection, and path of total devotion. Addresses seeking safety, feeling unprotected, building faith, and divine love.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “9.1 "Lord Shri Krishna said: I will now reveal to thee, since thou doubtest not, that profound mysticism, which when followed by experience, shall liberate thee from sin.” and develops toward “9.20 Those who are versed in the scriptures, who drink the mystic Soma-juice and are purified from sin, but who while worshipping Me with sacrifices…”

This passage begins with “9.21 Yet although they enjoy the spacious glories of Paradise, nevertheless, when their merit is exhausted, they are born again into this world of mortals. They have followed the letter of…” and develops toward “9.34 Fix thy mind on Me, devote thyself to Me, sacrifice for Me, surrender to Me, make Me the object of thy aspirations, and thou shalt assuredly…”

chapters.name: Vibhuti Vistara Yoga
chapters.summary: The infinite divine opulences of God manifested in the creation. Addresses seeing divinity in nature, appreciation, and cosmic beauty.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “10.1 "Lord Shri Krishna said: Now, O Prince! Listen to My supreme advice, which I give thee for the sake of thy welfare, for thou art My beloved.” and develops toward “10.20 O Arjuna! I am the Self, seated in the hearts of all beings; I am the beginning and the life, and I am the end of them all.”

This passage begins with “10.21 Of all the creative Powers I am the Creator, of luminaries the Sun; the Whirlwind among the winds, and the Moon among planets.” and develops toward “10.40 O Arjuna! The aspects of My divine life are endless. I have mentioned but a few by way of illustration.”

This passage begins with “10.41 Whatever is glorious, excellent, beautiful and mighty, be assured that it comes from a fragment of My splendour.” and develops toward “10.42 But what is the use of all these details to thee? O Arjuna! I sustain this universe with only small part of Myself."”

chapters.name: Viswarupa Darshana Yoga
chapters.summary: Manifestation of the cosmic form of Krishna to Arjuna on the battlefield. Addresses fear, awe, and realizing the grand scale of existence.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “11.1 "Arjuna said: My Lord! Thy words concerning the Supreme Secret of Self, given for my blessing, have dispelled the illusions which surrounded me.” and develops toward “11.20 Alone thou fillest all the quarters of the sky, earth and heaven, and the regions between. O Almighty Lord! Seeing Thy marvellous and…”

This passage begins with “11.21 The troops of celestial beings enter into Thee, some invoking Thee in fear, with folded palms; the Great Seers and Adepts sing hymns to Thy Glory, saying All Hail.'” and develops toward “11.40 Salutations to Thee in front and on every side, Thou who encompasseth me round about. Thy power is infinite; Thy majesty immeasurable; thou…”

This passage begins with “11.41 Whatever I have said unto Thee in rashness, taking Thee only for a friend and addressing Thee as O Krishna! O Yadava! O Friend!' in thoughtless familiarity, no understanding Thy…” and develops toward “11.55 He whose every action is done for My sake, to whom I am the final goal, who loves Me only and hates no one - O My dearest son, only he can…”

chapters.name: Bhakti Yoga
chapters.summary: The path of devotion, characteristics of a dear devotee, personal vs impersonal worship, tolerance, and compassion. Addresses relationship hurt, handling unrequited love, dealing with feeling unloved or rejected by family/friends, cultivating compassion, and forgiveness.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “12.1 "Arjuna asked: My Lord! Which are the better devotees who worship Thee, those who try to know Thee as a Personal God, or those who worship Thee as Impersonal and Indestructible?” and develops toward “12.20 Verily those who love the spiritual wisdom as I have taught, whose faith never fails, and who concentrate their whole nature on Me, they indeed…”

chapters.name: Kshetra Kshetrajna Vibhaga Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of the field and the knower of the field (matter and spirit, body and soul). Addresses body image issues, health anxiety, and separating identity from the physical body.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “13.1 "Arjuna asked: My Lord! Who is God and what is Nature; what is Matter and what is the Self; what is that they call Wisdom, and what is it that is worth knowing? I wish to have this…” and develops toward “13.20 Know thou further that Nature and God have no beginning; and that differences of character and quality have their origin in Nature only.”

This passage begins with “13.21 Nature is the Law which generates cause and effect; God is the source of the enjoyment of all pleasure and pain.” and develops toward “13.35 Those who with the eyes of wisdom thus see the difference between Matter and Spirit, and know how to liberate Life from the Law of Nature, they…”

chapters.name: Gunatraya Vibhaga Yoga
chapters.summary: The three modes of material nature (Sattva/purity, Rajas/passion, Tamas/ignorance) and transcending them. Addresses anger, laziness, lethargy, procrastination, greed, and toxic environments.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “14.1 "Lord Shri Krishna continued: Now I will reveal unto the Wisdom which is beyond knowledge, by attaining which the sages have reached Perfection.” and develops toward “14.20 When the soul transcends the Qualities, which are the real cause of physical existence, then, freed from birth and death, from old age and…”

This passage begins with “14.21 Arjuna asked: My Lord! By what signs can he who has transcended the Qualities be recognized? How does he act? How does he live beyond them?” and develops toward “14.27 For I am the Home of the Spirit, the continual Source of immortality, of eternal Righteousness and of infinite Joy."”

chapters.name: Purushottama Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of the supreme person, comparing the cosmic tree of creation and transcending the perishable. Addresses feeling disconnected, seeking roots, and finding freedom.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “15.1 "Lord Shri Krishna continued: This phenomenal creation, which is both ephemeral and eternal, is like a tree, but having its seed above in the Highest and its ramifications on this…” and develops toward “15.20 Thus, O Sinless One, I have revealed to thee this most mystic knowledge. He who understands gains wisdom and attains the consummation of life."”

chapters.name: Daivasura Sampad Vibhaga Yoga
chapters.summary: The divine (fearlessness, truth, charity) and demonic (anger, pride, greed, arrogance) qualities inherent in human beings. Addresses anger issues, dealing with toxic/arrogant people, greed, ego control, and developing virtues.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “16.1 "Lord Shri Krishna continued: Fearlessness, clean living, unceasing concentration on wisdom, readiness to give, self-control, a spirit of sacrifice, regular study of the scriptures,…” and develops toward “16.20 So reborn, they spend life after life, enveloped in delusion. And they never reach Me, O Prince, but degenerate into still lower forms of life.”

This passage begins with “16.21 The gates of hell are three: lust, wrath and avarice. They destroy the Self. Avoid them.” and develops toward “16.24 Therefore whenever there is doubt whether thou shouldst do a thing or not, let the scriptures guide thy conduct. In the light of the scriptures…”

chapters.name: Shraddhatraya Vibhaga Yoga
chapters.summary: The three divisions of faith (food, sacrifice, penance, charity). Addresses healthy eating habits, spiritual discipline, and charity.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “17.1 "Arjuna asked: My Lord! Those who do acts of sacrifice, not according to the scriptures but nevertheless with implicit faith, what is their condition? Is it one of Purity, of Passion…” and develops toward “17.20 The gift which is given without thought of recompense, in the belief that it ought to be made, in a fit place, at an opportune time and to a…”

This passage begins with “17.21 That which is given for the sake of the results it will produce, or with the hope of recompense,or grudgingly - that may truly be said to be the outcome of Passion.” and develops toward “17.28 Whatsoever is done without faith, whether it be sacrifice, austerity or gift or anything else, as called Asat' (meaning Unreal') for it is the…”

chapters.name: Moksha Sanyasa Yoga
chapters.summary: Summary of all teachings, surrender, duty, and final liberation. Addresses decision making paralysis, guilt, fear of making wrong choices, seeking ultimate freedom, and total surrender.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “18.1 "Arjuna asked: O mighty One! I desire to know how relinquishment is distinguished from renunciation.” and develops toward “18.20 That knowledge which sees the One Indestructible in all beings, the One Indivisible in all separate lives, may be truly called Pure Knowledge.”

This passage begins with “18.21 The knowledge which thinks of the manifold existence in all beings as separate - that comes from Passion.” and develops toward “18.40 There is nothing anywhere on earth or in the higher worlds which is free from the three Qualities - for they are born of Nature.”

This passage begins with “18.41 O Arjuna! The duties of spiritual teachers, the soldiers, the traders and the servants have all been fixed according to the dominant Quality in their nature.” and develops toward “18.60 O Arjuna! Thy duty binds thee. From thine own nature has it arisen, and that which in thy delusion thou desire not to do, that very thing thou…”

This passage begins with “18.61 God dwells in the hearts of all beings, O Arjuna! He causes them to revolve as it were on a wheel by His mystic power.” and develops toward “18.78 Wherever is the Lord Shri Krishna, the Prince of Wisdom, and wherever is Arjuna, the Great Archer, I am more than convinced that good fortune,…”

description: Systematic collection of aphorisms outlining the science of Yoga, mind control, and meditation.

chapters.name: Samadhi Pada
chapters.summary: Deals with the definition of Yoga, fluctuations of the mind, practice (abhyasa) and detachment (vairagya), and samadhi. Addresses focus, concentration, overthinking, anxiety, and mental peace.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Now, the teachings of yoga are presented since the student is ready to received these teachings.” and develops toward “For others, the path to realization involves faith, energy, right cognition in form of memory and to understand the various elevated levels of wisdom.”

This passage begins with “Those who earnestly seek liberation will find that realization is within reach, depending on the intensity of their practice.” and develops toward “Complete absorption in any object, whether subtlest and (macroscopic or) microscopic , is possible through meditation.”

This passage begins with “As the fluctuations of mind fade away, a transparent mode of perception called 'samapatti'* alike to a transparent jewel/prism takes over. This transparent mind reflects everything in its…” and develops toward “When these impressions too come to an end, and the movement of consciousness is entirely pacified, this stage of 'impressionless' consciousness is…”

chapters.name: Sadhana Pada
chapters.summary: Explains the practice of Kriya Yoga, the five obstacles (kleshas) such as ignorance, egoism, and attachment, and the limbs of Ashtanga Yoga (Yama, Niyama, Asana, Pranayama, Pratyahara). Addresses habits, discipline, physical health, and breath control.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Kriya Yoga comprises of self discipline, study of self, and devotion towards Ishvara.” and develops toward “Pure awareness (drashta*) is simply the act of seeing (hence called seer), but it typically appears to function through the perceiving mind despite…”

This passage begins with “The purpose of the phenomenal world (drashya*) is essentially to reveal this truth (for the seer).” and develops toward “Practicing cleanliness, one becomes free from the body's compulsions and from the need of (physically) associating with others.”

This passage begins with “Developing sattva through the practice of cleanliness (mental purification) leads to radiance, increased ability to focus, sense control, and ability for self-awareness.” and develops toward “Afterwards, the supreme mastery over the senses is attained.”

chapters.name: Vibhuti Pada
chapters.summary: Focuses on concentration (dharana), meditation (dhyana), absorption (samadhi), and spiritual powers. Addresses deep focus and intuitive insights.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Concentration/Attention is focusing on a single place.” and develops toward “However, this insight does not extend to the object (alambana*) of their perceptions since it (the object) is not actually the subject matter of the…”

This passage begins with “By observing the body's physical form with perfect discipline (samyama*), it becomes invisible. This happens when the power to perceive is suspended amidst the eye and the incoming light.” and develops toward “By acheiving mastery over the flow of energy through the solar plexus (samana*), one becomes radiant.”

This passage begins with “By intently focusing (samyama*) on the inter-relationship of the organ of hearing and space, one can attain divine hearing.” and develops toward “The state of 'kaivalya' or liberation dawns when the purity of intellect equals to that of the pure consciousness.”

chapters.name: Kaivalya Pada
chapters.summary: Discusses liberation, karma, mind construction, and absolute freedom. Addresses overcoming karma and path to final liberation.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The mystic powers can manifest by the virtue from birth, herbal remedies, chanting of mantras, austerities and absorption in samadhi.” and develops toward “There cannot be discernment of both (the mind and the object it perceives) at the same time.”

This passage begins with “If (the mind) were cognized by another mind, then there would be an infinite regress of one intelligence (being known) by another intelligence. Moreover, there would also be confusion of…” and develops toward “Ultimate liberation is when the gunas, devoid of any purpose for the purusa, return to their original (latent) state; in other words, when the power…”

description: Short but profound scripture on the nature of Self, action without attachment, and the unity of creation.

chapters.name: Isha Upanishad Verses
chapters.summary: Contains 18 verses detailing seeing the Divine in all things, living a hundred years performing selfless duties, and the nature of Atman. Addresses greed, materialism, and feeling spiritually disconnected.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Om. All this - whatsoever moves on the earth - should be covered by the Lord (Ish). Protect (your Self) through that detachment. Do not covet anybody's wealth. (Or - Do not covet, for whose…” and develops toward “O Fire! O God! Knowing, as thou do, all our deeds, lead us by the good path for the enjoyment of the fruits of our deeds; remove from us all crooked…”

description: Short Upanishad analyzing the symbol OM and the four states of consciousness.

chapters.name: Mandukya Upanishad Verses
chapters.summary: Analysis of AUM and the four states of consciousness: waking (Vaishvanara), dreaming (Taijasa), deep sleep (Prajna), and the fourth state of pure non-dual consciousness (Turiya). Addresses overthinking, deep sleep issues, and finding absolute peace.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “OM! – This Imperishable Word is the whole of this visible universe. Its explanation is as follows: What has become, what is becoming, what will become, – verily, all of this is OM. And what…” and develops toward “The fourth is soundless: unutterable, a quieting down of all relative manifestations, blissful, peaceful, non-dual. Thus, OM is the Ātman, verily. He…”

description: Aphorisms of Kashmir Shaivism detailing path to divine realization.

chapters.name: Section 1
chapters.summary: Deals with the path of Shiva (divine consciousness), realizing the universe as one's own body, and expansion of awareness. Addresses seeking universal oneness and spiritual identity.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Consciousness which is omniscient and omnipotent (caitanyam) (is) the Self or true nature of Reality (ātmā)” and develops toward “(The other supernormal powers of the enlightened Yogī are: the power of) putting together (sandhāna) existent entities (bhūta); (the power of)…”

This passage begins with “Full acquisition (siddhiḥ) of mastery (īśatva) over the collective group of powers (cakra) (is achieved by the Yogī) through the emergence (udayāt) of Śuddhavidyā (śuddhavidyā)” and develops toward “By uniting (anusandhānāt) with the Great (mahā) Lake (hrada), (the Yogī has) the experience (anubhavaḥ) of the generative source --virility or…”

chapters.name: Section 2
chapters.summary: Deals with the path of Shakti (mental discipline, mantras, and pure thoughts). Addresses mental focus, willpower, and mantra meditation.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The mind (of someone who constantly ponders over the Highest Reality) (cittam) (is) the Mantra (mantraḥ)” and develops toward “On the submergence (saṁhāre) of the (Pure) Knowledge (vidyā), there is appearance (darśanam) of mental modifications (like in a dream) (svapna)…”

chapters.name: Section 3
chapters.summary: Deals with the path of the individual (breath control, body awareness, and physical discipline). Addresses breathing exercises, lifestyle discipline, and physical calmness.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The individual Self (ātmā) (is) mind (cittam)” and develops toward “The fourth state of consciousness, (which is a Witness) (caturtham), should be poured (āsecyam) like (vat) (a continuous flow of) oil (taila) into…”

This passage begins with “One should enter (that fourth state of consciousness --see aphorism 20--) (praviśet) by being immersed (into it) (magnaḥ) with one's own (sva) mind (cittena) (which must be devoid of any…” and develops toward “Because of the desire based on a feeling of want (abhilāṣāt), there is extroversion (bahirgatiḥ) of the limited being (saṁvāhyasya) (who is thus…”

This passage begins with “(Nevertheless,) in the case of (the great Yogī) whose awareness (pramiteḥ) (is) established (ārūḍha) in That --in the fourth state or Turya-- (tad), with the removal (kṣayāt) of that…” and develops toward “(Regarding the enlightened Yogī), there is (syāt) over and over again (bhūyas) the awareness of the Supreme Self both internally and externally…”

description: Profound Advaita dialogue on non-duality and absolute freedom.

chapters.name: Self-Realization Instruction
chapters.summary: Sage Ashtavakra instructs King Janaka on the witness consciousness, freedom from desires, and seeing oneself as pure awareness. Addresses mental bondage, feeling trapped, and seeking inner freedom.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Janaka said: How is one to acquire knowledge? How is one to attain liberation? And how is one to reach dispassion? Tell me this, sir.” and develops toward “Just as one and the same all-pervading space exists within and without a jar, so the eternal, everlasting God exists in the totality of things.”

chapters.name: Joy of Self-Realization
chapters.summary: Janaka describes his own realization and the joy of discovering the Self is untouched by the world. Addresses depression, lack of self-worth, and searching for joy.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Janaka said: Truly I am spotless and at peace, the awareness beyond natural causality. All this time I have been afflicted by delusion.” and develops toward “The body, heaven and hell, bondage and liberation, and fear too, all this is pure imagination. What is there left to do for me whose very nature is…”

This passage begins with “I do not even see dualism in a crowd of people, so what do I gain if it is replaced by a desert?” and develops toward “How wonderful it is that in the infinite ocean of myself the waves of living beings arise, collide, play, and disappear, in accordance with their…”

chapters.name: Test of Self-Realization
chapters.summary: Ashtavakra warns against greed and sensory attachments after receiving knowledge. Addresses self-discipline, greed, and moral lapses.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: Knowing yourself as truly one and indestructible, how could a wise man possessing self-knowledge like you feel any pleasure in acquiring wealth?” and develops toward “An object of enjoyment that comes of itself is neither painful nor pleasurable for someone who has eliminated attachment, and who is free from…”

chapters.name: Glory of Self-Realization
chapters.summary: Praise of the sage who abides in the Self without expectation. Addresses seeking validation, performance anxiety, and ego control.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: The wise person of self-knowledge, playing the game of worldly enjoyment, bears no resemblance whatever to samsara’s bewildered beasts of burden.” and develops toward “Rare is the man who knows himself as the nondual Lord of the world, and he who knows this is not afraid of anything.”

chapters.name: Four Ways to Dissolution
chapters.summary: Methods of dissolving the ego and identifying with pure space. Addresses overthinking and letting go of control.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: You are not bound by anything. What does a pure person like you need to renounce? Putting the complex organism to rest, you can find peace.” and develops toward “Equal in pain and in pleasure, equal in hope and in disappointment, equal in life and in death, and complete as you are, you can find peace.”

chapters.name: The Self Higher than Creation
chapters.summary: Understanding that the universe exists within the Self, not vice versa. Addresses existential dread and loneliness.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: I am infinite like space, and the natural world is like a jar. To know this is knowledge, and then there is neither renunciation, acceptance, or cessation of it.” and develops toward “Alternatively, I am in all beings, and all beings are in me. To know this is knowledge, and then there is neither renunciation, acceptance, or…”

chapters.name: The Self is the Infinite Ocean
chapters.summary: Seeing oneself as the infinite ocean in which the waves of the world arise. Addresses anxiety, panic attacks, and fear of change.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Janaka said: In the infinite ocean of myself the world boat drifts here and there, moved by its own inner wind. I am not put out by that.” and develops toward “I am pure consciousness, and the world is like a magician’s show. How could I imagine there is anything there to take up or reject?”

chapters.name: Bondage and Liberation
chapters.summary: Definition of bondage (desiring objects) vs liberation (letting go of desire). Addresses addictions, attachment, and unhealthy habits.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: Bondage is when the mind longs for something, grieves about something, rejects something, holds on to something, is pleased about something or displeased about something.” and develops toward “When there is no “me,” that is liberation, and when there is “me” there is bondage. Consider this carefully, and neither hold on to anything nor…”

chapters.name: Detachment
chapters.summary: Rising above dualities like good/bad, success/failure. Addresses stress about results, exam failure, and rejection.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: Knowing when the dualism of things done and undone has been put to rest, or the person for whom they occur has, then you can here and now go beyond renunciation and…” and develops toward “One’s desires are samsara. Knowing this, abandon them. The renunciation of them is the renunciation of it. Now you can remain as you are.”

chapters.name: Quietude
chapters.summary: Desisting from seeking wealth, pleasure, and action. Addresses burn out, career exhaustion, and mid-life crisis.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: Abandon desire, the enemy, along with gain, itself so full of loss, and the good deeds which are the cause of the other two — practice indifference to everything.” and develops toward “How many births have you not done hard and painful labour with body, mind, and speech. Now at last, stop!”

chapters.name: Wisdom
chapters.summary: Understanding that birth, death, and karma happen naturally, allowing the mind to rest. Addresses fear of death, bereavement, and grief.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: Unmoved and undistressed, realising that being, non-being and change are of the very nature of things, one easily finds peace.” and develops toward “Realising that all this varied and wonderful world is nothing, one becomes pure receptivity, free from inclinations, and as if nothing existed, one…”

chapters.name: Abiding in the Self
chapters.summary: Resting in one's own natural state without practicing meditation or ritual. Addresses spiritual dry spells and restlessness.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Janaka said: First of all I was averse to physical activity, then to lengthy speech, and finally to thought itself, which is why I am now established.” and develops toward “He who has achieved this has achieved the goal of life. He who is of such a nature has done what has to be done.”

chapters.name: Happiness
chapters.summary: Experiencing the natural happiness of doing nothing and seeking nothing. Addresses chronic stress, exhaustion, and worry.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Janaka said: The inner freedom of having nothing is hard to achieve, even with just a loin-cloth, but I live as I please, abandoning both renunciation and acquisition.” and develops toward “Continually observing the drawbacks of such things as pleasant objects, I live as I please, abandoning the pleasant and unpleasant.”

chapters.name: Peace
chapters.summary: The quietness of a mind that has cooled down all desires. Addresses anger, emotional outbursts, and mental turbulence.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Janaka said: He who by nature is empty-minded, and who thinks of things only unintentionally, is freed from deliberate remembering like one awakened from a dream.” and develops toward “The various states of one who is free of uncertainty within, and who outwardly wanders about as he pleases like an idiot, can only be known by…”

chapters.name: Knowledge of the Self
chapters.summary: Ashtavakra outlines the direct instructions on the nature of Brahman. Addresses seeking truth and intellectual confusion.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: While a man of pure intelligence may achieve the goal by the most casual of instruction, another may seek knowledge all his life and still remain bewildered.” and develops toward “Give up meditation completely but don’t let the mind hold on to anything. You are free by nature, so what will you achieve by forcing the mind?”

chapters.name: Special Instructions
chapters.summary: Forgetting bookish learning to realize the Self through direct experience. Addresses excessive studying, intellectual ego, and confusion.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: My son, you may recite or listen to countless scriptures, but you will not be established within until you can forget everything.” and develops toward “If even Shiva, Vishnu, or the lotus-born Brahma were your instructor, until you have forgotten everything you cannot be established within.”

chapters.name: The True Knower
chapters.summary: Traits of the liberated sage who lives in the world like a child. Addresses social anxiety, peer pressure, and feeling out of place.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: He who is content, with purified senses, and always enjoys solitude, has gained the fruit of knowledge and the fruit of the practice of yoga too.” and develops toward “He whose thinking mind is dissolved achieves the indescribable state and is free from the mental display of delusion, dream, and ignorance.”

chapters.name: Peace and Quietude
chapters.summary: Deepest Advaita meditation on the voidness of all phenomena. Addresses ultimate spiritual liberation, void, and deep meditation.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashtavakra said: Praise be to That by the awareness of which delusion itself becomes dream-like, to that which is pure happiness, peace, and light.” and develops toward “The wise man who just goes on doing what presents itself for him to do, encounters no difficulty in either activity or inactivity.”

This passage begins with “He who is desireless, self-reliant, independent, and free of bonds functions like a dead leaf blown about by the wind of causality.” and develops toward “How can there be self-knowledge for him whose knowledge depends on what he sees? The wise do not see this and that, but see themselves as infinite.”

This passage begins with “How can there be cessation of thought for the misguided who is striving for it? Yet it is there always naturally for the wise man delighting in himself.” and develops toward “He who of his very nature feels no unhappiness in his daily life like worldly people, remains undisturbed like a great lake, cleared of defilement.”

This passage begins with “Even abstention from action has the effect of action in a fool, while even the action of the wise man brings the fruits of inaction.” and develops toward “There is neither heaven nor hell nor even liberation during life. In a nutshell, in the sight of the seer nothing exists at all.”

This passage begins with “He neither longs for possessions nor grieves at their absence. The calm mind of the sage is full of the nectar of immortality.” and develops toward “A man at peace does not run off to popular resorts or to the forest. Whatever and wherever, he remains the same.”

chapters.name: Repose in the Self
chapters.summary: Janaka describes his state of resting in his own glory. Addresses confidence issues and self-doubt.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Janaka said: Using the tweezers of the knowledge of the truth I have managed to extract the painful thorn of endless opinions from the recesses of my heart.” and develops toward “For me remaining in myself, there is no need for talk of the three goals of life, of yoga or of knowledge.”

chapters.name: Complete Liberation
chapters.summary: The final state where there is neither bondage nor liberation, only the Self. Addresses seeking ultimate freedom.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Janaka said: In my unblemished nature there are no elements, no body, no faculties, no mind. There is no void and no despair.” and develops toward “There is no being or non-being, no unity or dualism. What more is there to say? There is nothing outside of me.”

description: Song of the Avadhuta (Dattatreya) on absolute non-duality and formless reality.

chapters.name: The Nature of the Self
chapters.summary: Dattatreya describes the all-pervading Self which is free from division, space, time, and modifications. Addresses feeling disconnected, fear of isolation, and existential anxiety.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The desire to gain the knowledge of non-duality arises in humans by the grace of the universal mind, which relieves them of greatest fears.” and develops toward “Like all those scriptures say, Consciousness is very pure, devoid of any qualities or imperfections. It has no body or form, it is the only Truth.…”

This passage begins with “Know that everything that has a form is virtual, an illusion. The formless one is eternal and true. Once you understand this teaching, reincarnation will be impossible.” and develops toward “I am not at all that which acts, eats, performs rituals or donates etc. I am pure, unborn, immortal Self.”

This passage begins with “Know that the whole existence is formless or empty, it is changeless, pure, even though seems to have a form and its nature is Consciousness.” and develops toward “If I am omnipresent, effulgent, changeless, perfect and eternal, how can I see the difference between internal or external?”

This passage begins with “The existence is bright, undivided and continuous. Maya, attachments, duality, non-duality are all imaginations.” and develops toward “There exists no nothingness or something-ness, there are no truths or untruths. One speaks the scriptures out of one’s own nature.”

chapters.name: The Self Beyond Division
chapters.summary: Explains why the Self cannot be divided or defined by scriptures. Addresses confusion about religions, sects, and spiritual paths.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Do not think of those who are immature, hedonistic, idiots, servants, householders etc. as inferior. Who throws away a gem that has gone dirty?” and develops toward “Knowledge of external world is illusory. Knowledge of internal cause is the right one. Know what lies inside that interior, like water in the…”

This passage begins with “Consciousness is one, like a full moon in the night, one and very pure. Seeing duality is an error.” and develops toward “Where mind and speech is useless, Guru’s teachings say nothing. How can a Guru tell about that Truth which reveals itself?”

chapters.name: Immortal and Formless Self
chapters.summary: The Self is ever pure, immortal, and untouched by physical changes. Addresses body image issues, fear of aging, and physical sickness.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “How can I worship that Consciousness which has no qualities and also is not quality-less, which has no attachments and detachments, which is pure and innocent, which is all pervading like…” and develops toward “If I am not the three places, how can I be the forth? If I am not the three times how can I be the forth? I am the ultimate truth and extremely…”

This passage begins with “I do not have the divisions of big and small. I do not have the divisions of wide and narrow. I do not have the divisions of angular or circular. I am the essence of all knowledge,…” and develops toward “Your nature has not even tiniest amount of detachment, not even the tiniest amount of attachment, not even the tiniest amount of desires. I am the…”

This passage begins with “At your core is no absorption, no meditator, at your core is no exterior, no meditation, at your core is no time and space, no object of meditation. I am the essence of all knowledge,…” and develops toward “Renounce the illusory world and completely give up the renouncing. Renunciation as well as clinging, both are poison. Your essence is pure, natural…”

chapters.name: Realization Without Attributes
chapters.summary: Realizing the Self without attributes (Nirguna) and beyond mental concepts. Addresses overthinking, mind chatter, and mental noise.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “What is the use of invocations and offerings, what is the use of flowers and leaves, what is the use of meditation and mantras? I am the same as Consciousness and it's worshipper.” and develops toward “I am not an idiot, I am not a wise. I do not observe silence or non-silence. What can I say about logic and argument? I am not Maya, my nature is…”

This passage begins with “I never had a father, mother, family, caste, never born, never die. What can I say of affection and attachments? I am not Maya, my nature is freedom.” and develops toward “Where there is no knowledge, verses do not go there. I am involved in the world and I am renounced. I have spoken the ultimate truth.”

chapters.name: Equal Vision
chapters.summary: Viewing all circumstances, people, and events with equal vision (Samatvam). Addresses sibling rivalry, relationship conflicts, jealousy, and envy.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “As it is chanted, the Aum is like space. It has no high or low. The play of light and dark is never obstructed. How can the dot on Aum have any effect?” and develops toward “If I am equal, free of caste and casteless-ness, if I am equal, free of causes and effects, if I am equal, free of division and non-division, why do…”

This passage begins with “Here all minds are one unbroken and undivided. Here all minds are only pure. Here there are no men, everything is mind. Why do you cry O mind? You are the same in everything.” and develops toward “Where there is no knowledge, verses do not go there. I am involved in the world and I am renounced. I have spoken the ultimate truth.”

chapters.name: Beyond Mind and Speech
chapters.summary: Realizing the reality which cannot be described in words. Addresses communication breakdown, feeling misunderstood, and silence.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “As the scriptures say in many ways, the manifested universe is like a mirage. If everything is one eternal Consciousness, how can there be comparison and compared?” and develops toward “It is free of sadness and joy it is free of pride and humility. If everything is one eternal Consciousness, how can there be attachments and…”

This passage begins with “Detachments and attachments do not arise in it. Greed and contentment do not arise in it. If everything is one eternal Consciousness, how can there be discrimination and indiscrimination?” and develops toward “Where there is no knowledge, verses do not go there. I am involved in the world and I am renounced. I have spoken the ultimate truth.”

chapters.name: Traits of the Avadhuta
chapters.summary: Description of the free, wanderer sage who lives without rules. Addresses feeling restricted by society, social pressure, and rule compliance stress.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Clad in rejected clothes thrown on the streets, he follows a path free of right or wrong actions. He sits naked in an empty dwelling, absorbed in the pure stainless eternal one.” and develops toward “Where there is no knowledge, verses do not go there. I am involved in the world and I am renounced. I have spoken the ultimate truth.”

chapters.name: Absolute Truth
chapters.summary: Final statements on the absolute truth of non-duality. Addresses seeking ultimate clarity and truth.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Making a pilgrimage to you destroys the fact of omnipresence of you. By meditating on you, the fact of your being beyond mind is destroyed. By chanting praises of you, the fact of your…” and develops toward “This song is composed by Dattatreya Avadhuta, who is pure bliss by nature. Those who read it or listen to it will never be born again.”

description: Dialogue between young seeker Nachiketa and Yama (Lord of Death) on immortality.

chapters.name: Nachiketa's Journey & Boons
chapters.summary: Nachiketa travels to Death's abode and receives three boons, seeking to know what happens to a person after death. Addresses fear of death, mortality, afterlife, and grief over deceased loved ones.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Yearning ( superior rewards,) Vajasravasa gifted all his material wealth. He had a son named Nachiketa.” and develops toward “(Nachiketa said): "Upon the death of a person, there arises this doubt - some declare that something outlives the body, other say, nothing remains. I…”

This passage begins with “(Yama said) : " This subtle matter is not easily understood. In the days of yore, even Gods had entairtained doubts about this. Therefore, O! Nachiketa! choose another boon. Do not press…” and develops toward “O! Death! Instruct me on the superior life after death regarding which people have reservations. Nachiketa chooses not other boon other than this…”

chapters.name: Good vs Pleasant Choice
chapters.summary: Yama explains the distinction between Shreya (the good/spiritual path) and Preya (the pleasant/sensual path). Addresses temptation, short-term pleasure vs long-term success, habits, and self-control.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Two concepts bind the man and are of different objectives. One is what is beneficial and the other is what is pleasing. He who chooses the beneficial, gains and the one who goes for the the…” and develops toward “Smaller than the smallest, greater than the greatest is the Atman that dwells in the cave of the heart of beings. He who is desireless, realises the…”

This passage begins with “While seated, he travels far ; lying down he goes all-over. Who can know other than me that Shining One, who is both joyful and joyless.” and develops toward “He for whom the Brahmana and the Kshatriya are food, and Death the pickle, who can know where He (The Self, Atman) is ?”

chapters.name: The Chariot Metaphor
chapters.summary: Metaphor of the chariot: the body is the chariot, intellect is the driver, mind is the reins, senses are the horses, and the Self is the passenger. Addresses self-discipline, focus, overthinking, and study habits.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The cave of the heart is occupied by two entities (the cosmic self and the individual self), enjoying the fruits of their actions. Those who are realised, call them light and shade, as also…” and develops toward “Whoever relates with devotion , this highly secret-laden episode in the assembly of the learned or at the time of performing rites and rituals for…”

chapters.name: Inner Self vs Outward Senses
chapters.summary: How the Creator made the senses turn outward, but the wise man turns them inward to see the Soul. Addresses sensory distractions, focus issues, and screen addiction.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The Self-Created (God) made the senses turn outward; hence one looks outward, does not see what is within oneself. Rare is the wise man, who, longing for immortality, turns his eyes inward…” and develops toward “As water that is pure, when poured into water that is pure remains pure, so does the Self of the realised one remains pure on knowing (uniting with)…”

chapters.name: The City of Eleven Gates
chapters.summary: The soul residing within the physical body and its independence from the body. Addresses health anxiety, sickness, and separating identity from the physical body.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “There is the city of eleven gates belonging to Him who is unborn and of unchanging consciousness. By meditating upon Him, one grieves no more; and being liberated one becomes free from…” and develops toward “" Neither the sun, the moon , the stars, nor the lightning shines here, nor to speak of this fire. Every other thing shines following That and due to…”

chapters.name: The Tree of Samsara & Yoga
chapters.summary: The cosmic tree of creation and the practice of Yoga to still the senses and realize Brahman. Addresses spiritual practice, focus, and ultimate peace.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “With its root above and branches below stands this Banyan-tree. That (root) is indeed the Pure, that is Brahman, the Immortal. All the worlds are contained in It and no one goes beyond It.…” and develops toward “Instructed by Yama , in the knowledge, in this manner, as also in the technique of Yoga Sastra, Nachiketa became sinless and transcended physical…”

description: Details the division of lower and higher knowledge and the metaphor of two birds.

chapters.name: Mundaka 1, Khanda 1
chapters.summary: Distinguishing between ritualistic knowledge (Apara Vidya) and knowledge of the imperishable Brahman (Para Vidya). Addresses seeking true wisdom, academic stress, and religious confusion.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Om. Brahma, the Maker of the universe and the Preserver of the world, was the first among the devas. He told His eldest son Atharva about the Knowledge of Brahman, the foundation of all…” and develops toward “For him who knows all and understands everything, whose austerity consists of knowledge-from Him, the Imperishable Brahman, are born Brahma, name,…”

chapters.name: Mundaka 1, Khanda 2
chapters.summary: Distinguishing between ritualistic knowledge (Apara Vidya) and knowledge of the imperishable Brahman (Para Vidya). Addresses seeking true wisdom, academic stress, and religious confusion.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “This is the Truth: The sacrificial works which were revealed to the rishis in the hymns have been described in many ways in the three Vedas. Practice them, being desirous to attain their…” and develops toward “To that pupil who has duly approached him, whose mind is completely serene and whose senses are controlled, the wise teacher should indeed rightly…”

chapters.name: Mundaka 2, Khanda 1
chapters.summary: Distinguishing between ritualistic knowledge (Apara Vidya) and knowledge of the imperishable Brahman (Para Vidya). Addresses seeking true wisdom, academic stress, and religious confusion.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “This is the Truth: As from a blazing fire, sparks essentially akin to it fly forth by the thousand, so also, my good friend, do various beings come forth from the imperishable Brahman and…” and develops toward “The Purusha alone is verily the universe, which consists of work and austerity. O my good friend, he who knows this Brahman-the Supreme and the…”

chapters.name: Mundaka 2, Khanda 2
chapters.summary: Distinguishing between ritualistic knowledge (Apara Vidya) and knowledge of the imperishable Brahman (Para Vidya). Addresses seeking true wisdom, academic stress, and religious confusion.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The Luminous Brahman dwells in the cave of the heart and is known to move there. It is the great support of all; for in It is centred everything that moves, breathes and blinks. O…” and develops toward “That immortal Brahman alone is before, that Brahman is behind, that Brahman is to the right and left. Brahman alone pervades everything above and…”

chapters.name: Mundaka 3, Khanda 1
chapters.summary: Distinguishing between ritualistic knowledge (Apara Vidya) and knowledge of the imperishable Brahman (Para Vidya). Addresses seeking true wisdom, academic stress, and religious confusion.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Two birds, united always and known by the same name, closely cling to the same tree. One of them eats the sweet fruit; the other looks on without eating.” and develops toward “Whatever world a man of pure understanding envisages in his mind and whatever desires he cherishes, that world he conquers and those desires he…”

chapters.name: Mundaka 3, Khanda 2
chapters.summary: Distinguishing between ritualistic knowledge (Apara Vidya) and knowledge of the imperishable Brahman (Para Vidya). Addresses seeking true wisdom, academic stress, and religious confusion.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “He, the Knower of the Self, knows that Supreme Abode of Brahman, which shines brightly and in which the universe rests. Those wise men who, free from desires, worship such a person…” and develops toward “Thus the seer Angiras declared this truth in olden times. A man who has not performed the vow should not read it. Salutation to the great seers!…”

description: Devotional Upanishad linking Rudra (Shiva) to the non-dual Self and meditation.

chapters.name: Primal Cause & Maya
chapters.summary: Inquires into the source of the universe and explains God's power (Maya) as the cause. Addresses feeling lost, questioning the universe, and spiritual seeking.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rishis, discoursing on Brahman, ask: Is Brahman the cause? Whence are we born? By what do we live? Where do we dwell at the end? Please tell us, O ye who know Brahman, under whose guidance…” and develops toward “The Self, which pervades all things as butter is diffused in milk, has Self-Knowledge and austerity for its root. That is Brahman, the supreme…”

chapters.name: Practice of Meditation
chapters.summary: Details the posture, breath control, and signs of progress in meditation and Yoga. Addresses meditation posture, breath control, stress relief, and physical wellness.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “May the sun, at the commencement of yoga, join our minds and other organs to the Supreme Self so that we may attain the Knowledge of Reality. May He, also, support the body, the highest…” and develops toward “The Self-luminous Lord, who is fire, who is in water, who has entered into the whole world, who is in plants, who is in trees- to that Lord let there…”

chapters.name: The Cosmic Person
chapters.summary: Praise of Rudra (Shiva) as the all-pervading Lord residing in the heart. Addresses fear, seeking divine protection, and devotion.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The non-dual Ensnarer rules by His powers. Remaining one and the same, He rules by His powers all the worlds during their manifestation and continued existence. They who know this become…” and develops toward “The Self, smaller than the small, greater than the great, is hidden in the hearts of creatures. The wise, by the grace of the Creator, behold the…”

This passage begins with “I know this undecaying, primeval One, the Self of all things, which exists everywhere, being all-pervading and which the wise declare to be free from birth. The teachers of Brahman, indeed,…” and develops toward “I know this undecaying, primeval One, the Self of all things, which exists everywhere, being all-pervading and which the wise declare to be free from…”

chapters.name: God the Magician
chapters.summary: Describing the universe as God's magic play and finding freedom by knowing the Magician. Addresses feeling manipulated by circumstances, and seeking freedom.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “He, the One and Undifferentiated, who by the manifold application of His powers produces, in the beginning, different objects for a hidden purpose and, in the end, withdraws the universe…” and develops toward “His form is not an object of vision; no one beholds Him with the eyes. They who, through pure intellect and the Knowledge of Unity based upon…”

This passage begins with “It is because Thou, O Lord, art birthless, that some rare souls, frightened by birth and death, take refuge in Thee. O Rudra, may Thy benign face protect me for ever!” and develops toward “O Rudra, do not, in Thy wrath, destroy our children and grand-children. Do not destroy our lives; do not destroy our cows or horses; do not destroy…”

chapters.name: Individual Soul vs Brahman
chapters.summary: The tiny individual soul and its potential to expand to infinite Brahman. Addresses low self-esteem, feeling insignificant, and self-doubt.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “In the Immutable, infinite Supreme Brahman remain hidden the two: knowledge and ignorance. Ignorance leads to worldliness and knowledge, to Immortality. Brahman, who controls both knowledge…” and develops toward “Those who know Him who can be realised by the pure heart, who is called incorporeal, who is the cause of creation and destruction, who is all good…”

chapters.name: Path of Surrender
chapters.summary: Finding refuge and surrendering to the divine light of wisdom. Addresses crisis management, total surrender, and seeking mental peace.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Some learned men speak of the inherent nature of things and some speak of time, as the cause of the universe. They all, indeed, are deluded. It is the greatness of the self-luminous Lord…” and develops toward “When human beings can roll up space like a piece of hide, only then could misery end without the Knowledge of the Lord.”

This passage begins with “Through the power of austerity and through the grace of the Lord, the sage Svetasvatara realised Brahman and proclaimed the highly sacred Knowledge, supremely cherished by the company of…” and develops toward “If these truths have been told to a high-minded person who feels the highest devotion for God and for his guru as for God, then they will surely…”

description: Dialogue of ethical and moral questions from the Mahabharata.

chapters.name: The searching about for the deer
chapters.summary: Yudhishthira answers 125 questions regarding: What is the greatest wonder? Who is happy? How to find true dharma? Addresses moral dilemmas, ethical choices, exam prep, family duty, handling death of brothers, and wisdom in crisis.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Janamejaya said : Krishna being thus abducted the Pandavas experienced very great sorrow. What did they next do after having rescued her?” and develops toward “"In our race virtue has never been sacrificed nor there has been any loss of wealth through idleness. Again, we have never refused anything to any…”

chapters.name: The death of Nakula and others
chapters.summary: Yudhishthira answers 125 questions regarding: What is the greatest wonder? Who is happy? How to find true dharma? Addresses moral dilemmas, ethical choices, exam prep, family duty, handling death of brothers, and wisdom in crisis.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Yudhishthira said : There is no limit to misfortunes and neither their effects nor their causes can be ascertained. It is Dharma who distributes the fruits of both virtue and sin.” and develops toward “Then Yudhishthira, the son of Kunti, said to Vijaya (Arjuna) “O Vivatsu, O tormentor of foes, your brothers (Nakula and Sahadeva) have been long out.”

This passage begins with “May you be in peace. Go and bring them and also water. O affectionate one, you are the refuge of all of us when in distress."” and develops toward “Then that best of men, the royal son of Kunti of mighty arms, whose heart was burning in grief, after much deliberation rose up and entered that…”

This passage begins with “Adorned with trees of blue and bright colours and ringing with the hum of bees and warbling of birds. And that highly renowned.” and develops toward “Adorned with rows of lotuses, Sindhuvara flowers together with cane trees and covered all over with Ketakas, Karaviras and Pippalas. Oppressed with…”

chapters.name: Section 313
chapters.parent: ARANEYA PARVA)-Continued The questions put by Yaksha (to Yudhishthira
chapters.summary: Yudhishthira answers 125 questions regarding: What is the greatest wonder? Who is happy? How to find true dharma? Addresses moral dilemmas, ethical choices, exam prep, family duty, handling death of brothers, and wisdom in crisis.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Vaishampayana said : He (Yudhishthira) then beheld his brothers, glorious as Shakra himself, lying dead like the Lokapalas dropped down form their regions at the end of a Yuga.” and develops toward “Each of these foremost of men is possessed of the force of a water fall. Who else, therefore, can vanquish them except that being who carries away…”

This passage begins with “Thinking it to be certain, he plunged into that water. And as he got into it he heard these words from the firmament.” and develops toward “Yudhishthira said : It is the arrows ard weapons that constitute their divinity. Sacrifices (on their part) resemble the virtuous practices of the…”

This passage begins with “The Yaksha said: What is that one thing) which is the sacrificial Soma? What is that which constitutes the sacrificial Yaju? What is that which sacrifice can not do without?” and develops toward “Yudhishthira replied The soul of a man is his son. The wife is his friend given by Destiny. The clouds are his principal support and charity is his…”

This passage begins with “The Yaksha asked What is the best of all praise-worthy objects? What is the best of all sorts of wealth? What is the most important of all gains? And what is the best of all kinds of…” and develops toward “Yudhishthira replied Anger is the most invincible enemy. Covetousness is the incurable disease. description: The epic narrative of Lord Rama, illustrating dharma, righteousness, devotion, and the victory of good over evil.

chapters.name: Sarga 1
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ascetic Valmiki enquired of Narada, preeminent among the sages ever engaged in the practice of religious austerities or study of the Vedas and best among the eloquent.” and develops toward “Pious, firm in his vows, he is ever intent on doing good to his subjects. He is, illustrious, wise, and pure at heart. He is obedient to elders (or…”

This passage begins with “Auspicious like Brahma, Sri Rama is the sustainer of this world, destroyer of enemies and protector of all living beings and of the moral code.” and develops toward “Mighty Sri Rama inorder to please Kaikeyi and obey the word of command of his father, went to the forest and help the king to keep his promise to…”

This passage begins with “Lakshmana beloved brother to Rama is drawn towards him. Endowed with modesty he is an enhancer of the joy of his mother Sumitra. This passage begins with “Hanuman arrived at the city of Lanka ruled by Ravana and found Sita in the Ashoka garden meditating on Rama.” and develops toward “All the animate and inanimate beings, gods and sages in the three worlds were very pleased at this noble deed of the great Rama.”

This passage begins with “After coronating the rakshasa chief Vibhishana in the city of Lanka, Rama free from distress, exceedingly rejoiced after having accomplished his objective.” and develops toward “Rama will establish hundredfold royal dynasties and employ the four castes to do their respective duties, in this world.”

This passage begins with “Rama, reigning the kingdom for eleven thousand years, will attain Brahmaloka.” and develops toward “A brahmin becomes proficient in the eighteen branches of learning a kshatriya gets lordship over landed possessions a vaisya gets the fruits of his…”

(On the way) at Pushkara he took some rest at noon.” and develops toward “Then Sunassepha will be protected. The king's sacrifice will continue without hindrance. The gods will be gratified. My words also will come true".”

This passage begins with “Thus bound, the son of the sage praised the two devatas, Indra and Upendra with moving words.” and develops toward “O Best among men the righteous and mighty ascetic Viswamitra also continued his austerities at Pushkara for a thousand years". इत्यार्षे…”

(But)…” and develops toward “Viswamitra whose wealth was his penance carried on his rigid austerities for a thousand years amidst five fires, in summer, under the open sky in…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “"O Rambha you must entice Viswamitra by lust and passion. This important work must be done by you in the interest of the gods".” and develops toward “'O luckless Rambha, since you have endeavoured to distract me from my desire to conquer wrath and passion, you shall turn into a rock and remain so…”

This passage begins with “'O Rambha an effulgent brahmin, endowed with the power of asceticism, will liberate you from this state into which you have fallen on account of my anger'.” and develops toward “"O Descendant of Raghu in this way that eminent ascetic took an unprecedented vow to practise penance for a thousand years". इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “"O Rama thereafter the great ascetic (Viswamitra), abandoning the north of the Himavat mountain reached the eastern quarter and performed rigorous austerities.” and develops toward “His penance was flawless. If his wishes were not granted, he will surely destroy by his austerities the three worlds with all movables and immovables.”

This passage begins with “There is bewilderment everywhere. It is dark all over. The oceans are disturbed. The mountains have cracked.” and develops toward “Thereupon, pleased by the gods, Vasishta, the best among ascetics, lovingly said to Viswamitra "You are a Brahmarshi, let it be so".”

This passage begins with “"You are a Brahmarshi, no doubt. You will achieve ascetic perfection", said the gods and returned to their respective places from where they had come.” and develops toward “O Sage of great austerities come to me tomorrow at dawn. O Excellent ascetic welcome And allow me to leave".”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The day dawned bright and clear when the king (Janaka) performed his daily routine rites and welcomed the great Viswamitra with the two Raghavas (Rama and Lakshmana).” and develops toward “O Lord that gem of a bow belonging to Siva was given to the great gods who in turn got it deposited in trust with our ancestor.”

This passage begins with “Thereupon while I was ploughing and cleaing the (sacrificial) ground, Sita, a wellknown name, was lifted up by the blade of the plough. Thus she was obtained by me.” and develops toward “Then those wicked kings who were exhausted were doubtful about their energy. They were beaten and defeated. They fled away along with their ministers…”

This passage begins with “O Sage you are a tiger among asceties, you are faithful to your vows, I shall show this effulgent bow to your Rama and Lakshmana .” and develops toward “O Sage if Rama could lift and string this bow I shall give my daughter Sita, not born from a woman, to him (son of Dasaratha)". इत्यार्षे…”

This passage begins with “That Sita, the reward of prowess is to be given to Rama be reventially communicated in detail to king Dasaratha and he may be conveyed here.” and develops toward “The son of Kausika (Viswamitra) also said, "So be it". Come here soon with your preceptors and priest. You ought to be reumitd with Rama amd Lakshmana.”

This passage begins with “O Indra among kings you will not only bring me pleasure, you will also be happy to see your sons.” and develops toward “If you approve the action, of the distinguished Janaka, let us hurry to the city of Mithila. Let there be no delay".”

This passage begins with “"O Heroic lord of Ayodhya, Janaka, the lord of Mithila desires to see you along with preceptors and priest".” and develops toward “Bana's son was the most brilliant and valiant Anaranya whose son Pruthu was succeeded by Trisanku.”

This passage begins with “Trisanku's famous son was Dundumara also known as Yuvanashva whose son was king Mandhata.” and develops toward “This eldest queen who was a devoted wife, afflicted with sorrow due to loss of her husband gave birth to a son by the grace of Chyavana.”

This passage begins with “Since poison was given to her by her rival queen with the intention of destroying the embryo, the son was born with poison and so came to be known as Sagara.” and develops toward “Nabhaga's son was Aja. Aja was the father of Dasaratha and Dasaratha, the father of Rama and Lakshmana.”

I am the eldest and valiant Kusadhwaja is my younger brother.” and develops toward “Today the star Makha is on the ascendant. This passage begins with “"The wise, O Brahmin think the marriage is favourable when Bhaga is Prajapati on the succeeding day of the ascent of the two stars. Though mighty, he was never proud of his overwhelming strength.” and develops toward “He was firm in his devotion and steady in intellect. He accepted nothing ignoble nor used bad words. This passage begins with “He was versed in scriptures. He was grateful (to those who did him some good) and skilled in differentiating the comparative merits of men.
