sections.summary: This passage begins with “Annoyed with Kaikeyi and filled with rage and sorrow, Manthara discarded the ornament (gifted to her) and said these words:” and develops toward “All the women of Rama's (palace) will be delighted indeed. With the decline of Bharata, your daughtersinlaw will be deprived of all happiness.”

This passage begins with “Having seen that morose Manthara speaking thus, Kaikeyi commended the virtues of Rama.” and develops toward “O Kaikeyi of beautiful limbs, for that reason kings bestow the governance of the kingdom either on the eldest or on other virtuous sons.”

This passage begins with “O (Kaikeyi) dear cut off from royal succession as well as from its comforts, your son will become totally forlorn and brokenhearted.” and develops toward “Like the leader of an elephantherd attacked by a lion in the forest, Bharata is going to be oppressed by Rama. You should save him.”

This passage begins with “Being fortunate (being the favourite wife of your husband) you slighted your cowife earlier with pride. How will she, Rama's mother (Kausalya) remain without retaliating?” and develops toward “When Rama obtains this earth, Bharata will be certainly ruined. Therefore, right now think of the kingdom for your son and the means of banishment…”

chapters.name: Sarga 9
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Thus spoken to, Kaikeyi, her face burning with anger, breathed a deep, hot sigh and said these words to Manthara:” and develops toward “O queen during the war between gods and asuras, your husband went with royal saints intending to render assistance to Indra, king of the gods, taking…”

This passage begins with “That mighty asura wellknown as Sambara, capable of a hundred deceitful forms, challenged Indra to a battle which could not be conquered by hosts of gods.” and develops toward “No doubt, you have always been a beloved wife to your husband. For your sake the great king will even enter into the burning fire.”

This passage begins with “The king is afraid of inciting your ire. When you are angry, he dare not look at your indignant countenance. Indeed he will forsake even his life for your pleasure.” and develops toward “Thus, having been made to accept the evil design as a beneficial objective, Kaikeyi understood (the entire matter) and in delight spoke to Manthara.”

This passage begins with “That Kaikeyi, even though endowed with sound foresight, expressed her sense of wonder at the words of the hunchback and chose a wrong path like a young (immature) girl.” and develops toward “O Manthara, when my objective is accomplished and I am fully satisfied, I will smear your hump with the best of refined liquid gold.”

This passage begins with “O Manthara, I shall get a tilaka made of gold for your auspicious, lovely face and shall also order exquisite ornaments (for you).” and develops toward “Addressing queen Kaikeyi, the mother of Bharata, with extremely powerful words, that hunchback again made these utterances beneficial to Bharata and…”

This passage begins with “It is true that if Rama secures this kingdom, you along with your son are bound to suffer. O auspicious one, hence act in a way your son Bharata will be installed.” and develops toward “Thus queen Kaikeyi's face enveloped by the darkness of violent anger and her body stripped of all valuable ornaments, she lay restless, looking like…”

chapters.name: Sarga 10
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Perversely advised by the extremely sinful hunchback the queen (Kaikeyi) lay down on the ground like a kinnari struck as though by a poisonous arrow.” and develops toward “The maharaja entered the inner apartment of Kaikeyi's prosperous abode filled with parrots and peacocks, reverberating with cries of kraunchas and…”

This passage begins with “The maharaja entered the inner apartment of Kaikeyi's prosperous abode filled with parrots and peacocks, reverberating with cries of kraunchas and swans and sounds of musical instruments…” and develops toward “The guileless, old king beheld his youthful wife who was dearer to him than his own life, filled with deceitful intentions. She looked like a severed…”

This passage begins with “The guileless, old king beheld his youthful wife who was dearer to him than his own life, filled with deceitful intentions. She looked like a severed creeper, like a goddess fallen down,…” and develops toward “My authority on the land is extended till such places the wheel of any chariot rotates such as the regions in the east, SindhuSauvira areas,…”

This passage begins with “My authority on the land is extended till such places the wheel of any chariot rotates such as the regions in the east, SindhuSauvira areas, Saurashtra, Southern regions, Banga, Anga,…” and develops toward “Thus spoken to, she (Kaikeyi) breathed a sigh of relief. Intending to tell him the unpleasant thing (which was in her mind), she began tormenting her…”

chapters.name: Sarga 11
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “To the ruler of the earth (the king), struck by the darts of Kamadeva and overtaken by passion Kaikeyi spoke (these) dreadful words.” and develops toward “Delighted by the words (of the king), Kaikeyi disclosed him her highly dreadful wish which entered her mind like a sudden visitation of death:”

This passage begins with “You are repeatedly swearing to grant me boons. Let the thirtythree crore gods headed by the firegod, listen.” and develops toward “Preparations for Rama's consecration have been made. With these very materials for installation, my son Bharata should be consecrated.”

This passage begins with “Then in the war between gods and asuras, O lord, pleased with me you have promised a second boon and the time has come to grant it.” and develops toward “O king of kings, by being true to your vow, protect your race, character and lineage. Those sages whose ascetism is their wealth maintain that there…”

chapters.name: Sarga 12
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “After hearing the dreadful words of Kaikeyi, the maharaja (Dasaratha), engulfed in grief, was extremely distressed for a moment.” and develops toward “When I see my firstborn , I experience supreme delight. But if I do not see him, my life loses its meaning.”

This passage begins with “This world can exist without the Sun or crops (can be grown) without water but without Rama, life would not exist in my body.” and develops toward “O woman with attractive eyes, why do you want Rama who is so charming and serving you to be exiled to a distant place?”

This passage begins with “Rama is doing greater service to you than Bharata. Therefore, I do not find any reason for you to have a special love for Bharata.” and develops toward “O Kaikeyi I bow to you with folded palms. I touch your feet, too. Be the saviour of Rama. Let no sin taint me here (in this life).”

This passage begins with “Then the fierce Kaikeyi used fiercer words in reply to the king who was tormented with grief and filled with tears, senses switched off, head reeling, overwhelmed with pain, beseeching…” and develops toward “If I were to see Rama's mother receiving homage from everybody even for a day, I shall prefer my death.”

This passage begins with “O king, I swear on Bharata and on my own life that nothing less than Rama's banishment will satisfy me.” and develops toward “O cruel one, O woman of sinful resolve, O vile (creature), O woman of evil deeds, what wrong or sin do you really see in Rama or in me.”

This passage begins with “Depriving Rama, Bharata in no way will occupy the kingdom because in the matter of righteousness I think he is superior even to Rama.” and develops toward “Alas Sita will listen in a pitiable state the two unpleasant events namely my death and Rama's exile to the foest.”

This passage begins with “What a pity Vaidehi, lamenting like a Kinnari forsaken by a Kinnara on the Himalayan slopes, will cause death to me.” and develops toward “'(Rama) already emaciated by observing vows related to ritual and celibacy in compliance with the orders of the preceptors has to undergo hardships…”

This passage begins with “My son Rama will not say a second word to me, if I tell him 'Go to the forest'.He will say, 'Certainly so'.” and develops toward “You lived in my palace under the name of a princess. You have brought me, for sure, unparalleled dishonour and disgrace. Everybody will look upon me…”

This passage begins with “How will my son, Rama, who used to ride on chariots, elephants and horses roam the wilderness barefoot?” and develops toward “With Rama and Lakshmana withdrawn from me, my kinsmen killed, let Bharata along with you rule the city and the kingdom causing delight to my enemies.”

This passage begins with “O cruelhearted woman, you strike one in adversity. Why are your teeth with which you are now speaking (such harsh words) do not fall to the ground, broken into a thousand fragments?” and develops toward “The king, with his heart in the grip of a woman, wailing like an orphan tried to reach her feet, exceeding the limit of selfrespect but, unable to…”

chapters.name: Sarga 13
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The maharaja lying on the floor resembled (king) Yayati fallen from heaven after his merits (earned earlier) were exhausted. He neither deserved nor was accustomed to such (humiliating)…” and develops toward “If death comes to me right now I shall be very happy because I will not have to inflict suffering on Rama who does not deserve it.”

This passage begins with “O cruel, malevolent Kaikeyi, why do you propose such a harmful act against Rama who has truth as his prowess and who is so dear to me? Certainly, you will get unparalleled infamy and…” and develops toward “The king with a clean heart, whose eyes had been rendered red with weeping wailed piteously. Even after hearing it, that malicious woman full of…”

This passage begins with “Seeing his dear wife not pleased (despite his pleadings) and urging him, to the contrary, for the banishment of his son, the king overcome with grief fell down unconscious on the floor.” and develops toward “The night passed off with the highminded king lamenting this way and heaving deep sighs. When he got up, the eminent king stopped (the panegyrists)…”

chapters.name: Sarga 14
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The evil woman (Kaikeyi) said to the king of the Ikshvaku race who lay unconscious on the floor writhing and tormented with grief on account of his son.” and develops toward “Like a bullock, restlessly moving between the wheels and the yoke, Dasaratha's heart started beating wildly and his face turned pale.”

This passage begins with “The king was unable to see with his eyes overwhelmed (with tears). Gaining with great difficulty a firm footing and courage he addressed Kaikeyi:” and develops toward “I am bound by bond of righteousness. My intellect is destroyed. I wish to see my beloved, virtuous firstborn, Rama.”

This passage begins with “The night dawned. Ths Sun rose. The auspicious time with the sacred star in (favourable) conjunction arrived. The virtuous Vasistha, surrounded by his disciples, soon entered the best of…” and develops toward “Here are the pots filled with water from river Ganga and golden vessels with water from the seas. And throne made of udumbara wood for the…”

This passage begins with “Here are the pots filled with water from river Ganga and golden vessels with water from the seas. And throne made of udumbara wood for the installation ceremony. All kinds of seeds,…” and develops toward “Just as Indra's charioteer at this time (at dawn) extols him, the conquerer of demons, so do I rouse you (with a song).”

This passage begins with “Just as the Vedas and Vedangas awaken the selfborn lord Brahma, so do I awaken you.” and develops toward “Having heard the sorrowful words of the king and having seen his wretched state Sumantra with palms folded moved away a little from the place.”

This passage begins with “When the king himself in that wretched state was not able to speak to Sumantra, crafty Kaikeyi said to him in reply:” and develops toward “Then Sumantra hastened towards the gate where he saw kings, wealthy people and citizens who had already arrived and waiting there. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 15
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The brahmins wellversed in the Vedas stayed waiting all night and assembled at the pavilion (for the consecration ceremony) along with royal priests.” and develops toward “Eight auspicious maidens adorned with all types of ornaments, all sorts of musical instruments, panegyrists and others (were in waiting).”

This passage begins with “Just as articles necessary for the consecration of the descendants of the Ikshvakus, are customarily arranged in the kingdom, in the same way things were arranged for the coronation of the…” and develops toward “O scion of the Raghu dynasty brahmins, army commanders and merchants have arrived and they beseech your audience. Please awake.”

This passage begins with “Dasaratha waking up saw the charioteer and expert counsellor Sumantra extoling him, and said:” and develops toward “The charioteer (Sumantra) beheld Rama's palace closed with two heavypanelled doors. Adorned with hundreds of galleries, it had a peak mounted with…”

This passage begins with “The charioteer (Sumantra) beheld Rama's palace closed with two heavypanelled doors. Adorned with hundreds of galleries, it had a peak mounted with golden idols. The arches were studded with…” and develops toward “Important ministers who were dear to the king and welladorned arrived there on their horses, chariots and elephants. Sumantra finding his way through…”

This passage begins with “The palace looked like a mountain peak covered by a cloud and a great vimana surrounded by splendid apartments. The charioteer entered the palace unobstructed like a crocodile entering the…” and develops toward “The palace looked like a mountain peak covered by a cloud and a great vimana surrounded by splendid apartments. The charioteer entered the palace…”

chapters.name: Sarga 16
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Sumantra, who was conversant with ancient traditions, passed through the entrance of the inner apartment full of people and reached the sparsely crowded courtyard. There alert, loyal young…” and develops toward “On seeing Rama of charming countenance relaxing on a couch, Sumantra, honourable to the king (Dasaratha) said these words with folded palms.”

This passage begins with “O Rama, worthy son of Kausalya, your father along with queen Kaikeyi desires to see you.You may go there. Please do not delay.” and develops toward “May Indra protect you on the east, Yama in the south, Varuna in the west and Kubera in the north.”

This passage begins with “Adorned in auspicious ornaments, Rama took leave of Sita and departed from his palace accompanied by Sumantra.” and develops toward “At that time, sounds of musical instruments and songs of eulogy by panegyrists, roarings of warriors were heard on the way.”

This passage begins with “Rama, the subduer of enemies, proceeded while the wellattired women standing at the windows of their mansions showered heaps of flowers on him from every side.” and develops toward “With bards, panegyrists and great musical performers invoking blessings and walking ahead, he (Rama) proceeded, like Kubera amid intoxicated…”

chapters.name: Sarga 17
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Surrounded by friends overwhelmed with great joy, that glorious Rama aboard the chariot beheld the city decorated with banners and pennants and made fragrant with expensive incense and…” and develops toward “Indifferent to such warm words by his friends in his favour Rama passed along the highway.”

This passage begins with “Even though Rama, the best among men had driven past them none was able to withdraw his mind or eyes from him. (They continued).” and develops toward “When the prince approached his father all the people were delighted and awaited his return like the ocean waiting for the rise of the Moon. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 18
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama beheld his father reclined on an auspicious couch and by his side was seated Kaikeyi. He looked wretched with a pale face.” and develops toward “Father has always been affectionate to me but today why is he in a dejected mood? Why does his countenance look pale and wretched? Why does he not…”

This passage begins with “Is he suffering from any physical ailment or mental agony? It is affliction of either the body or the mind. (For) perpetual happiness is indeed rare.” and develops toward “O Rama, this (truth) is the basis of righteousness. The virtuous also know this. The king being angry (with me), should not abandon the truth for…”

This passage begins with “If you fulfil whatever the king says, pleasant or unpleasant, I shall relate to you everything.” and develops toward “Bharata shall be consecrated, O descendant of the Raghu dynasty with all these preparations made by the king to install you.”

This passage begins with “Forsake this consecration, wear matted locks and deerskin and take refuge in Dandaka forest for fourteen years.” and develops toward “Even though she was speaking harsh words, Rama did not come to grief. But the magnanimous king was greatly afflicted to see the calamity on his son.…”

chapters.name: Sarga 19
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The destroyer of enemies (Rama) did not feel distressed to hear these words painful like death. To Kaikeyi he said:” and develops toward “Kaikeyi, happy to hear the words of the son of the Raghus as she was convinced that his departure (to the forest) was certain, hastened him (to set…”

This passage begins with “Let it be so. Messengers shall go at once on swift horses to bring Bharata from his maternal uncle's house.” and develops toward “Since in my case you have exercised more authority (than my father) and pleaded with the king, it follows, you, for sure, do not see any virtue in…”

This passage begins with “After taking leave of my mother and consoling Sita, today itself I shall go to that wild Dandaka forest.” and develops toward “All the glorious people around him did not observe any change in the countenance of that dignified and truthful Rama.”

This passage begins with “The mightyarmed (Rama) did not leave his habitual cheerfulness like the autumnal Moon his own brightness.” and develops toward “Rama entered the palace which was full of great rejoicing. He did not disclose the obstruction that had come on the way of achieving his objective…”

chapters.name: Sarga 20
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When the tiger among men (Rama) was coming out with folded palms, there arose a huge cry of distress among the ladies of the harem:” and develops toward “Rama wished the brahmins and saw old men, women and children vigilantly guarding the door in the third courtyard.”

This passage begins with “Wellpleased, the women then greeted him wishing prosperity. They hurried into the abode of Kausalya and gave her the happy news (of Rama's arrival).” and develops toward “O scion of the Raghu race see the king, your righteous and truthful father, who will consecrate you as princeregent today itself.”

This passage begins with “Invited (by Kausalya) to partake the food, Rama out of abashment only touched the seat provided to him and having stretched his folded palms spoke to his mother.” and develops toward “O my son, if you were not born to me, I, even as a woman without progeny, would not have experienced grief, more intense than this.”

This passage begins with “O my son a barren woman has only one mental agony that she has no progeny. Except that she does not have any other sorrow.” and develops toward “Through meditation, through fasts and with a great deal of efforts you have been brought up by this luckless (mother). But alas this is all in vain.”

This passage begins with “I think my heart must be very firm (hard) like the bark of a great river which, touched by (a flood of) fresh water in the rainy season does not disintegrate.” and develops toward “Kausalya, filled with anger due to extreme sorrow reflected over her misfortune, and burst into harrowing tears, looking at Rama, like a kinnari…”

chapters.name: Sarga 21
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While Rama's mother, Kausalya was lamenting, miserable Lakshmana said to her these words appropriate to the occasion:” and develops toward “Even if our father instigated by Kaikeyi becomes our enemy, he shall be imprisoned or even slain regardless of our relationship with him.”

This passage begins with “Even a preceptor who follows the unrighteous path and is filled with haughtiness and does not know how to discriminate between good and bad, deserves to be disciplined (punished).” and develops toward “Just as the king is worthy of your respectful homage, so am I. I will not permit you to go from here to the forest.”

This passage begins with “Separated from you, I have no use of this life or happiness. I prefer to live with you even if it means eating grass.” and develops toward “I will not do as duty anything contrary to what is done by others in this world. No one who obeys the commands of his father falls off the path of…”

This passage begins with “One who is the best among the knowers of speech and best of all archers, Rama spoke to his mother in this manner and then said to Lakshmana:” and develops toward “You, Sita, Lakshmana, Sumitra and I -- all of us shall abide by the words of my father. This indeed is eternal law.”

This passage begins with “O mother in withdrawing the articles (for consecration) and also in subduing the sorrow in your heart approve of my plan to dwell in the forest.” and develops toward “While the righteous king is living, more importantly treading the righteous path, how can mother Kausalya, like an ordinary widow, accompany me…”

This passage begins with “Grant me permission O mother, to depart for the forest. Bless me and perform ceremonies for my wellbeing so that I shall return (to the city) after completion (of my exile) like Yayati (who…” and develops toward “Rama, the best among men, thus pacified his mother insisting on his desire to depart for Dandaka forest with courage. And elaborately instructed his…”

chapters.name: Sarga 22
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The selfpossessed Rama held his composure and approached his beloved, intimate brother Lakshmana who was (looking) miserable with his mental agony. To him, who was hissing like a king…” and develops toward “Immediately after my departure to the forest, the king's (Kekaya's) daughter having accomplished her objective, will enthrone Bharata without any…”

This passage begins with “Kaikeyi will attain peace of mind only after my departure to the forest in tattered clothes, an antelope skin and a crown of matted locks.” and develops toward “Any unanticipated and sudden hindrance to the action undertaken shall be deemed to be the act of destiny.”

This passage begins with “Even though my consecration was thwarted, there is no feeling of sadness in me. In fact I controlled my mind by my intellect.” and develops toward “O Lakshmana our younger mother (Kaikeyi) cannot be blamed for her obstruction to my coronation. Overpowered by destiny, she is speaking such…”

chapters.name: Sarga 23
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While Rama was speaking thus, Lakshmana listened to him with his head down, experiencing both grief and joy.” and develops toward “When you are able to counter (this move), why do you want to execute the unjustifiable and contemptible words of the king who is under the spell of…”

This passage begins with “I am grieved that even though this plot has been hatched by a treacherous mind, you are not taking it in the right perspective. Censurable is adherence to virtue.” and develops toward “Any one who opposes my terrible valour will come to grief. The power of destiny will not cause that much sorrow as my valour will.”

This passage begins with “When after ruling the subjects for a thousand years you withdraw into the forest (for yativrata) your sons will rule them.” and develops toward “Striking each of my foes with many arrows and many with one arrow, I shall aim them at the vitals of men, horses and elephants.”

This passage begins with “O Lord today you shall see the power of my glorious weapons in depriving the king (Dasaratha) of his authority and establishing your supremacy over the kingdom.” and develops toward “Tell me your enemy who should be deprived of his life, fame and friends. Command me as to how this earth shall come under your control. Now itself I…”

chapters.name: Sarga 24
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having perceived that Rama was determined to carry out his father's command, Kausalya with her throat choked with tears said these words of piety:” and develops toward “For a woman, to desert her husband is a cruel act. You must not do that which is contemptible even to think.”

This passage begins with “As long as the king, my father, the lord of the earth, a descendant of Kakutshta lives, you shall serve him. This is eternal virtue (in a woman).” and develops toward “See that the old king does not succumb to this terrible grief. Serve him with a composed mind.”

This passage begins with “A woman may be highly pious engaged in vows and fastings, but if she does not follow her husband, she will be condemned to hell.” and develops toward “Go now, O my mightyarmed son and come back safe. And on your return, delight me with your sweet, gentle utterances.”

This passage begins with “O my dear child, how pleasing would it be, if today were to be the day of your return from the forest when I can see you wearing matted hair and robes of bark” and develops toward “Seeing Rama full of auspicious qualities resolved to go to the forest and having spoken to him with the fullness of her heart, Devi Kausalya now…”

chapters.name: Sarga 25
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Highminded mother Kausalya, her anguish subdued, performed the sipping rite (achamana) and her self rendered pure, performed auspicious ceremonies for Rama.” and develops toward “May Siddhas, guardians of the quarters invoked by me protect you from all directions in that forest at all times, O my son”

This passage begins with “When you dwell in the forest may all the mountains, seas, lord Varuna, heaven, space, and earth and also all rivers, all stars and planets with their presiding deities, day, night, dawn and…” and develops toward “May Fire, Wind, Smoke and Mantras emanated from the mouths of rishis protect you when you take your ablutions, O Son of the Raghu race O Joy of the…”

This passage begins with “May Siva, lord of all the worlds, Visnu, supporter of living beings, Brahma, rishis and all the rest of the gods protect you while you dwell in the forest” and develops toward “O mightyarmed Rama may the auspicious and prosperous seasons, seas, islands. the Vedas, the worlds and the quarters bless you”

This passage begins with “The largeeyed Kausalya placed the remains of the offerings on his (Rama's) head and applied sandal cream to his body. She tied an auspicious medicinal herb known as Vishalyakarani to his…” and develops toward “Circumambulated by mother Kausalya, the illustrious scion of the Raghu race clasped his mother's feet again and again. And shining in his glory,…”

chapters.name: Sarga 26
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “After the benedictory rites were performed by his mother Kausalya, Rama paid obeisance to her and staying on the righteous path decided to depart to the forest. He entered the highway…” and develops toward “(How is it that) O best among men eloquent panegyrists, charioteers, bards are not seen singing your praise with auspicious words?”

This passage begins with “How is it that brahmins versed in the Vedas have not duly sprinkled the mixture of honey and curd on your head (prior to your consecration)?” and develops toward “I have come to see you before departing for the desolate Dandaka forest. You should never praise me in the presence of Bharata because intellectuals…”

This passage begins with “I have come to see you before departing for the desolate Dandaka forest. You should never praise me in the presence of Bharata because intellectuals cannot tolerate others being praised…” and develops toward “Kings abandon (even) their own sons if they are harmful and receive capable people even if they are strangers.”

This passage begins with “O auspicious one live here by being obedient to king Bharata. Absorbed in righteousness, follow truth and (religious) vows.” and develops toward “O my beloved Sita I am going to the wild forest. Stay here and never cause displeasure to any one in any way. Could you carry out these words of…”

This passage begins with “O respector of individuals when you are capable of protecting other people in the forest, what of me?” and develops toward “Although Sita was thus appealing, Rama, devoted to righteousness, was (still) unwilling to take her. sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard the words of Rama, thus spoke Sita in a faint voice in sadness, with tears flowing incessantly from her eyes:” and develops toward “I am aware, O valiant one, that there are hardships of every kind in forest life. This passage begins with “When I was young in my father's house, a holy female mendicant foretold in the presence of my mother that I would dwell in the forest (in this life).” and develops toward “That selfpossessed scion of the Kakutstha dynasty consoled the copperredlipped Sita in several ways to dissuade her (from going to the forest).…”

This passage begins with “I shall regard, O charming Rama, the harmful dust raised by the stormy wind and settled on me as the most excellent sandal powder.” and develops toward “Crystallike tears arising out of distress flowed from her eyes like water trickling from two lotuses.”

This passage begins with “Her face with large eyes assumed the brightness of an immaculate Moon and withered with tears like a lotus plucked out of water.” and develops toward “By fulfilling father's wish, nothing is difficult to achieve, whether it is heaven, or wealth, or foodgrains, or learning, or sons or other comforts.”

This passage begins with “Great men who are devoted to the service of their parents attain the world of the gods, of gandharvas, of cows, of Visnu, of Brahma and other worlds.” and develops toward “Having come to know that her departure to the forest is acceptable to her husband, Sita, highly delighted, quickly got ready to give away (things).”

This passage begins with “Therefore, people are deeply pained to see Rama afflicted. They look like aquatic creatures in summer when the water dries up.” and develops toward “Let Kaikeyi along with her son and her relatives acquire that country which will be inhabited by birds and animals living on grass, fruits, and…”

I swear this in your presence in the name of truth and on my merits acquired.”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “As the night advanced towards dawn, broadchested Rama of great renown said to Lakshmana, son of Sumitra, who had auspicious marks on his body:” and develops toward “The charioteer (Sumantra) approached the righteous Rama and asked him with folded hands: What can I do?”

This passage begins with “Rama then caressed Sumantra with his right hand and said, Sumantra, go back immediately and never be inattentive in serving the king.” and develops toward “To make Kaikeyi happy, do whatever little the great king Dasaratha orders you to do.”

This passage begins with “The minds of kings create no hurdles in all that they want to do. That is why they rule kingdoms.” and develops toward “Princeregency is awaiting you because of our father's loving desire. See that through you (your actions) he is able to attain happiness in both the…”

This passage begins with “When Sumantra was asked by Rama to go back, he grew griefstricken. Having heard all that was said by the scion of the Kakutsthas, he replied:” and develops toward “That is why, O sinless one I cannot go back to Ayodhya without you. Allow me to accompany you into your exile.”

This passage begins with “If you forsake me in spite of my request I shall immediately enter fire along with my chariot.” and develops toward “O Sumantra, listen I know your deep devotion to me (I also know) you are loyal to your master (Dasaratha). So I am sending you to the city (Ayodhya)…”

This passage begins with “When my younger mother Kaikeyi sees that you have returned to Ayodhya, she will believe that Rama has really gone to the forest.” and develops toward “O Guha you must be vigilant about your army, treasury, forts and provinces. A kingdom, it is (rightly) said, is protected with great difficulty.”

This passage begins with “Rama, Delight of the Iksvakus, then permitted Guha to take leave. And departed quickly with his consort and Lakshmana in peace.” and develops toward “O Ganga, protected by you, this son (Rama) of Dasaratha, the sagacious maharaja, will carry out his father's command. He along with his brother will…”

This passage begins with “O Ganga, protected by you, this son (Rama) of Dasaratha, the sagacious maharaja, will carry out his father's command. He along with his brother will return after living in the forest for…” and develops toward “O best of men, I will follow you and Sita and keep watch. We must ensure mutual protection.”

This passage begins with “We have not yet confronted any hardship. From now on Sita will come to know the difficulties of forest life.” and develops toward “Famished, they (Rama and Lakshmana) killed a boar, a rishya (whitefooted male antelope), a spotted deer and a great deer with black stripes. They…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On reaching the foot of the tree, Rama offered oblations to Sandhya of the west. And then he who is the best in keeping people in good humour said to Lakshmana:” and develops toward “The king is advanced in age and I have to dwell in the forest. Therefore, Bharata alone will be the chief of this entire kingdom.”

This passage begins with “Any king who abandons dharma and artha and adopts a life of pleasure will very soon reach this state as Dasaratha has attained.” and develops toward “My mother is unlucky as she has been separated from me. Extremely distressed, she must have plunged into an ocean of grief.”

This passage begins with “Once enraged, O Lakshmana I can overpower Ayodhya, even the whole world all by myself with my arrows. But exhibition of prowess without any reason is not desirable.” and develops toward “Then the two mighty perpetuators of the Raghu race, who were like two lions dwelling on the slopes of mountains, lived in that lonely forest without…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “They (Rama, Sita and Lakshmana) spent that auspicious night under that great tree and set out from there after the bright sun rose.” and develops toward “Rama entered the hermitage and saw that highsouled Bharadwaja surrounded by his disciples. He was a distinguished sage who (himself) kindled…”

This passage begins with “Lakshmana's elder brother (Rama) introduced himself (to Bharadwaja), O venerable one, we are both sons of Dasaratha, Rama and Lakshmana.” and develops toward “O venerable sage, I feel the people living in nearby villages and towns, perceiving that we can be easily seen here, will frequent this ashram. This…”

This passage begins with “O venerable sage, I feel the people living in nearby villages and towns, perceiving that we can be easily seen here, will frequent this ashram. This is the reason I don't wish to stay here.” and develops toward “When the night passed into dawn, Rama the best of men approached the resplendent Bharadwaja and said.”

This passage begins with “O venerable sage devoted to truth, we have spent the night in your hermitage. Please permit us to depart for the dwellingplace (suggested by you).” and develops toward “The auspicious mountain (Chaitrakuta) resounds with the note of cheerful lapwings and cuckoos. Intoxicated elephants and deer move about in the…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “After spending the night, the princes, destroyers of enemies, offered obeisance to that great sage (Bharadhwaja) and went towards that mount Chitrkuta.” and develops toward “Thus talking with one another, the two nobleminded tigers (best) among men advanced towards river Kalindi (Yamuna) with Sita walking in front.”

This passage begins with “Thereafter, they all reached the rapidly flowing river Kalindi and began pondering how to cross it.” and develops toward “Having reached the banyan tree, Sita invoked it, saying, 'O great tree I offer you my salutations. May my husband fulfil his vow May I behold…”

This passage begins with “Having reached the banyan tree, Sita invoked it, saying, 'O great tree I offer you my salutations. May my husband fulfil his vow May I behold Kausalya and the illustrious Sumitra on my…” and develops toward “They reached the tableland on the bank of that river and moved in high spirit in the lovely forest resounding with cries of flocks of peacocks and…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When the night was over, Rama, Delight of the Raghus, gently awakened Lakshmana who was asleep.” and develops toward “Then they both along with Sita walked to that lovely, delightful Chitrakuta mountain.”

This passage begins with “Rama arrived at that enchanting mountain full of various kinds of birds, fruits and roots, and sweet water. (And said to Lakshmana:)” and develops toward “Lakshmana, slayer of heroes on the enemy's side, understood and implemented what he was told. Rama again said to him:”

This passage begins with “Cook this venison, O handsome one We will offer it to the presiding deity of this hut. Hasten, the day and time are fixed (for the rites)” and develops toward “Then they with their senses conquered, enjoyed perfect happiness in that splendid forest teeming with many varieties of animals and birds, with trees…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Until Rama reached the southern bank, Guha kept talking with Sumantra for long and returned home with great sorrow.” and develops toward “He saw them gathering in groups, saying, Henceforth we will not be able to see Rama and without him, we are lost indeed.”

This passage begins with “No longer will we see Rama at sacrifices, weddings, great assemblies and at places of charitable activity.” and develops toward “On entering the eighth courtyard, he saw in a pale white chamber king Dasaratha desolate, anguished and withered with the sorrow of separation from…”

The city was crowded again. इत्यार्षे…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When the king regained his consciousness and was consoled, he summoned the charioteer to hear news about Rama.” and develops toward “Tell me, O charioteer where Rama sat, where he slept, what (food) he ate. Only your words will enable me to live like Yayati among saints.”

This passage begins with “Thus questioned by the king the charioteer, his voice choked with tears and words faltering in grief, addressed the king:” and develops toward “Shedding profuse tears, Rama continued, 'Look after my mother who is covetous of her son as your own mother'.”

This passage begins with “While saying this the red lotuslike eyes of illustrious Rama streamed with tears, O maharaja” and develops toward “When she saw me leaving, she looked at her husband with a dry (pale) face and suddenly burst into tears.”

This passage begins with “Supported by Lakshmana's arms, Rama stood, his face streaming with tears and palms folded, while the wretched Sita, glaring at me and the royal chariot, weeping. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…” and develops toward “Supported by Lakshmana's arms, Rama stood, his face streaming with tears and palms folded, while the wretched Sita, glaring at me and the royal…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Listening to the charioteer, the best of ministers (Sumantra), the king asked him to tell the rest.” and develops toward “Bunches of flowers grown in water and on land, as well as fruits with their scant fragrance do not shine as before.”

This passage begins with “Pleasuregardens are all deserted as the birds have vanished. O best of men, as such they do not look beautiful.” and develops toward “Surely it is destiny and this calamity has befallen for the wilful destruction of the (entire) race, O charioteer”

This passage begins with “O Charioteer, if ever I have rendered you any favour, quickly take me to Rama. My life is hastening me (fast running out).” and develops toward “O queen Kausalya, I am completely immersed in this ocean of sorrow. The misfortune due to Rama's separation is its breadth. Sita's separation is the…”

This passage begins with “Though I wish I cannot see Rama together with Lakshmana. Indeed this is very unfortunate. Lamenting thus, king Dasaratha of great reknown fell on the bed, unconscious.” and develops toward “Wailing, Dasaratha fell unconscious. He was doubly grieved due to his yearning for Rama. Rama's mother was seized with fear hearing those…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Thereafter Kausalya, trembling as if possessed by an evil spirit and rolling on the floor like one who her senses now and again, said to the charioteer (Sumantra):” and develops toward “Seeing villages, cities, trees of different kinds and courses of rivers, Sita is enquiring from Rama or Lakshmana about them. She feels as though she…”

This passage begins with “Seeing villages, cities, trees of different kinds and courses of rivers, Sita is enquiring from Rama or Lakshmana about them. She feels as though she is sporting in the pleasuregarden just…” and develops toward “Though Sumantra was speaking with befitting words to prevent Kausalya from weeping over her son which had enfeebled her, she did not stop crying, 'O…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When Rama, the greatest among those who please the people, he who was conscious of his duty left for the forest. Kausalya, sobbing in bitter anguish, said to her husband:” and develops toward “Some first feed their own relatives in shraddha ceremony and thereafter look for the best of brahmins to feed.”

This passage begins with “The godlike brahmins who are virtuous and learned will not accept the food after others have partaken even if it is ambrosia.” and develops toward “Valiant and mightyarmed Rama, with his golden shafts can annihilate all living beings and oceans like at the time of deluge everything is consumed in…”

This passage begins with “Such Rama, the best among men, powerful like a lion, with eyes fierce like that of a bull has been destroyed by his own father just as the big fish destroys its own offsprings.” and develops toward “O king for a woman the first refuge is the husband, the second her son, the third her relatives and there is no fourth alternative in this world.”

chapters.name: Sarga 62
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Forced to hear the harsh words of angry Kausalya, the king, deeply distressed, was absorbed in sad thought.” and develops toward “O lord, by falling on the ground at your feet, bowing my head I beg of you to be pleased. By your act of beseeching me, I have been badly hurt. I do…”

This passage begins with “O heroic one a woman, who is entreated by her sagacious and praiseworthy husband, loses both the worlds.” and develops toward “While Kausalya was speaking these hearttouching words, the rays of the Sun began to fade and the night fell.”

chapters.name: Sarga 63
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Waking up in a moment tormented with grief, the king began to ponder once again.” and develops toward “Just as a boy consumes poison out of delusion, O Devi, this sorrow has befallen me as a result of my own action.”

This passage begins with “Like some one who is deluded by palasa trees I too did not realise the consequences of shooting the target following the direction of the sound.” and develops toward “Then at a place that was out of the range of my sight in darkness I heard the sound of a pitcher being filled with water which resembled the sound of…”

This passage begins with “Wishing to kill the elephant, I seized my arrow glowing like a venomous snake and aimed it towards the sound.” and develops toward “On hearing the piteous voice the bow and arrow dropped from my (trembling) hands since I was keen on following the righteous path.”

This passage begins with “Having heard his pathetic lamentations at night, I was extremely frightened by the force of grief and stood bewildered.” and develops toward “'Gently remove the arrow from my body, O king the sharp arrow is piercing deep into my vital just like the current of the river hits its elevated…”

This passage begins with “The thought of removing the arrow kept me pondering. If not removed, it will be painful, and if removed, he will die.” and develops toward “'O king Dasaratha, lord of the land, I am not a brahmin. Feel no sense of guilt in your mind. I was born of a sudra mother and a vaisya father.”

chapters.name: Sarga 64
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Grieving for having unfairly killed the great sage the rightenous descendant of the Raghus (Dasaratha) coutinued to tell Kausalya about it:” and develops toward “After a lot of effort, with great fear controlling my thoughts and the power of speech, I related to him the calamity that had befallen his son.”

This passage begins with “O magnanimous sage I am a kshatriya known as Dasaratha. I am not your son. A pathetic incident has been caused by my own action, condemnable by the virtuous.” and develops toward “'If a kshatriya kills any one knowingly, particularly when in vanaprastha (third stage) this sin can displace even Indra, wielder of the thunderbolt…”

This passage begins with “'If any one deliberately discharges a weapon upon an ascetic observing austerities or on him who expounds the Vedas, his head will split into seven pieces.” and develops toward “'O son how can I support your wretched and pitiable mother, who is blind, old and yearning for her son?”

This passage begins with “'My dear son, stay with me, do not go (now) to the abode of Yama, (lord of death). Tomorrow accompanied by me and your mother you may” and develops toward “The virtuous son of that ascetic assumed a celestial form through the merit of his good deeds and ascended heaven at once with Indra.”

This passage begins with “The ascetic in the company of Indra consoling the aged couple for a brief moment said:” and develops toward “Just as a man eating forbidden food and drink falls sick, so have I fallen prey to grief, because of my sin, O Devi”

This passage begins with “O gentle lady, the words of that noble ascetic have come true today. Hence I am now going to give up my life.” and develops toward “Lucky men will behold Rama return to Ayodhya after the completion of his exile, like the planet Venus moving forward on its orbit.”

This passage begins with “O Kausalya, my heart, it appears is sinking and on account of delusion I am not able to feel the sense objects like sound touch and taste together.” and develops toward “King Dasaratha, a man of noble vision and lord of men, anguished by the exile of his son, kept pitiably muttering till past midnight until tormented…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then on the following day (unaware of Dasaratha's death) panegyrists uttering benedictions, highly accomplished bards and genealogists proficient in reciting auspicious words, singers…” and develops toward “The women who usually attend to the bed (maids who do the bed in the morning) of Dasaratha (king of Kosala) without any restrictions, approached the…”

This passage begins with “Conducting themselves agreeably with restraint and obedience, the women touched the bed of Dasaratha but did not perceive any sign of life.” and develops toward “Dasaratha with his body lying still, all the women looked at Kausalya fallen down on the floor like a slain female elephant.”

This passage begins with “Then all the wives of the king led by Kaikeyi, burning with grief and weeping, lost their senses and fell down.” and develops toward “Realising that the great and illustrious king, Dasaratha, had passed away, his wives gathered around him, weeping piteously and bitterly and holding…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On seeing the king who ascended the heaven just as a blazing fire suddenly extinguishes, or like the ocean emptied of water or the Sun bereft of splendour Kausalya raised the head of the…” and develops toward “As his faithful wife, I shall enter the fire by clasping this (Dasaratha's) body and go to death today itself”

This passage begins with “The servants led away that unfortunate Kausalya who was lamenting in great anguish embracing the body of Dasaratha.” and develops toward “Like night without the Moon, like a woman forsaken by her husband, this city of Ayodhya without that magnanimous king has lost its earlier splendour.”

This passage begins with “With the people of Ayodhya shedding tears, the housewives crying, in anguish, 'alas alas' the courtyards houses and squares of the roads deserted, the city has lost its earlier brilliance.” and develops toward “After the demise of the king, men and women in the city assembled in groups and denounced the mother of Bharata. sections.summary: This passage begins with “The night stretched interminably in Ayodhya, which was filled with sobbing people rendered cheerless by their lamentations.” and develops toward “In a country where there is no king, no assemblies are convened by pleasure seeking people, nor do they build delightful gardens or sacred edifices.”

This passage begins with “In a country without a king, selfcontrolled brahmins in charge of sacrificial ceremonies, wellversed in the study of Vedas and strict followers of austerity will not function.” and develops toward “In a country without a king, there is no security of possessions and the army cannot withstand the enemy in a battle.”

This passage begins with “In a country without a king, welldressed men are no longer enthusiastic to drive on excellent horses and on chariots.” and develops toward “If the king did not show the distinction between the good and evil actions, this world would have been enveloped by darkness. Nothing could have been…”

This passage begins with “Even while the great king was alive, we never defied your word like the ocean which does not overstep its shore.” and develops toward “O Vasistha, the best of brahmins, considering all that has happened and knowing that a kingdom without a king is like a forest, you may consecrate a…”

There they met the…” and develops toward “O tiger among men (Bharata) those of whose welfare you are enquiring are all doing well. May the goddess of wealth and prosperity await you and let…”

This passage begins with “Having thus been addressed, Bharata said to the messengers. Let me seek the permission of the great king of Kekaya to leave and say goodbye, informing him that the messengers are hastening…” and develops toward “All the wealth given by the king of Kekaya did not delight Bharata, son of Kaikeyi who was in a hurry to depart for Ayodhya.”

This passage begins with “Then his heart was filled with extreme anxiety due to the haste of the messengers to depart and also due to the dream he had.” and develops toward “Under the protection of the army, the illustrious Bharata who was devoid of enemies, accompanied by ministers who were comparable to him and…”

This passage begins with “I see the unswept homes of householders standing dirty with doors not closed. There is no beauty anywhere. No one offers oblations (at the time of worship). sections.summary: This passage begins with “Not finding his father in his abode, Bharata went to see his mother in her apartment.” and develops toward “I had come here with the hope to see king Dasaratha who spends most of his time in my mother's residence. But today I do not see him .”

This passage begins with “I would like to pay obeisance at my father's feet. Will you answer my question, O mother, and tell me regarding his whereabouts? Or perchance could he be at the residence of my eldest…” and develops toward “O illustrious prince arise, arise. Why are you lying down on the floor? Learned people like you, who are worthy of honour in the assembly ought not…”

This passage begins with “O highly intelligent one, by your wisdom, virtuous conduct and scriptural knowledge you are entitled to dispense charity and perform sacrifices. You will always shine incessantly like light…” and develops toward “The magnanimous king, the foremost of those who attained the excellent state after, death, went to the other world, lamenting 'O Rama, O Sita, O…”

He never casts his eyes on another's…”

This passage begins with “My son, I on hearing of Rama's consecration asked your father to confer the kingdom on you and banish Rama.” and develops toward “My son, give up sorrow and agony and take recourse to courage. This city as well as the kingdom, free from all obstacles, is under your control.”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

What possible reason do you have for sending him, clothed in bark, into…”

This passage begins with “Being avaricious, you are, I think, unaware of my devotion to Rama. This great calamity has been brought about by you for (your greed for) the kingdom.” and develops toward “Even the kings in your ancestral family were highly distinguished men. How did this condemnable deflection originate in your mind?”

This passage begins with “O woman of sinful disposition, I on my part will not fulfil your cherished desire. You have started a distressing deed to bring an end to my existence.” and develops toward “Having inflicted on his mother these unpleasant words in this manner, the highsouled Bharata, overwhelmed with grief, roared again like a lion in a…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Overtaken by great wrath, Bharata reproached his mother and then said to her again:” and develops toward “O woman of sinful convictions you will go to hell. You have separated righteous Kausalya from her son. For having committed such a sin, what…”

This passage begins with “O cruel one why do you not realise that Rama, born of Kausalya, is the eldest son and equal to my father? He is selfrestrained and a refuge to his kith and kin.” and develops toward “'God forbid, O Indra, lord of the Immortals no calamity has befallen you from any quarter. Only I am in trouble. I am weeping for these pair of…”

This passage begins with “'These bulls were born from my body and they are oppressed by the burden. Having seen their anguish, I am extremely grieved. sections.summary: This passage begins with “On regaining his senses after a long time, the valiant Bharata looked at his unfortunate mother whose eyes were filled with tears, and condemned her amidst the counsellors.” and develops toward “What advantage did cruel Kaikeyi visualize in sending away my son to dwell in the forest wearing robes of bark?”

This passage begins with “Kaikeyi should send me also without any delay to that place where my illustrious Rama with golden navel dwells.” and develops toward “May the one who counselled the exile of my esteemed brother acquire the sin in the same way as a man acting treacherously against a king who rules…”

This passage begins with “May the one who counselled the exile of my esteemed brother acquire unrighteousness in the same way as a king who, having received one sixth of the income as tax from people does not…” and develops toward “May the one who counselled the exile of my esteemed brother live an entire life of anguish without begetting children through his wife or without…”

This passage begins with “May the one who counselled the exile of my esteemed brother acquire the same sin as one acquires for murdering a king, a woman, children and the aged and deserting dependents” and develops toward “The one who counselled the exile of my noble, longarmed and broadchested brother be banished like the one who commited the sin of leaving the…”

This passage begins with “May the one who counselled the exile of my esteemed brother be always miserable with many sons, poverty and fever and disease” and develops toward “Kausalya said these words to Bharata who fell unconscious, consumed with grief, while swearing on painful oaths.”

This passage begins with “O son by swearing on oaths, you are choking the very breath of my life and my suffering has grown still more.” and develops toward “Afflicted by sorrow and lamenting thus, great Bharata's mind became unsteady owing to swooning and besieged by grief.”

This passage begins with “The sacrificial fire of the king has been put outside the firesanctuary in accordance with the ritual precepts and were withdrawn by the attendants and priests wellversed in Vedic lore.” and develops toward “The wives of the king, counsellors and priests along with Bharata offered waterlibation and entered the city with tearful eyes. sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having observed the tenday mourning period, Bharata purified himself on the twelfth day and got the shraddha rites (obsequies) performed.” and develops toward “Recollecting the many virtues of his father again and again, like a madman, Satrughna, mentally unsteady, lamented in profound sorrow.”

This passage begins with “With Manthara as the source, Kaikeyi as a crocodile, this terrible sea of sorrow which has submerged all of us lies unruffled.” and develops toward “The knower of reality Sumantra helped Satrughna to rise, after having consoled him, imparted him the truth about the inevitability of birth and death.”

This passage begins with “Having risen from the earth both the illustrious tigers among men, Bharata and Satrughna looked faded and weather beaten like the banners of Indra that had faded under the Sun and rain.” and develops toward “The counsellors hastened up both the sons, who were wiping away the tears and speaking with blodshot eyes, to complete the remaining part of the…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “As the griefstricken Bharata was intending to proceed to Rama, Lakshmana's younger brother Satrughna said to him:” and develops toward “Saying thus, he forcibly caught hold of the hunchback amidst her companions when she filled the house with her cries.”

This passage begins with “Perceiving the enraged Satrughna all her companions shook in terror and fled in different directions.” and develops toward “Hearing the words of Bharata, Satrughna, the younger brother of Lakshmana restrained his fury and also released Manthara.”

This passage begins with “Manthara, tormented with grief, fell at the feet of Kaikeyi and began heaving deep sighs.” and develops toward “. Seeing the hunchback thrown down by Satrughna and lying senseless and anguished like a female krauncha bird caught in a net looking in different…”

I shall dwell in the inaccessible forest and Rama shall become king.”

This passage begins with “Let the road be aligned by artisans and uneven ground be levelled. The guards who judge the inaccessible terrains should follow them on their way.” and develops toward “All the ministers and members of the council, relieved of their distress, were delighted to hear these words and said O best among men, on your…”

This passage begins with “The highway for the army was paved smooth, with its surface decorated with mosaic. It was lined with blossoming trees with flocks of birds intoxicated with singing. A matter of great urgency awaits us.”

This passage begins with “Bring all the royal attendants, Satrughna, the illustrious Bharata, Yudhajit, Sumantra and all those wellwishers of the king.” and develops toward “The assembly hall looked splendid like a lake of tranquil waters with gems, shells pebbles, grains of sand and teeming with whales and serpents in…”

This passage begins with “Then, the wives of the soldiers having come to know of the expedition were all delighted and in every household hastened up their husbands.” and develops toward “Thereafter, men from every household, kshatriyas, vaisyas, sudras and brahmins came out and harnessed their chariots to the camels and asses,…”

This passage begins with “While he (Bharata's) was speaking this way, all the ministers assented by saying 'Be it so' and made arrangement for their troops to rest separately according to their pleasure.” and develops toward “The army encamped by Bharata looked splendid with royal insigaia. I see none equal to you on this earth.”

This passage begins with “You desire to bring back Rama who is in great difficulty. This passage begins with “Thereupon delighted Guha, overlord of the nishadas, told Bharata all that he had provided to Rama, his beloved friend and guest:” and develops toward “Then, holding the best of arrows and bow along with my indefatigable kinsmen similarly armed with bows, I stood by the side of Lakshmana guarding…”

This passage begins with “Having enjoyed the hospitality of Bharadwaja the foot soldiers, horse and elephant riders made utterances ignoring their leaders.” and develops toward “There were thousands of pots, lakhs of plates, crores of vessels -- all made of gold and filled with wellgarnished food. This passage begins with “There were thousands of pots, lakhs of plates, crores of vessels -- all made of gold and filled with wellgarnished food. This passage begins with “O distinguished lord of the army, if you lead your army full of elephants, horses and chariots through the southern path or towards the southwest, you will come across Rama there.” and develops toward “She is my mother Kaikeyi who is irascible, lacks the power of discrimination, proud, boastful of her beauty, ambitious of wealth, ignoble in the…”

This passage begins with “Like the people of the south, the trees on the mountain ridges with branches, brilliant like clouds, are crowned with fragrant flowers as adornments.” and develops toward “Having heard these words acceptable to the pious, Bharata, the crusher of enemy forces, addressed the entire army:”

This passage begins with “All of you carefully watch here. Do not proceed any further. Sumantra, the preceptor and myself shall go forward.” and develops toward “The army thus halted by Bharata, gazing at the space before them rejoiced at the thought that not long before they would rejoin their beloved Rama.…”

This passage begins with “O Lady, behold those lotus garlands crushed and cast aside by lovers and also many fruits (tasted by them and) lying there.” and develops toward “O my beloved Sita, by spending time with you and Lakshmana following the path of the virtuous with utmost restraint and by enhancing the…”

This passage begins with “The waters of river Mandakini is perpetually agitated by siddhas who are endowed with asceticism, selfrestraint and tranquillity and who are cleansed of sins by taking bath in the river.…” and develops toward “Rama, the perpetuator of the Raghu dynasty, describing the river Mandakini at length appropriately in this manner, wandered in the company of his…”

This passage begins with “O Rama, Bharata has abandoned righteousness and has harmed you. This passage begins with “I hope you have employed highly competent servants for important tasks, mediocre servants in mediocre tasks and low people in inferior tasks.” and develops toward “I hope you collect information (of their secret efforts) intelligently through unrecognisable spies with three of them closely watching each of the…”

This passage begins with “O slayer of foes, I hope you are always alert about your foes who were defeated by you and have come back. A king must protect all those people living in his country in accordanc…”

This passage begins with “I trust, you keep the women pacified and wellprotected, you do not believe their words and do not divulge any secrets to them.” and develops toward “O Bharata, I trust you wish to thrive by treating the old, children and foremost of learned people with the three expedients namely gifts, affection…”

This passage begins with “I trust you pay homage to the preceptors, the aged ones, ascetics, gods and guests, to shrines and to accomplished brahmins.” and develops toward “I trust, the Vedas you have studied are fruitful, the acts which you commenced are productive, your wife is fruitful (blessed with a son) and the…”

This passage begins with “O Bharata, I trust your intellect is in conformity with what I said conducive to long life, and fame in accordance with righteousness, (legitimate) pleasure and prosperity.” and develops toward “The highly sagacious and learned king, having acquired the entire earth and punishing the people in accordance with tradition and righteously ruling…”

This passage begins with “O Rama, whoever protects the inhabitants of his kingdom at all times like his own son who he loves more than his own life will attain the world of Brahma and remain there for long years and…” and develops toward “I came here to acomplish your task incidentally, Therefore, my stay in the forest will yield great results.”

This passage begins with “I wish to kill in the fight the demons who are your enemies. O great ascetics, you may witness my prowess supported by my brother.” and develops toward “Brave Rama, steadfast in righteousness, worthy of honour, gave assurance to protect the ascetics endowed with the wealth of penance, and proceeded…”

This passage begins with “Selfpossessed Rama replied to the truthful ascetic who had performed intense penance. Just as Kasyapa would address Lord Brahma:” and develops toward “Seeing that the evening had passed and night had set in, the great sage Sutikshna with due hospitality, served those best of men, food fit for…”

See how delightful is the location of their hermitages.”

This passage begins with “You will see the beautiful forestareas with abundant fruits and roots, flowers in full bloom, herds of animals, flocks of quiet birds tanks and lakes filled with clean waters and shining…” and develops toward “Handsome Rama and Lakshmana, glowing in their radiance held the bows and exited with Sita. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये अरण्यकाण्डे…”

I wish your ultimate good. [The next two lines appear, more or…”

This passage begins with “O brave Rama, I do not like your going to Dandaka forest. I shall tell you the reason. Do listen.” and develops toward “Contact with the weapon has led to this in the past. Like contact with fire, it leads man to destruction. I say this out of my love and reverence for…”

This passage begins with “Contact with the weapon has led to this in the past. Like contact with fire, it leads man to destruction. sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard these words spoken by Sita who was devoted to her husband, replied Rama who was firm in righteousness.” and develops toward “'O sinless one, these cannibals are invincible. They arrive at the time of our sacrificial rituals, on the days of festivities or at the time of…”

This passage begins with “'You are the supreme refuge for us ascetics, tormented by demons, helpless men in search of shelter.” and develops toward “Having said this to Sita, the princess of Mithila, Rama along with Lakshmana, bow in hand, moved about the delightful penancegrove. इत्यार्षे…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama walked in front, Sita with a beautiful waist, in the middle and Lakshmana holding the bow, behind them৷৷” and develops toward “Great sage Mandakarni standing in water and living on air performed rigorous penance for ten thousand years.”

This passage begins with “Thereafter very much perturbed, all the gods led by Agni gathered together at one place and started deliberating among themselves.” and develops toward “Rama, the scion of the Kakutstha race, wielder of great weapons, stayed in that beautiful hermitage, accepting the hospitality of the sages extended…”

This passage begins with “Rama lived happily here for about ten months, there one year, here four or five or six months and there over one or half a month or three or eight months in another.” and develops toward “I also wished you, O Raghava, to visit Agastya along with Sita and Lakshmana.”

This passage begins with “Luckily you have asked me on your own. I will tell you where the great sage Agastya lives.” and develops toward “Surely this is the hermitage of great Agastya's brother, a man of holy deeds.”

This passage begins with “This is the glade we were told about with thousands of trees drooping down with abundant fruits and flowers.” and develops toward “Then hearing his brother's voice, Vatapi would bleat like a sheep and jump out, tearing open the bodies of those brahmins.”

This passage begins with “In this manner, thousands of brahmins used to be killed daily by those carnivorous demons who assumed any form at their free will.” and develops toward “When the night passed and the Sun became bright, Rama took leave of the brother of Agastya.”

This passage begins with “O Lord, the night has been wellspent. Now we humbly seek leave of you to see your respectable elder brother.” and develops toward “The southern side favourable to the people was famous by the name Agastya in the three worlds and stood unassailable to the demons.”

This passage begins with “This is the hermitage of the longlived Agastya who is renowned for his deeds and is served by humble people. The great mountain Vindhya that was growing incessantly and obstructing the path…” and develops toward “Worshipped by benevolent beings, the gods here bestow on them the status of demigods or of the immortals or several kinds of kingdoms.”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Lakshmana, the younger brother of Rama, entered the hermitage of Agastya and met one of his disciples and said to him these words :” and develops toward “'So be it ' said the disciple respectfully with folded palms in response to what the great sage, knower of dharma, commanded.”

This passage begins with “Then the disciple in a state of excitement came out of the hermitage and said to Lakshmana, 'Where is Rama? Let him come himself to see the sage'.” and develops toward “Having said so, the mightyarmed Rama, the scorcher of enemies, grasped the feet of Agastya who was shining like the Sun.”

This passage begins with “Then righteous Rama with Sita, the princess of Videha, and Lakshmana offered respects with folded palmsstanding.” and develops toward “O bestower of honour, receive this bow, the two quivers, the sword and the arrow for your victory just like Indra accepts the thunderbolt.”

This passage begins with “The luminous lord Agastya, having said this, gave all the excellent weapons to Rama. And continued : इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये अरण्यकाण्डे द्वादशस्सर्गः।। Thus ends the…” and develops toward “The luminous lord Agastya, having said this, gave all the excellent weapons to Rama. sections.summary: This passage begins with “O Rama ,I am pleased. I wish you well. I am glad that both of you have come here with Sita to pay respect to me.” and develops toward “On hearing the words of Rama, Agastya, the best among the sages, coolheaded and righteous, thought a while and uttered these words coolly :”

This passage begins with “O dear two yojanas away is a place wellknown as Panchavati, a beautiful resort with many roots, fruits, plenty of water and many animals.” and develops toward “Then climb the elevated ground not very far from the mountain. There is the wellknown Panchavati with forests, ever in full bloom.”

This passage begins with “Thus addressed by Agastya, Rama with Lakshmana paid respects to the sage wedded to truth and took leave of him.” and develops toward “Both the princes, unafraid of battles, held their bows, tied the quivers and carefully proceeded towards Panchavati along the path suggested by the…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On the way to Panchavati, Rama, the delight of the Raghu race, met a vulture with a huge body and fearful strength.” and develops toward “Kasyapa, pleased with them said to his young wives, Beget sons of my stature who can rule over the three worlds.”

This passage begins with “O best of men, O longarmed Rama while Aditi, Diti and Kalika seriously considered his advice, the rest of the wives did not.” and develops toward “To Hari were born lions, and swiftmoving monkeys and to Bhadramada, a daughter by name Iravati.”

This passage begins with “O bull among men Matangi gave birth to elephants.The mighty elephant Airavata who became the lord of the world was born to Iravati.” and develops toward “Entresting the protection of Sita, the princess of Mithila, to that strong vulture, Jatayu, and accompanied by Lakshmana Rama went to Panchavati…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On reaching Panchavati, which was full of various vicious animals, Rama said to Lakshmana, his brother who was glowing like fire:” and develops toward “As said by the selfrealised soul, sage Agastya, this Godavari is beautiful, surrounded by trees with flowers in bloom, filled with flocks of swans,…”

This passage begins with “O handsome one, neither far nor near are seen tall mountains and flowering trees and herds of deer. Their numerous caves echo with sounds of peacocks.” and develops toward “Lakshmana then went to river Godavari, had his bath, and returned with some lotuses.”

This passage begins with “Lakshmana offered oblation of flowers, invoked peace as per tradition (before occupying a newly made home) and showed Rama the cottage he had built.” and develops toward “Righteous Rama, served by Lakshmana, and Sita lived there for some time like a god in heaven. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये…”
