sections.summary: This passage begins with “While the great self Rama lived happily there, autumn passed and dear winter set in.” and develops toward “At this time people do not sleep under the open sky. With the month of Pushya approaching, nights feel cool and look red due to frost. They prolong…”

This passage begins with “With the radiance of the Sun dimmed, with its orb reddened by frost, the moon does not shine clear like a mirror blurred with breath.” and develops toward “Now the rivers are not visible as the waters are covered with vapour. They are only inferred through the cackling of swans and the sands of the bank…”

This passage begins with “Due to snowfall, mildness of the Sun, and coldness (of weather) even the water flowing from the mountain top is not generally tasty.” and develops toward “When pious Lakshmana said thus to Rama out of his love for him, Rama replied, unable to tolerate the words of censure against mother (Kaikeyi) :”

This passage begins with “O dear, Kaikeyi, our second mother, is never to be criticised like that. You may, however, speak more of Bharata, the lord of the Iksvakus.” and develops toward “Accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana,Rama finished his bath in the river.He looked splendid like Lord Rudra followed by Nandi and the daughter of the…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ablution over, Rama, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana, went back from the bank of the river Godabari to their hermitage.” and develops toward “Rama's face was lovely, Surpanakha's was hideous. Rama had a slender waist, she had a huge belly. His eyes were large, hers were deformed. His hair…”

This passage begins with “O Rama you are wearing matted hair like an ascetic, but living with your wife and holding bow and arrows. What brings you to this place haunted by demons ? You should tell me the truth ?” and develops toward “Kumbhakarna is another brother of mine. He is very strong. He is always in deep sleep. Vibhisna another brother. He is righteous free from any…”

This passage begins with “Setting them aside, I came here, as soon as I saw you. You are the finest among men and I am here wishing you to be my husband.” and develops toward “Thus addressed, Rama, skilled in speech, laughed and started speaking to that woman of bewitching eyes. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये…”

This passage begins with “While Lakshmana was thus making fun of the dreadful, droopingbellied Surpanakha, she presumed his words to be true.” and develops toward “Dripping blood profusely, the frightful demoness lifted her arms and entered the great forestroaring.”

This passage begins with “A disfigured demoness, she fell down like the thunderbolt from the sky on the ground while her fiercelooking brother Khara sat surrounded by demons at Janasthana.” and develops toward “Thereafter the sister of Khara, wild with fear and delusion and drenched in blood, narrated all about the arrival of Rama, Sita and Lakshmana in the…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Burning with anger to see his sister fallen down disfigured and drenched in blood, Khara enquired:” and develops toward “You should tell me as you slowly regain your consciousness which bragart has overpowered you in the forest with his strength.”

This passage begins with “On hearing her angry brother, Surpanakha replied with words choked with tears:” and develops toward “O demons quickly go and crush them with your power and fulfil my sister's sincere wish.”

This passage begins with “Thus commanded, the fourteen demons along with Surpanakha rushed there like windblown clouds.” and develops toward “The nightrangers were unable to crush him(Rama) despite their sharp, glowing weapons just as wild elephants cannot face all at once the rising flame…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The frightful Surpanakha came to the hermitage of Rama and showed both the brothers and Sita to the demons.” and develops toward “You have incited the wrath of Khara, our great master for which you will now pay with your life in the fight.”

This passage begins with “How can you face us alone? We outnumber you? Leave alone fighting, you cannot even stand in front of us.” and develops toward “Then desperately screaming and shedding tears with a sad face beside her brother, she roared again and again.”

This passage begins with “When Surpanakha saw the demons fall dead on the battleground, she ran to Khara,her brother and told in detail about the death of the demons. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये…” and develops toward “When Surpanakha saw the demons fall dead on the battleground, she ran to Khara,her brother and told in detail about the death of the demons.…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Seeing Surpanakha collapse, Khara said in clear terms about the unfortunate situation she had created:” and develops toward “I am drowned in a vast sea of sorrow where crocodiles in the form of distress reside. It is full of rows of waves of fright. Why do you not save me?”

This passage begins with “The demons who live on flesh, who came following me fell down on earth and lay dead, shot by the sharp arrows of Rama.” and develops toward “Overcome by sorrow, the bigbellied demoness cried in several ways and being very sad started beating her chest with both hands. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ashamed and accused thus in the presence of the demons, the valiant Khara spoke to Surpanakha in harsh words:” and develops toward “O expert in warfare, I wish to lead those great warriors of Paulasthya family, to kill that immodest Rama.”

This passage begins with “While Khara was speaking thus, Dusana reported that a huge chariot of the colour of the Sun yoked with good horses of variegated hues stood ready.” and develops toward “Knowing the mind of Khara, the charioteer hastened the horses of the colourful chariot decorated with pure gold.”

This passage begins with “Spurred by the charioteer, the chariot of Khara, the slayer of enemies, produced a rattle that filled all four quarters and the intermediate directions.” and develops toward “Khara in extreme anger hastened, like Yama, to slay his enemy. He roared loudly like a cloud raining hail stones and impelled his charioteer to speed…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “As the army marched from there (Janasthana) a huge dark cloud of the colour of an ass rained dreadful inauspicious water, red as blood.” and develops toward “The planet Ketu appeared like an iron beam near the Sun. Although it was not the fullmoon or newmoon day, Rahu eclipsed the Sun. Wind blew hard and…”

This passage begins with “The stars rose in the sky and glittered like fireflies even before night arrived. The fishes and the aquatic birds in the lotusponds hid themselves under water. Lotuses dried up in tanks…” and develops toward “When angry, I can kill even (Indra) the wielder of the thunderbolt, rider of the intoxicated Airavata and lord of the gods. What to speak of these…”

This passage begins with “Fallen into the deathtrap, the great army of the demon, felt very happy on hearing Khara roaring.” and develops toward “The mighty, swiftfooted army of valiant demons surrounded the princes (Rama and Lakshmana) all on a sudden like the planets around the Sun and the…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When Khara endowed with malicious might was marching towards the hermitage, Rama and his brother saw the same omens of impending calamity the demon had seen.” and develops toward “Therefore, take shelter along with Sita in the inaccessible mountain cave, overgrown with trees, ready with your bow and arrows.”

This passage begins with “O dear, I do not like you to oppose this word of honour. You must touch my feet and go without delay.” and develops toward “Rajarsis, siddhas, great brahmins with their clan and gods on the celestial chariots waited to see the war whetted with curiosity.”

This passage begins with “Seeing Rama standing on the war front, engrossed and radiating heroic lustre, all beings were alarmed.” and develops toward “Seeing Rama flashing (in anger) and standing on the warfront all creatures ran away frightened.”

This passage begins with “The army of demons equipped with blazing armour, bows and banners appeared like a mass of blue clouds at Sunrise. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये अरण्यकाण्डे चतुर्विंशस्सर्गः।।…” and develops toward “The army of demons equipped with blazing armour, bows and banners appeared like a mass of blue clouds at Sunrise. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Khara accompanied by his pilot party arrived at the hermitage of Rama, the destroyer of enemies and saw him in fury, standing ready with his bow.” and develops toward “Rama resisted the weapons released by the demons like the sea, the inflow of river waters.”

This passage begins with “Rama remained unhurt like a great mountain struck by many glowing thunderbolts. He felt no pain even though his limbs were hit by the dreadful weapons.” and develops toward “The bodies of the demons torn into pieces by the very sharp iron arrows and by the arrows called vikarni, they cried and shouted aloud in pitiable…”

This passage begins with “Just like the dried up forest burnt by the fire, the army was totally destroyed by Rama's arrows that pierced into the vital parts of the soldiers.” and develops toward “As the infuriated demons again hurtled towards Rama, filling the quarters and the intermediate directions and raining missiles, Rama aimed at them…”

This passage begins with “Rama then let out from his bow, stretched round, thousands of arrows that covered all the ten quarters.” and develops toward “Slain thus by Rama's arrows, the ground was littered with (bodies of) demons with limbs dismembered, headdresses and shoulders cut off, arms, thighs…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Mightyarmed Dusana saw the army getting killed in war. He led the forces of five thousand demons, dreadfuly quick at fighting, dangeous to face and who will not beat a retreat.” and develops toward “With the spear that looked like a mighty serpent, Dusana, the cruel demon, advanced towards Rama.”

This passage begins with “Seeing Dusana going to pounce upon him, Rama cut off with two arrows both his arms adorned with armlets.” and develops toward “Dusana and his followers are killed in the battle. You, all the demons, along with your great army and different kinds of weapons should kill that…”

This passage begins with “Dusana and his followers are killed in the battle. You, all the demons, along with your great army and different kinds of weapons should kill that wicked human being, Rama.” and develops toward “Of that entire army great warrior Khara,Trisira and Rama, destroyer of foes alone remained.”

This passage begins with “The rest of the mighty, dreadful, intolerable demons were killed on the war front by Rama, elder brother to Lakshmana.” and develops toward “Thereafter seeing the formidable army killed by mighty Rama, Khara lifted a thunderbolt like Indra, and advanced towards Rama in a great chariot.…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “As Khara advanced towards Rama, Trisira the chief of the army approached him and said these words:” and develops toward “Oh see the strength of this undaunted demon whose arrows fell like flowers on my forehead and only left a scratch.”

This passage begins with “Receive the arrows released from my bowstring. Saying so, the angry and agitated Rama discharged fourteen arrows, venomous like a snake, on the chest of Trisira .” and develops toward “Personally persuading the fleeing army to come back, the angry Khara leaped forward towards Rama just as Rahu approaches the moon. इत्यार्षे…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Khara was overtaken by fear at the prowess of Rama, when he saw Dusana and Trisira killed.” and develops toward “Khara thought valiant Rama who had destroyed his whole army might be exhausted by now.”

This passage begins with “When Rama saw Khara aggressively advancing like a lion to attack him, he was not perturbed, for he knew he was a fawn before a lion (which Rama was).” and develops toward “Quite enraged, Khara who knew how to hit the tender parts (of the body) in an encounter sent out four arrows on Rama. It was like poking an…”

This passage begins with “Rama, hit by the arrows released from the bow of Khara, all his limbs drenched in blood, became very furious.” and develops toward “Then the gods, along with great sages came down in aerial chariots and assembled there. Delighted, they adored Rama, the great warrior, with folded…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Effulgent Rama looked at Khara who stood with a mace in hand minus his chariot and said to him first in a gentle voice and then harshly :” and develops toward “Slain by me in the battle today, you and your army will follow the righteous men eaten by you in Dandaka forest.”

This passage begins with “The great seers whom you have earlier tortured and killed will, from their aerial vehicles, see you fallen into hell.” and develops toward “You have killed these fourteen thousand demons. Therefore, I will wipe their tears by destroying you.”

This passage begins with “Having said this, highly enraged Khara adorned with large shoulder bands, hurled at Rama, his mace glowing like a thunderbolt.” and develops toward “The mace shattered by the arrows of Rama , fell down scattered to pieces on the ground like a female serpent subdued by the force of mantras and…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama, fond of dharma, broke into pieces Khara's mace with his arrows, and smiling at bewildered Khara, said:” and develops toward “O cruel, mean, vile fellow, you had been always opposing the brahmins who pour oblations in fire under fear.”

This passage begins with “When Rama was thus speaking in a state of excitement in the middle of war, the choleric Khara started abusing him in a harsher tone.” and develops toward “Rama took a dart glowing like fire which resembled another Brahmadanda to kill Khara in the war.”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While Sita was being kidnapped, she saw no one to protect her. Only five great monkeys stood on the top of a mountain.” and develops toward “Ravana went through the well laidout roads of the city of Lanka and through the well many guarded halls of the palace and entered the inner apartment.”

This passage begins with “Sita, with her dark eye lashes and lost in utter sorrow was kept in the harem by Ravana, like demon Maya kept Mayamayi.” and develops toward “I want to take revenge on my foe. I shall not sleep until I kill him in a combat.”

This passage begins with “I will be happy only after killing the destroyer of Khara and Dusana, just as a poor man feels happy only on acquiring wealth.” and develops toward “Then the demon Ravana felt greatly happy for getting Sita, princess of Mithila, without knowing in his delusion that he had created the bitterest…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “After ordering the eight most fierce and powerful demons, Ravana had a sense of accomplishment within himself because of his perverse intellect.” and develops toward “Ravana showed Sita who was overwhelmed with sorrow, stepwells surrrounded by various trees and tanks.”

This passage begins with “The sinful Ravana showed Sita, princess of Videha, the best of all his mansions in order to allure her, saying:” and develops toward “It is not possible to bind the wind blowing at high speed in the sky. Similarly it is not possible to catch the brilliant glow of flaming fire.”

This passage begins with “O lovely lady, I do not see any one in these three worlds who can, by his valour, take you away from me guarded by my arms.” and develops toward “My throat has dried up. I have spoken these words in a forlorn state. Ravana has never bowed down to a woman in obeisance.”

This passage begins with “Having said this to the daughter of Janaka, the tenheaded Ravana who had fallen under the control of Yama, lord of death, misconceived that she had become his own. इतयार्ष श्रीमद्रामायणे…” and develops toward “Having said this to the daughter of Janaka, the tenheaded Ravana who had fallen under the control of Yama, lord of death, misconceived that she had…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Thus addressed by Ravana, Sita, immersed in deep sorrow and unafraid of him, placed a blade of grass between her and him (intending not to speak to him straight) and replied:” and develops toward “Your life is coming to an end. Your fortunes will wane, your strength will be sapped. Your senses will be dissipated. By your deeds Lanka will attain…”

This passage begins with “This evil deed(of abduction) will not yield happy results.You have brought me from my husband from the forest without any forethought.” and develops toward “O comely princess from Mithila, O lady with a sweet smile listen to my words. If you do not accept my hand in the course of twelve months, the cooks…”

This passage begins with “O comely princess from Mithila, O lady with a sweet smile listen to my words. If you do not accept my hand in the course of twelve months, the cooks will cut you off into small bits for the…” and develops toward “Sita had no peace as she was threatened excessively by those women of deformed looks. Remembering her beloved husband who was also her god, and…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama killed Maricha, the demon assuming the form of the deer as per his will and swiftly took the return path.” and develops toward “Dejected at heart, the birds and animals piteously kept the great soul (Rama) to the left and produced a dreadful cacophony.”

This passage begins with “On seeing such dreadfu omens at that time, Rama hurried back to his hermitage.” and develops toward “With my heart dejected and depressed, my left eye throbbing, O Lakshmana, I have no doubt that Sita is either abducted or dead or abandoned on the…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama, son of Dasaratha, the righteous self, saw Lakshmana downcast and dejected. Seeing him without Sita, Rama enquired:” and develops toward “Vaidehi is delicate, young and she has never faced any sorrow.She will be certainly disheartened due to separation from me.”

This passage begins with “The evilminded, wicked demon calling out loudly 'O Lakshmana' must have by all means instilled fear in your mind.” and develops toward “Thus blaming his dejected brother, the valiant Rama entered the hermitage, sighing deeply, his face pale and his throat dried up with hunger,…”

chapters.name: Sarga 59
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama, the delight of the Raghus, once again questioned Lakshmana who had come out of the hermitage, deeply hurt:” and develops toward “O fair lady, for some reason some demon has uttered these words 'Save me, save me' adapting my brother's voice.”

This passage begins with “'Lakshmana, save me' are the words uttered in an altered tone. Like an ordinary, lowborn woman you are not to feel concerned for this.” and develops toward “Provoked by the words of Sita spoken in anger you came without respecting my orders. It is definitely an act of disobedience.”

This passage begins with “This demon who misled me in the guise of a deer and took me away from the hermitage, lies dead, killed by my arrow.” and develops toward “Hit by the arrow, the demon imitating my voice cried out in desperation which could be clearly audible even from a distance. On hearing the dreadful…”

chapters.name: Sarga 60
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “As Rama was returning( to the hermitage), his left eye throbbed repeatedly and he stumbled and his body trembled.” and develops toward “O Kadamba tree, tell me if you have seen my beloved with a fair face and with love for kadamba flowers.”

This passage begins with “O Bilva tree, tell me if you have seen a lady delicate like your tender leaf, dressed in yellow silk, a lady whose breasts are round like Bilva fruits.” and develops toward “O elephant tell me if you have seen her whose thighs are like the trunk of an elephant. O best of elephants, tell me if you know her.”

This passage begins with “O tiger tell me freely and fearlessly if you have seen my beloved princess from Mithila with a face like the moon.” and develops toward “Rama ran from forest to forest, now jumping, now taking strong strides, but all the time weeping, a madman busy in search of his beloved.”

This passage begins with “He rushed through forests, rivers, hills and mountain streams and through dense jungles with an unstable mind.” and develops toward “Rama went about the vast forest searching for the princess from Mithila all over not giving up hope of finding her. He continued to make great…”

chapters.name: Sarga 61
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama, son of Dasaratha, saw the empty cottage devoid of Sita. The mats were displaced. He searched everywhere but unable to see Vaidehi lifted his lovely arms and said:” and develops toward “Lakshmana, dedicated to his wellbeing, spoke to Rama, son of Dasaratha, who was sinking like an elephant into the quagmire, when he failed to trace…”

This passage begins with “Lakshmana, dedicated to his wellbeing, spoke to Rama, son of Dasaratha, who was sinking like an elephant into the quagmire, when he failed to trace Sita.” and develops toward “O longarmed brother O wise sire just as Visnu obtained the earth by binding Bali you will obtain Sita certainly.”

This passage begins with “Having been addressed thus by Lakshmana, Raghava with a griefstriken heart said these piteous words :' Lakshmana,having spoken like that very affectionately to Rama,who was disturbed by…” and develops toward “Without caring for the words from the lips of Lakshmana, Rama sobbed loudly, unable to see his beloved Sita. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय…”

chapters.name: Sarga 62
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The righteous, the longarmed,lotuseyed Rama, unable to see Sita wept with his senses overwhilmed by grief.” and develops toward “By Sita's abduction it will be clear to the world that I lack manliness and compassion. My cowardly nature will clearly manifest.”

This passage begins with “After completing the period of my exile when Janaka , king of Mithila, enquires of her welfare how can I face him?” and develops toward “When Rama, devoid of Sita, the lady with beautiful hair, wailed piteously after reaching the forest, Lakshmana too, his face pale out of fear and…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Prince Rama, separated from his beloved, lost his senses out of sorrow. Seeing his brother in a distressed state, he sank again into intense grief.” and develops toward “On this rock, O Lakshmana, my goodnatured lady with her lovely smile sat with me in the past and chatted with you.”

This passage begins with “Would she have gone to Godavari, the foremost of the rivers so dear at all times to my beloved ? But I think she has not gone there alone at any time.” and develops toward “Rama who carries on the (glorious) progeny of the Raghu dynasty, did not (however) consider seriously the words of Lakshmana, a man of great prowess.…”

chapters.name: Sarga 64
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama in dejection said these pitiable words to Lakshmana,O Lakashmana, go swiftly to the river Godavari and find out if Sita has gone there to get lotuses.” and develops toward “Where could have gone that Vaidehi, who removed all my sorrows and sufferings when I lost my kingdom and survived on food available in the forest?”

This passage begins with “Away from relatives and away from the princess, I keep awake during nights which seem too long.” and develops toward “Talking with each other, the two brothers took the path on earth strewn with flowers.”

This passage begins with “Seeing showers of flowers fallen on the ground, valiant Rama sadly said these words to sorrowful Lakshmana:” and develops toward “While Rama was angry with his eyes burning, he saw on the ground as he advanced large footprints of a demon and then the footprints of Maithili which…”

This passage begins with “Bewildered to see the broken bow and quiver and many splinters of the chariot scattered all over, Rama said to his loving brother Lakshmana:” and develops toward “Whose are these pointless arrows of the size of a chariot axle adorned with bright gold destroyed and scattered ? Who is he that has performed such…”

This passage begins with “O Lakshmana look at these two quivers filled with arrows and lying shattered. The charioteer is holding a whip and reins in his hands and is lying dead. To whom do they belong?” and develops toward “I will obstruct the movement of planets and the Moon, destroy the radiance of the Sun, the fire and block the wind crush the mountain tops, dry up…”

This passage begins with “I will obstruct the movement of planets and the Moon, destroy the radiance of the Sun, the fire and block the wind crush the mountain tops, dry up the tanks, destroy the trees shrubs and…” and develops toward “After speaking these words, handsome Rama, his eyes red with anger, pressed his bow, took out the glowing arrow that was dreadful like a poisonous…”

This passage begins with “After speaking these words, handsome Rama, his eyes red with anger, pressed his bow, took out the glowing arrow that was dreadful like a poisonous serpent, fixed it to his bow and said…” and develops toward “I will topsyturvy this entire world including the gods, the gandharvas, human beings, serpents and mountains if they do not show me the princess from…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Tormented and emaciated due to Sita's abduction, resembling the fire at the time of dissolution of the creation, ready to destroy the world, Rama looked at his bow strung again and again,…” and develops toward “O king bow in hands, followed by me and with the help of great sages you should search for the one who has abducted Sita.”

This passage begins with “We will search oceans, mountains, forests and several dreadful caves, rivers and lotusponds.” and develops toward “O king if you fail to get Sita through appeasement, through humility or diplomacy then you destroy (the three worlds) by your goldtipped stream of…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Lakshmana, son of Sumitra, consoling the mighty miserable Rama, who was afflicted with sorrow, and was weeping helplessly like an orphan, drowned in great delusion, devoid of consciousness,…” and develops toward “Even if Vaidehi is killed or abducted, you should not grieve like ordinary men, O sinless hero”

This passage begins with “O Rama, you always see the truth. So in the face of great difficulties men like you, whose vision is not blurred, should not lose heart.” and develops toward “O best among men where is the good in the destruction of all? Know your sinful enemy before you uproot him. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकेय…”

chapters.name: Sarga 67
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Even though Rama was elder, he could grasp the quintessence of the wise counsel only after Lakshmana had said it.” and develops toward “This demon in the guise of a vulture has evidently eaten away the largeeyed Sita and is happily relaxing in the forest. With my terrific arrows with…”

This passage begins with “Having siad so, Rama took his bow, fixed the sharp arrow, and aimed at the vulture. The seabound earth trembled.” and develops toward “I have been expelled from the kingdom. I am made to stay in the forest. I have lost Sita (my beloved wife). This bird (who was my wellwisher) has…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Seeing the vulture lying on the ground thrown down by the fierce demon, Rama said to friendly Lakshmana:” and develops toward “The time Ravana kidnapped Sita is known as 'Vinda'. The effect (of that time), is that her husband will regain his lost wealth. O scion of the…”

This passage begins with “Ravana, king of the demons, will perish soon for running away with your beloved Janaki just like a fish carrying away a fishhook (which causes its destruction).” and develops toward “O Lakshmana valiant souls and protectors, being righteous and honest are found even among animals and birds.”

This passage begins with “O scorcher of enemies, O handsome Lakshmana the grief I experience due to Jatayu's death on my account is more intense than Sita's abduction.” and develops toward “Thereafter both the scions of the Raghu dynasty bathed in water, offered libations for the king of vultures as ordained in the scriptures.”

This passage begins with “The king of vultures had performed a very difficult and glorious feat by laying down his life fighting. sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama and Lakshmana offered libations (for Jatayu) and went westward, looking for Sita in the forest.” and develops toward “Then the two brothers saw a fearful demoness who could strike terror in the mind of the timid, an uncouth figure with huge curved skins, with a big…”

This passage begins with “Then the two brothers saw a fearful demoness who could strike terror in the mind of the timid, an uncouth figure with huge curved skins, with a big stomach, dishevelled hair, sharp and long…” and develops toward “As both the brothers with great prowess were searching for Sita all over, a huge sound exploded as if shattering the forest.”

This passage begins with “It appeared as though a fierce wind rose from the forest and filled the sky.” and develops toward “The two mighty brothers who wielded swords and strong bows and whose bodies were of fiery splendour became helpless, seized by the hands of Kabandha.”

This passage begins with “Of the two heroes, Rama being courageous was not worried but Lakshmana being younger and not having as much courage, was very much concerned and depressed. He said to Rama:” and develops toward “O tiger among men see how powerful the might of time is for all beings Just see yourself and myself afflicted wth this great calamity.”

This passage begins with “O Lakshmana look at the power of time over all creatures There is nothing too heavy for time. chapters.name: Sarga 70
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Kabandha saw the brothers, Rama and Lakshmana, stand there bound by the shackles of his arms and said:” and develops toward “Imbued with auspicious signs, Lakshmana revealed the identity of Rama to great Kabandha.”

This passage begins with “Here is Rama, an heir of the Ikshvaku family, known to the people and I am his younger brother Lakshmana for your information.” and develops toward “O best among men hear how I have been deformed due to my haughtiness. इत्यार्ष श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये अरण्यकाण्डे सप्ततितमस्सर्गः।। Thus…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “O longarmed, mighty and powerful Rama in the past I was handsome like the Moon or Indra or the Sun inconceivable to all the three worlds.” and develops toward “Hit by the thunderbolt, my thighbones, head and face were broken. Somehow I could manage to live for a long time without food.”

This passage begins with “Having been entreated, Indra created in me two long arms extending upto a yojana and set my mouth with sharp teeth in the stomach.” and develops toward “O hero we will cremate you in a huge pit to be prepared for this occasion, collecting dry logs of wood broken by elephants.”

This passage begins with “If you really know where SIta has been kidnapped and by whom, tell me. You will be doing a great service.” and develops toward “O Rama, there is nothing that he does not know in the three worlds. In the past for a different reason he roamed all the (three) worlds. इत्यार्ष…”

chapters.name: Sarga 72
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Thus said by Kabandha, the two lords of men, the heroes reached a cave in the mountain and lighted the fire.” and develops toward “A hero of great selfrespect, he lives with four monkeys on a beautiful mountain Rishyamuka extending up to lake Pampa.”

This passage begins with “This king of monkeys is energetic with great strength and limitless lustre. He is humble and truthful, valiant, patient and intelligent. He is great.” and develops toward “Along with his monkeys, he will search all rivers, mountains and caves and will find out your consort.”

This passage begins with “Sugriva will find out the lady of purity and chastity brooding over you in separation in the abode of Ravana. He can send gigantic monkeys in different directions in order to search for her.” and develops toward “Sugriva, king of the monkeys, will reach the demons and restore you that blameless, beloved consort of yours, even if she is there on top of mount…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having shown Rama the means to find Sita, Kabandha spoke again these significant words:” and develops toward “Moving in the waters of Pampa you will see swans, ducks, krauncha birds and curlews cackling and cooing, O Son of the Raghus.”

This passage begins with “Not knowing that they may be killed, the innocent creatures will not be scared of human beings. Both of you can kill and eat the fat birds comparable to balls of ghee.” and develops toward “While drops of sweat from the bodies of sage Matanga's disciples fell on earth under the heavy load of forest products they carried for their…”

This passage begins with “O Kakutstha even now their attendant, named Sabari, an old ascetic is there even though they have left long ago.” and develops toward “The great, nimble elphants, dark like clouds, drenched with the flow of ichor come together and then separate and scatter.”

This passage begins with “The wandering elephants of the forest drink plentiful water, pure and cool, from Pampa and contented, plunge into the forest.” and develops toward “Kabandha assumed his true form, his body shining like the radiance of the Sun. He stood in the sky showing his form and looking at Rama, said, Make…”

chapters.name: Sarga 74
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The two princes set forth in the western direction towards Pampa shown by Kabandha.” and develops toward “O best of men, O highest of the gods my birth has become fruitful today with your worship, (you have made) even attainment of heaven quite certain.”

This passage begins with “O delight of the Raghus, your handsome look has sanctified me, O subduer of enemies, by your grace I will reach the inmperishable worlds.” and develops toward “O Rama see, even now they illuminate the sacrificial altars with the power of their penance. They shed light in all directions with a matchless…”

This passage begins with “Look at the seven seas, which have reached here at their mere thought as they could not reach them due to fatigue from fasting.” and develops toward “By virtue of her selfmeditation, Sabari went to that sacred world where the holy sages lived because of their good deeds. इत्यार्ष श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 75
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When Sabari departed for heaven with her brilliance, Rama contemplated (about the power of sages)with Lakshmana.” and develops toward “He saw the sacred lake Pampa whose sweet flow of water was used by great seers. It abounded in trees and creepers. Its surface was red with lotuses…”

This passage begins with “Rama reached the Matanga lake which he could see from a distance and bathed in it.” and develops toward “There were lovely pleasuregardens in full bloom filled with sal and champk trees. Honeybees on flowers looked lovely and bright. The waters were…”

This passage begins with “The famous mount, sacred Rishyamuka is situated on the bank of Pampa full of blossoming trees.” and develops toward “Thereafter, going slowly to a distance, Rama and Lakshmana saw Pampa surrounded by beautiful forests full of many varieties of birds. इत्यार्ष…”

chapters.name: Sarga 1
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Arriving with Laksmana at the lake filled with lotuses, lilies and fishes, Rama, his senses oppressed, burst into tears.” and develops toward “Various forest trees shaken by the force of the wind scatter flowers on the beautiful rocky surface of earth.”

This passage begins with “O Saumitri! look, the breeze appears to be playing with the flowers; that are fallen and that are still on the trees.” and develops toward “'Lakshmana, while I am already lovestricken, the swamphen (gallinule, a marshbird) troubles me with its joyous cackles in the beautiful foreststream.”

This passage begins with “'While in the hermitage in the past, my beloved was very glad to hear such sounds, invite me to join her and rejoice again.” and develops toward “'Delightful are the peacocks dancing here and there, their feathers looking like crystal windows flung open by the wind.”

This passage begins with “'The peacocks intoxicated with passion for the peahens surround them intensifying my longing (for Vaidehi).” and develops toward “'Or, if there is spring where my beloved lives, what can that lady of beautiful hips do, threatened by enemies.”

This passage begins with “'Eager to speak with her gentle voice, my young beloved, blessed with eyes like lotus petals, will certainly give up her life, unable to enjoy the spring.” and develops toward “'O Saumitri O' tiger among men look at the kinnaras wandering here and there, in the wonderful forest around the Pampa lake.”

This passage begins with “'O Lakshmana look at these lotuses with fine fragrance all over the water glowing like the rising Sun.” and develops toward “'Look at the very spectacular karnikara tree with its blossmed branches on the mountain slope towards the southern side of Pampa, O Saumitri”

This passage begins with “'This king of mountains is rich with plenty of minerals and, therefore, generates multicoloured dust particles splattered by the speed of the wind.” and develops toward “'While the branches of trees swayed by the wind draw close, the creepers follow the trees like lovely women in a drunken state.”

This passage begins with “'The wind blows from tree to tree, mountain to mountain, and forest to forest as though content to enjoy all the fragrance.” and develops toward “'If I can sport with Sita in this beautiful green grassy land, I will not have any worry, I would not desire for any other pleasure.”

This passage begins with “'These trees with their beautiful leaves and varieties of flowers bring me agony as I live in this forest separated from my beloved.” and develops toward “'She followed me, O Lakshmana as I was deposed from the kingdom and distraught. Now helpless, how can I live without her?”

This passage begins with “'Unable to see her beautiful, face with auspicious eyes like fragrant lotuses, I feel (so) depressed.” and develops toward “'O noble prince be blessed. maintain composure. Without making any effort it is not possible to achieve the objective and recoup loss.”

This passage begins with “'O revered Sire, enterprise is supreme strength. Nothing is difficult in this world for one who is up and doing.” and develops toward “Having seen the mighty Rama and Lakshmana, all other monkeys got frightened and ran into a sacred and safe hermitage. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

'O longarmed chief of the monkey clan O warrior pardon me any wrong done unknowingly, inconsiderately. I touch your feet with my head.'

This passage begins with “'O distinguished monkey Janaka's daughter will greet you without any hesitation when you present this signet to her. She will know that you have come from me.” and develops toward “O Hanuman son of the Windgod, O valiant among the Vanaras, I bank on your strength. Do whatever you can with your mighty valour so that the daughter…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Summoning the entire body of vanaras, Sugriva, the bull among monkeys, said, 'Go to the places I have described and look for Sita in order to fulfil Rama's objective'.” and develops toward “'I shall kill Ravana singlehanded in war and quickly get back Janaka's daughter churning (the enemy army).' (said one)”

This passage begins with “'I will alone restore Sita, exhausted and trembling (in fear), even from the nether world. Wait here' (said another leader).” and develops toward “'I can doubtless cross a distance of a hundred yojanas' said one. Another said,' I can leap a hundred yojanas more'.”

This passage begins with “Then I saw the earth as though it were the surface of a mirror, like a whirling firebrand, appearing as small as a cow's hoof-print.” and develops toward “'O king that way I surveyed the entire earth directly and returned to this cave and lived here. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये…”

This passage begins with “'We searched again and again the interior, inaccessible and uneven locations,and killed huge animals. We searched in regions difficult to penetrate.” and develops toward “'O king of monkeys we hope, his exhalted highness, the mighty vanara, son of the Windgod, Hanuman has gone in the direction in which Maithili has…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Hanuman, the monkey accompanied by Tara and Angada proceeded as instructed by Sugriva in the (South) direction.” and develops toward “There was a sage called Kandu who was truthful, outrageous and shorttempered. He observed strict austerities and was rich in penance.”

This passage begins with “The great sage was enraged because he had lost his sixteenyearold son in the forest.” and develops toward “Again they searched the forest and entered another dreadful mountain cave.”

This passage begins with “'Once again let us explore the mountains, caverns, forests, desolate places and mountain streams.” and develops toward “The bulls among vanaras and Hanuman, the chief of the group again started exploring everywhere in the Vindhya range starting from the beginning.…”

It looked like the abode of the king of demons difficult (to enter), dreadful and…”

This passage begins with “Then Hanuman, son of the Windgod who appeared like a huge mountain and knew about forests spoke to the fellowmonkeys:” and develops toward “Having reached inside the cave the monkeys saw a forest shining like blazing fire, dispelling the surrounding darkness. There they noticed sala,…”

This passage begins with “Having reached inside the cave the monkeys saw a forest shining like blazing fire, dispelling the surrounding darkness. There they noticed sala, tala, tamala, punnaga, vanjjula, dhava,…” and develops toward “(They found) excellent drinks and many kinds of honey, wonderful collections of clothes, colourful blankets and heaps of animal skin.”

This passage begins with “The monkeys marked here and there a collection of gold, glittering like blazing fire.” and develops toward “Then Hanuman who was of the size of a mountain greeted the old lady with folded hands and enquired her 'Who are you? To whom does this mansion, this…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On meeting the distinguished, righteous ascetic woman robed in deer skin, Hanuman said:” and develops toward “'He (Maya) performed rigorous penance in the dense forest for thousands of years seeking the favour of Brahma, the creator and obtained a boon that…”

This passage begins with “Then the mighty Maya who received the power of (executing) desires created this mansion and lived here happily for some time in this dense forest.” and develops toward “'Eat these wonderful roots and fruits and have these drinks and then tell me all about you. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “After the monkey leaders had taken rest, the noble ascetic with undiverted attention said these words:” and develops toward “'Drenched in water the swans, kuraras, and sarasas were seen emerging with water dripping from thier wings.”

This passage begins with “'I suggested to the fellowmonkeys to enter this cave, inferring that there would be water and all of them came to the same conclusion.” and develops toward “'I am very much pleased with all of you, smart monkeys. As I am engaged in the practice of righteousness, I do not need your services. इत्यार्षे…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “To the auspicious words of the righteous, blameless hermitess Hanuman replied:” and develops toward “'This Vindhya mountain has a rich collection of trees and bushes. This is Prasravana hill and this is the great ocean.O monkeys. Good luck to you.…”

This passage begins with “'This Vindhya mountain has a rich collection of trees and bushes. This is Prasravana hill and this is the great ocean.O monkeys. Good luck to you. (Now) I am going back home'. Having said…” and develops toward “'You are all celebrated for bravery and incomparable in your activities. You have followed me in obedience to the orders of the tawnyeyed Sugriva.”

This passage begins with “'We should be killed since we have failed to achieve the objective (of finding Sita). There is no doubt about it. Who can live in peace without executing the orders of the king?” and develops toward “'Those who have failed in their mission, being offenders should not go to meet the king. Offenders are not forgiven. Here itself we will search for…”

This passage begins with “'On hearing the words of the frightened monkeys, Tara said, 'Enough of this sorrow. If it pleases you, we will all go to the cave and reside there' ''” and develops toward “All the monkeys agreed to the favourable suggestion of Tara which was acceptable to Angada. 'A plan may be made in such a way by which we will not be…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Hanuman listened to the proposal of Tara, whose face was like the Moon, lord of the stars. (After listening to Tara) he thought Angada might take over the kingdom from Sugriva.” and develops toward “'It is said that when a strong man fights a weak one, the weak opponent should adopt the strategy of asana (watching) for selfprotection. The weak…”

This passage begins with “'You may think it safe to stay here in this opening of the earth. It is a petty task for the arrows of Lakshmana to break open this socalled cave.” and develops toward “'Your father's brother is virtuous, wellintentioned, loving, steadfast in action, and true to his promise. He will not kill you.”

I will fast unto death here only. Death for me is desirable.”

This passage begins with “'Convey my greetings with due courtesy to Rama and Lakshmana on my behalf and my respects to my father's younger brother, king of monkeys, Sugriva and wish him well.” and develops toward “While the numerous monkeys, resembling peaks of high mountains were roaring, it resounded through the mountain caves. It was like the sound of the…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While the monkeys were preparing to fast unto death on the plateau of the mountain, Sampati, the prosperous king of vultures, the longlived elder brother of Jatayu widely known for his…” and develops toward “'Jatayu, the knower of dharma, has laid down his life for the wellbeing of Rama. We are exhausted in the process of helping Rama. We have lost hope…”

This passage begins with “'The king of vultures was slain by Ravana in a combat. He attained supreme status. He had no fear for Sugriva (as we have).” and develops toward “The wise king of vultures was shaken up mentally on seeing the monkeys lying on the ground and hearing the unhappy narration of Angada. इत्यार्षे…”

He had two sons called Sugriva and Vali.Both were strong like…”

This passage begins with “'O powerful bird my revered grandfather Riksharaja was a glorious king of monkeys. He had two sons called Sugriva and Vali.Both were strong like floodwaters. My father Vali was wellknown in…” and develops toward “'Then Rama made friendship with my father's brother, great self Sugriva and killed my father.”

My wings are burnt and my strength is depleted. The only substantial help I can render Rama is through my words.”

This passage begins with “'I know Varuna's worlds(seas and oceans) and all the three worlds measured by three strides by Vamana. Indeed I also know about the friction between the great gods and demons and the…” and develops toward “'O monkeys traverse a hundred yojanas across the sea. On reaching the southern coast you will be able to see Ravana's Lanka defended and surrounded…”

This passage begins with “'O monkeys traverse a hundred yojanas across the sea. On reaching the southern coast you will be able to see Ravana's Lanka defended and surrounded by the sea. Now proceed swiftly and prove…” and develops toward “Th mightiest of monkeys carried Sampati, whose wings were totally burnt, to the sea shore and after he had offered oblations took him back to the…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The mighty monkeys were delighted to hear the nectarlike words of the king of vultures.” and develops toward “'O dear father I went to get food at the right time and stood blocking the entrance of mount Mahendra.”

This passage begins with “'There I stood alone looking down, obstructing the path of thousands of creatures inhabiting the ocean.” and develops toward “'I will please you with my word and thought and do not doubt that Rama's task is dear to me as well.”

This passage begins with “'Indeed, you monkeys are very wise, strong and sensitive. The king of monkeys has sent you on this mission. You cannot be conquered even by gods.” and develops toward “'Do not bother about the time limit. Resolve to achieve the objective, for wise people like you should not lag behind in accepting challenges.'…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Sampati bathed and offered libations to Jatayu. Then all the monkeys sat round him on the beautiful mountain.” and develops toward “'In the vicinity of that hermitage there was no tree without flowers or fruits and fragrant was the breeze that blew around.”

This passage begins with “'On reaching the sacred hermitage, I stood and waited under a tree for the audience of the divine sage Nishakara.” and develops toward “'What disease has affected you? Why are your wings dropped down? Who punished you in this way? Tell me.' इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीय आदिकाव्ये…”

Once again I focused my attention (on the Sun) and could see the Sungod with great…”

This passage begins with “'My mind was disturbed and I turned my attention from the object. Once again I focused my attention (on the Sun) and could see the Sungod with great effort. The Sun appeared to us equal in…” and develops toward “'Bereft of my kingdom, my brother and my wings, and having lost my strength I desire to fall down from the mountain top and die.' इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “'When thus I spoke to the venerable sage in that manner roaring very painfully the divine sage replied after reflecting silently for a moment:” and develops toward “'It is good for you in all respects to remain here. Where can you go (without wings)? Wait for appropriate time and place. You will recover your…”

This passage begins with “'I am not enthusiastic about giving you the wings now. Remain here and carry on the work for the welfare of the world.” and develops toward “'I also want to see Rama and Lakshmana. But I do not like to hold on to my life for long. Hence I will give up my body'. The knower of self, the sage…”

chapters.name: Sarga 63
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “'The sage who was skilful in speech, having praised me thus and in many other ways, permitted me to leave and entered his dwelling.” and develops toward “By all means make the effort; you will trace Sita, and this growing of my wings is a sign assuring your future success.”

This passage begins with “Sampati, the foremost of the birds thus spoke to the monkeys and flew up from the mountain's peak keen on finding the aerial path.” and develops toward “The monkeys as strong as the wind regained courage and went in search of the daughter of Janaka in the direction of Abhijit (constellation known as…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Hearing the words spoken by the king of vultures, the highly courageous monkeys assembled together and leaped with joyscreaming.” and develops toward “'If one succumbs to despondency while he has to exhibit his valour, his brilliance gets diminished and he does not succeed in his manly pursuits'.”

This passage begins with “With the night over, Angada joined the older monkeys again and sought their counsel.” and develops toward “'None of you at any time have faced any obstruction in doing your deeds. You are efficient in leaping. O bulls among monkeys now speak out, who among…”

chapters.name: Sarga 65
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Thereafter, on hearing Angada, all the leaders among the monkeys, Gaja, Gavaksha, Gavaya, Sarabha, Gandhamadana, Mainda, Dvivida, Sushena and Jambavan as well expressed one after the other…” and develops toward “'However in this situation, it is not possible for us to neglect (our task) as it is the order of the monkeyking and Rama who are determined to…”

This passage begins with “Know the extent of my leap as lessened by age: I can spring ninety yojanas, without doubt.” and develops toward “'O subduer of enemies in acomplishing this task you are the root. O dear therefore you should always be taken care of by us just like the wife looked…”

This passage begins with “'The root of any objective should be protected well. This is the basis of an accomplisher. Only when the roots are intact, the trees have the ability to yield flowers and fruits.” and develops toward “'O hero this work will not be balked. I will now inspire one who can achieve this task.'”

chapters.name: Sarga 66
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then Jambavan observed the worries of several hundred thousands of the monkey army and said to Hanuman:” and develops toward “'While the largeeyed lady was stationed on the mountain top, the Windgod gently let her auspicious yellow garment with red border fly away.”

This passage begins with “'Then he (Windgod) saw her curved, wellset thighs, her beautiful plump breasts and her lovely pleasing face.” and develops toward “'You fell on the edge of the mountain peak and your left chin got broken. Since then you are widely known as Hanuman (one who has a wounded chin).”

This passage begins with “The Windgod who is a carrier of fragrance and breaker of trees became very angry on seeing (the injury on) you and stopped blowing in the three worlds.” and develops toward “'O Hanuman, tiger among monkeys, rise up and cross this great ocean.You have that supreme capacity among all beings.”

This passage begins with “'O Hanuman All the monkeys look dejected. Why do you delay? O warrior, act quick with your rapid speed like Trivikrama who took three strides that covered the entire universe'.” and develops toward “'O Hanuman All the monkeys look dejected. Why do you delay? O warrior, act quick with your rapid speed like Trivikrama who took three strides that…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “'In order to cross a hundred yojanas, Hanuman, the best of monkeys expanded his might and strength. Seeing him, a warrior of great strength, all the monkeys, filled with enthusiasm at once…” and develops toward “'I can submerge this world including its mountains rivers and lakes. I can push back the sea waters quickly with my arms.”

This passage begins with “'Thrust by my thighs,the ocean will rise up, carrying with it the shanks and mighty crocodiles stunned by the force.” and develops toward “'Only Garuda and Maruta have the capacity to fly like me. No one other than the king of vultures and the Windgod have the speed to fly like me.”

This passage begins with “'Just as the lightning generated by the clouds lands at once flying in the sky without any support I can also land in less than a moment.” and develops toward “Then the tiger among monkeys, Hanuman said to those forestdwellers (monkeys), 'The earth cannot bear the thrust of my leaping.”

This passage begins with “'The rocks on the peaks of the ranges of mount Mahendra cannot with stand it, although they are huge and firm.” and develops toward “The serpents with their bodies half protruded out of the rocks and hissing looked like flags planted on the mountain.”

This passage begins with “The alarmed and agitated sages abandonned the mountain peaks.The mountain appeared like a lone traveller separated from the caravan in the great forest.” and develops toward “Magnanimous, wise and heroic Hanuman, destroyer of enemy warriors, fixed his attention on speed, betook himself mentally to Lanka (ere he reached it…”

chapters.name: Sarga 1
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Hanuman, crusher of enemies resolved to find the whereabouts of Sita, carried away by Ravana. He followed the path of the Charanas, celestial bards.” and develops toward “Pressed firmly by Hanuman, the mountain was shaken for a moment which caused the flowers to drop down from the tree tops.”

This passage begins with “With heaps of flowers full of fragrance fallen down from the trees all over the mountain, it looked as though it was a mountain of flowers.” and develops toward “Even the ascetics left the place approaching that the mountain would be broken into pieces by creatures. The alarmed vidyadharas leaped into the sky…”

This passage begins with “The intoxicated vidyadharas with eyes red like lotuses, wearing garlands around the neck and smeared with red sandalpaste flew into the sky.” and develops toward “The glorious and courageous Hanuman his head and shoulders contracted, summoned up all his energy and vigour.”

This passage begins with “Having cast his eyes upward in order to view the distance and direction in which he had to leap, he restrained his breath in the chest while looking at the sky.” and develops toward “Uprooted by the force of Hanuman's thighs,great trees like sala followed him just like the troops follow the king.”

This passage begins with “With numerous trees laden with flowers following him, Hanuman who looked like a mountain, presented a queer spectable.” and develops toward “By the reflection of his copperyred nose, his face looked red like the Sun's orb coming close at twilight.”

This passage begins with “As the son of the Windgod was flying through the sky, his coiled up tail looked splendid like the banner installed at the festival in honour of Indra (on the fifteenth day of the month of…” and develops toward “Hanuman moving along in high speed looked as if he was counting the mighty waves risen ike Meru and Mandara mountains.”

This passage begins with “Then the water raised by his onrush along with the cloud widely spread in the sky shone like an outstreched autumnal cloud.” and develops toward “The Sun spared Hanuman from the heat and even the Windgod served him, blowing cool while he was taking leaps in order to fulfil Rama's mission.”

This passage begins with “The sages praised him while he was coursing through the sky, the gods and gandharvas sang in praise of him extolling his majesty and vigour.” and develops toward “"I should help him for he is working for the welfare of the Ikshavaku family. The Ikshvaku kings are worthy of adoration to me and for you as well.”

This passage begins with “"You may act as our minister and let us not miss this chance. Pious men lose their cool if a worthy act is left unfulfilled.” and develops toward “The great vanara,who was swift in action felled down the mountain that had risen high instantly with his chest just as the wind would strike a cloud.”

This passage begins with “Felled by the vanara's speed, the tall mountain overwhelmed with joy roared in high pitch.” and develops toward “"Even a newcomer, however ordinary, deserves worship by a wise man who wishes to educate himself in the path of righteousness. Needless to say that…”

This passage begins with “"O foremost among the vanaras you are the son of the highsouled Windgod's, preeminent among the gods, you are also equal to him in speed.” and develops toward “"My commitment is hastening me. The day set (for the accomplishment of the task) is also drawing to a close. A vow is made and I will not halt in the…”

This passage begins with “Having said this, the mighty monkey gently touched the mountain with his hands before ascending to the sky with a smile on his face.” and develops toward “Then mount Mainaka, assured by Indra became secure. And Hanuman also crossed over the ocean in a short while.”

This passage begins with “Then the gods, gandharvas, siddhas and great sages approached Surasa, the mother of nagas, who was glowing like the Sungod and said:.” and develops toward “Or else, after seeing Maithili and the ever-untroubled Rama, I shall return into your mouth—this I truly promise you.”

This passage begins with “Having heard Hanuman, Surasa capable of taking any form at her free will told him that no one can cross (her region) as she had been given such a boon(by the Creator)৷৷” and develops toward “Glorious and mighty Hanuman quickly went into her mouth and got out and stood in the sky and said:.”

This passage begins with “"O Dakshayani I have indeed entered your mouth and your boon has come true (as nobody can go against the boon of the Creator). Allow me to proceed in quest of Vaidhehi. My salutations to…” and develops toward “Hanuman passed through the path of wind like Garuda. The region was refreshed with clouds that released torrents of rain and frequented by birds. The…”

This passage begins with “Hanuman, son of the Windgod was seen everywhere in the sky. He appeared like a longwinged king of mountains unsupported.” and develops toward “Hanuman, the vanara whose physique was as hard as thunderbolt suddenly contracted once again and entered her opened mouth.”

This passage begins with “The siddhas and charanas who were watching his performance of drowning in her mouth were amazed as though the moon is seized by Rahu on a fullmoon night.” and develops toward “Hanuman, the best among jumpers, even while in air he saw an island adorned with many trees, also gardens adjacent to Malaya mountain.”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The mighty Hanuman crossed the inviolable sea, stood comfortably on the peak of Trikuta mountain and looked at Lanka.” and develops toward “The mighty vanara saw around Lanka, trees like sarala, karnikara in full bloom, datepalms, priyala, muchulinda, kutaja, kethaka trees filled with…”

This passage begins with “The mighty vanara saw around Lanka, trees like sarala, karnikara in full bloom, datepalms, priyala, muchulinda, kutaja, kethaka trees filled with fragrance priyangu, kadamba, so also…” and develops toward “Observing Lanka that resembled mount Kailas, with sky scrapers appearing as if flying, filled with dreadful demons and nagas protecting the city of…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On hearing the many harsh words of dire threat by the ugly ogresses, the daughter of Janaka wept.” and develops toward “'There is a famous popular saying among the learned that untimely death is not possible either for a woman or a man.It is true that I am living in…”

This passage begins with “'There is a famous popular saying among the learned that untimely death is not possible either for a woman or a man.It is true that I am living in this miserable condition, deprived of…” and develops toward “'Fie upon this human life. This passage begins with “"Rama is renowned, wise, grateful and kindly. It is due to my misfortune that he has turned pitiless to me. There is no doubt.” and develops toward “"Like me weeping, the cry of ogresses with lost husbands will be heard in every house in Lanka. There is no doubt.”

This passage begins with “"If only Lakshmana and Rama in their quest for Lanka reach here, it is not possible within their sight for the enemy to survive even a moment.” and develops toward “Nothing is forbidden for sinful demons. A calamity will befall now due to this adharma. The flesheating ogres are aware of neither dharma nor adharma.”

This passage begins with “"This demon is determined to make me his morning meal. How can I do anything in the absence of my lord of pleasing countenance. However, death is not ordained for me even in this sorrowful state.”

This passage begins with “Blessed are the exhalted sages who have not committed any sin. They have self control and have subdued their senses. There is no pleasure or displeasure for them.” and develops toward “"Separated from the wise Rama who deserves pleasant things I will give up life being under the clutches of the sinful Ravana. इत्यार्षे…”

This passage begins with “"All the demonesses in the city of Lanka which had turned dry, reduced to ashes, were dancing, laughing and shouting loudly drunk with oil..” and develops toward “"This dream indeed served as an augery for good news about her. This passage begins with “"If Rama enquires about the message of Sita, this lady of beautiful waist, what can I speak without meeting and talking to her?” and develops toward “"Seeing me holding big and small branches of large trees and leaping from one place to the other, the ogresses will get scared.”

This passage begins with “"Indeed your plight, the superhuman beauty and your robes marked with asceticism, surely tell me that you are Rama's queen."” and develops toward “"The illustrious Rama who valued his father's word more than sovereignty, bowed to the command of the father.”

This passage begins with “"Rama, whose strength was his truthfulness, gave whatever others sought but would not accept anything in return. This passage begins with “"The demons are in large number and they will be armed with weapons. You are unarmed. How can you fight and protect me?” and develops toward “"O chief of vanaras I cherish my devotion towards Rama alone and not touch the body of any other man.”

I am dwelling in this great ocean and known as Mainaka.”

She cannot be destroyed'.” and develops toward “'I came here as a servant sent by your tireless husband to find you.”

This passage begins with “'O glorious lady the tiger among men, the illustrious son of Dasaratha himself sent this ring to you as a taken of identity.” and develops toward “"Thereafter, I increased my body to the size of a mountain, intent on fighting and started destroying the pleasure garden.”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The army of Vanaras settled that way by Rama's command shone like the autumnal full moon with auspicious stars.” and develops toward “"You may see the trees shaking violently by the pleasant breeze, inhabited by proud birds, drunken honeybees and crowded with cuckoos and other birds…”

This passage begins with “Dasaratha's son Rama thus talked to Lakshmana in that manner and asked to divide the task of the army in accordance with what is said in sastras.” and develops toward “Relieved by the word of Rama, tormented Suka who was highly terrified returned to the king of Rakshasas.”

This passage begins with “Laughing at Suka, Ravana spoke these words. "Why are your wings fastened? Your wings have been clipped, it appears!. I suppose you have not fallen under the clutches of the fickle minded…” and develops toward “Angry, redeyed Ravana, having heard Suka's words spoke as though he would burn him with his glance.”

This passage begins with “"Even if gods, Gandharvas and demons come to wage war with me, even if all the worlds threaten me, I shall not give away Sita."” and develops toward “"It is not possible (to win over) for the thousand eyed Indra, or even Varuna, the god presiding over water, or even for Yama, the god of death and…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On hearing about Rama, the son of Dasaratha crossing the shore of the ocean along with the army, exuberant Ravana spoke to his ministers Suka and Saarana.” and develops toward “The ocean of army settled on the mountain summits, in caverns and caves, on the shores of the ocean, in the forests and adjoining gardens some who…”

This passage begins with “"Tomorrow at the dawn of the day I will release my dreadful anger on Ravana and even on Rakshasas just as the thunderbolt of Indra was released on Rakshasas."” and develops toward “"Even if Rama and Lakshmana are protected, and even Sugriva also, the entire army seems formidable like suras and asuras."”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “There after Ravana having heard the words spoken by Suka about the army of Vanara heroes, seeing Lakshmana of extraordinary valour, the right shoulder of Rama standing close by Rama his own…” and develops toward “"Trees may remain unscathed even on touching forest fire, but those who are punishable do not remain unpunished on account of the king and are…”

This passage begins with “Reaching the Suvela mountain in disguise, they saw Rama and Lakshmana along with Sugriva and Vibheeshana.” and develops toward “Harassed by the valorous and swift Vanaras, those Rakshasas lost their senses and returned to Lanka gasping and panting.”

After releasing me, Rama is advancing into Lanka surrounded by mountains like a monkey army in the form of Garuda."”

This passage begins with “"Before Rama reaches the boundary, the wall, do one thing quickly. Either give away Sita or set out to give a good war."” and develops toward “"The firegod's son indeed is the army chief known as Nila, and here is windgod's son widely known as Hanuman."”

This passage begins with “"The indomitable and powerful young Angada is the grandson of Indra, the strong Mainda and Dwivida are both the twin sons of Ashwini Devas, Gaja, Gavaksha, Gavaya, Sarabha and Gandhamadana…” and develops toward “"I have described to you everything about the Vanara strength stationed on Suvela mountain. This passage begins with “While she was guarded by dreadful Rakshasa women surrounding her closely, Ravana went near her happily and saw her. He addressed Sita praising her.” and develops toward “"There after the head of Rama, who was sleeping, was severed by Prahastha, skilled at striking, with a big sword without any feeling of attachment."”

This passage begins with “"When Vibheeshana rose up he was caught by chance. Lakshmana and Vanaras fled in all directions."” and develops toward “"Many fierceeyed Rakshasas killed several Vanaras on the shore of the ocean, in the mountains and woods."”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On seeing the esteemed Rama's head and bow, remembering the description made by Hanuman about the friendship with Sugriva thinking of the eyes, complexion of the face, the hair, and curls…” and develops toward “"O Raghava of incomprehensible valour! the words predicted by astrologers that your life is long is falsified. Your life has been short."”

This passage begins with “"Or else, even the wisdom of a learned one is diffused and lost perhaps! the effect of time which is the cause of all living beings has caused this."” and develops toward “"Why are you, who performed all kinds of sacrificial rituals of fire with all offerings deserve sanctification without doing fire sacrifice?"”

This passage begins with “"Seeing only Lakshmana returning, of the three exiled, Kausalya will be immersed in grief (will ask)."” and develops toward “"O Lord! Accompanied by all ministers Prahastha is waiting and sends me desiring to see you."”

This passage begins with “"O great king, endowed with forgiveness and royalty! there is a little urgent work. Undoubtedly, they are waiting to see you please."” and develops toward “Then the attendants collected the great powerful army saying, 'so be it', to Ravana who was desirous of war. ।।…”

This passage begins with “"Rama having crossed the sea with his Vanara army will not go back with life. It is true."” and develops toward “There after making arrangements adequately for the protection of Lanka, taking leave of ministers who offered blessings for his prosperity and…”

This passage begins with “To make Rama happy, all the Vanara army divided into groups and started ascending the boundary (of Lanka) walls the king of Rakshasas was witnessing.” and develops toward “Praghasa the broad shouldered Vanara, was surrounded by Panasa for assisting him who encamped surrounded by many.”
