This passage begins with “"Even if all the sages, Devatas and Rakshasas collected together come, they cannot get liberated from this bondage of arrows."” and develops toward “"O Rakshasas! Look at these two brothers together bound by my fierce arrows in front of you."”

This passage begins with “Indrajith having spoken in that way to the Rakshasas, all of them were highly amazed by the action of Indrajith and went laughing.” and develops toward “Wise Vibheeshana having wiped Sugriva's face clean in accordance with time addressed with opportune words.”

This passage begins with “"O king of monkeys! This is not the time for faint heartedness. Indrajith presented details saying that both Rama and Lakshmana…”

This passage begins with “Then the Rakshasas took the aerial car near Sita who was overcome by her husband's grief.” and develops toward “Janaka's daughter of beautiful limbs and dark eyes, seeing her husband and Lakshmana, both powerful ones rolling, roared aloud.”

My hair on the skin is soft. When I touch the ground with my ten fingers and toes, it is said to leave…”

This passage begins with “"My palms and feet placed together have no space which are auspicious marks and brahmins have said that my gentle smile is also a mark of auspiciousness."” and develops toward “"If a husband ceases life in war, their face will not have an angry look or happy appearance."”

This passage begins with “"Vaidehi! Indeed, this aerial car called Pushpaka which is an auspicious one would not have brought you if they are ceased of life."” and develops toward “Thereafter Sita along with Trijata went to Pushpaka. Making Sita to get down, rakshasi also entered Ashoka grove.”
Having entered the Rakshasa's pleasure garden which had many trees, Sita gave way to intense sorrow having keenly observed the king's sons. ।।…

Me of sinful deeds and not a noble one, because of whose action Lakshmana's life having departed and lying on the bed of arrows."”

This passage begins with “"You were always comforting me when I was sad. Now however much I am grieved, ceased of life you cannot comfort me."” and develops toward “"Sugriva! Return back from here along with the army keeping Angada in front, even Neela and Nala. Cross the ocean."”

This passage begins with “"O King of Bears! King of Golangulas! Difficult action has been done by you and others in war. I am pleased with Hanumantha's action."” and develops toward “Seeing Vibheeshana resembling a dark mountain, thinking him to be Indrajith, all the Vanaras ran from there. ।।…”

This passage begins with “On seeing the body of Rama and Lakshmana fully covered by arrows, Vibheeshana also became piteous and sad.” and develops toward “"On regaining consciousness, take the brothers Rama and Lakshmana, the tamers of enemies to Kishkinda accompanied by the heroic monkey army."”

This passage begins with “"On killing Ravana and his sons along with his kin, I will on my part also bring Mythili just as Indra recovered his prosperity."” and develops toward “Then all the Vanaras perceived Garuda endowed with mighty prowess, the son of Vinatha blazing like fire.”

This passage begins with “Seeing the noble Rama and Lakshmana bound by the serpents in the form of arrows (released by Indrajith), the serpents fled.” and develops toward “"For the demons endowed with great strength and of mighty valour or for Gandharvas, or Vanaras or for even Devatas, or Indra who has done a hundred…”

They had won many wars and never turned back in war.…”

This passage begins with “Tearing up each other with exceedingly sharp teeth they were behaving like a lion and tiger. They had won many wars and never turned back in war. This passage begins with “Seizing mountain tops whose slopes were filled with trees Sugriva hurled at the night rangers. Watching this Ravana quickly broke them with his arrows fixed with golden shafts.” and develops toward “Sri Rama, who was highly energetic and truthful in enthusiasm to succeed, told Lakshmana, "You go and be careful in the battle."”

This passage begins with “Endowed with a great mind and energetic Hanuman became alert and seeking to fight with Ravana, feeling fresh, enraged, looking at Nila said "It is not correct to attack one who is fighting…” and develops toward “Provoked by the clamour of the vanaras, Ravana's heart was full of anger and did not know what to do.”

You have come under the path of my sight. You are going to meet your end. You who are perverted in mind, struck by a stream of my arrows…”

The strength of other Rakshasas is on account of their boons received by them."”

Therefore, remain sleeping like a dead one from today, ' said Brahma and…”

This passage begins with “"Oh! Kumbhakarna of extraordinary prowess! You were asleep for long happily. You do not know that I am afraid of Rama."” and develops toward “"You are a lover of wars. You are a lover of relations. You are always my dear well wisher. O self glorious one! Do this task of throwing out the…”

To talk of it now again is futile."”

This passage begins with “"You may speak of only what is proper to do now. Do not think of the past as it is gone."” and develops toward “"Be happy today, O Mighty armed brother! I will bring Rama's head from the battlefield. Sita will be sad. You will see."”

This passage begins with “" All the Rakshasas in Lanka whose relations have been killed will witness Rama's death which will be very welcome."” and develops toward “"O King! while I am here for what reason do you worry and have anxiety? I will go now and decidedly destroy your enemy."”

This passage begins with “"O King! Give up the fear, as both Rama and Lakshmana will be made short of work in the battlefield, as also mighty Sugriva and dispose of Hanuman, the destroyer of many Rakshasas and who…” and develops toward “"Through the destruction of Rama, I will bring happiness to you who are worthy of happiness. sections.summary: This passage begins with “Hearing the words of mighty Kumbhakarna, of massive body and round arms Mahodara said.” and develops toward “"How is it possible for you single handed to win Raghava of exceeding might, who killed many Rakshasas in the past at Janasthana?"”

You have awakened for the sake of putting an end to the enemy. It is a momentous period for the Rakshasas, O tamer…”

This passage begins with “"Therefore, taking up trident holding the noose in hand like the God of Death proceed to devour the monkeys as well as the two princes shining like the Sun."” and develops toward “"Therefore, go surrounded by the army. They are extremely difficult to encounter. They are like thunderbolts. Destroy them who are on the enemy side…”

This passage begins with “And then the brilliant Ravana got up from his seat, garlanded Kumbhakarna with garland s of flowers encrusted with gems.” and develops toward “Kumbhakarna, the enemy of Devas and Danavas determined and eager showered with a rain of flowers, head covered with parasol, excited with drink,…”

This passage begins with “Rakshasas of loud voice, great prowess, and fearful with frightening eyes, lifted the many weapons in hand and followed on foot.” and develops toward “The clouds appeared in the colour of donkey and there was thunder and lightning. The oceans, forests and earth seemed as though shaken.”

This passage begins with “She jackals were seen with flaming morsels throwing from their mouths. Birds wheeled in a circular way and settled.” and develops toward “Armed with a huge iron bar, Kumbhakarna stood for destroying the enemies like the great time spirit, deadly God of Death waits at the end of the…”

chapters.name: Sarga 66
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Mighty Kumbhakarna, a gigantic one, resembling a mountain peak, having swiftly crossed over the boundary of the city roared.” and develops toward “Enraged Kumbhakarna began to destroy Vanaras endowed with extraordinary vigour, just as great forest fire burns the forest.”

This passage begins with “Many bulls among the Vanaras lay on the ground like trees with red flowers on top tossed and fallen on the ground.” and develops toward “"He who is defeated lives, but people will say he is timid. Resorting to the path of good people and serving them gives up fear."”

This passage begins with “"If we are killed in war, fallen dead on the ground and our life span is reduced, we will attain the realm of Brahma which is difficult to attain."” and develops toward “Rshaba, Sarabha, Mainda and Nila, Kumuda, Susena, Rambha, and Tara headed by Dwiviida, Panasa and Hanuman marched ahead swiftly to the battlefield.…”

chapters.name: Sarga 67
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard Angada's words, the Vanaras of gigantic body firmly decided to wage war and returned back.” and develops toward “The battlefield on which the Rakshasas and horses were crushed, charioteers were killed by the speed of the mountain remained wet with blood.”

This passage begins with “The Rakshasa charioteers severed the roaring Vanara leaders forcibly with their arrows emitting frightening sounds. They were like the Lord of Death at universal dissolution.” and develops toward “Rshaba, Sarabha, Neela, Gavaksha and Gandhamadana, these five tigers among Vanaras attacked Kumbhakarna.”

This passage begins with “Those Vanaras of exceptional strength attacked gigantic Kumbhakarna on all sides by rocks, trees and by their fists, hands and feet also.” and develops toward “As Kumbhakarna thrust Vanaras into his mouth, the Vanaras came out through his nostrils and also through his ears.”

This passage begins with “The foremost of the Rakshasas, who resembled a mountain became enraged and mutilated the Vanaras.” and develops toward “Angada who was knowledgeable of warfare, endowed with prowess, a bull among Vanaras, stopped the pike approaching him with agility and escaped.”

This passage begins with “Jumping up that way, Angada slapped him with his palm on the chest in anger. By that the mountain like Kumbhakarna became unconscious.” and develops toward “On hearing Kumbhakarna's words, Sugriva forcibly discharged a mountain resembling a thunderbolt on the chest of Kumbhakarna.”

This passage begins with “Then the mountain peak dropped on the chest broke into pieces. Thereafter the monkeys were despaired, and Rakshasas roared in joy.” and develops toward “Then Kumbhakarna, an enemy of Indra, who was equal to Indra in heroism seizing Sugriva, the Indra of monkeys, who is equal to Indra, thought, 'If…”

This passage begins with “Seeing the army running away here and there and Sugriva being captured by Kumbhakarna, wise Hanuman, the son of Maruti started thinking.” and develops toward “Exceptionally strong Sugriva with parched grain and fragrant water showered on him in the royal path, being cool, regained consciousness.”

This passage begins with “(Sugriva) being strong and painfully gained senses, from the arms of the great Rakshasa, started looking at the royal path of the city. He was bewildered and started pondering this way.” and develops toward “Taking one, two, three, and many Vanaras along with Rakshasas hastily with one hand he was gulping them.”

This passage begins with “Then the mighty Kumbhakarna while being struck by peaks of mountains, he was feasting on Vanaras fat and blood oozing out from his mouth.” and develops toward “"He who is ready to face me, a rival of Lord of Death in conflict, with weapons in hand in great battle is worthy of honour to me."”

This passage begins with “"Indra mounted on the elephant, Airavata surrounded by all Devatas, indeed never stood before me in conflict."” and develops toward “Adorned by peacock's plumes, those arrows slipped into his breast, he lost senses, his mace naturally dropped down from his hand.”

This passage begins with “All his weapons scattered on the ground, the mighty Kumbhakarna deprived of weapons thought for himself and created havoc with his feet and fists.” and develops toward “Having heard the words of the wise Prince, the monkeys collected climbed on Kumbhakarna happily.”

This passage begins with “Enraged Kumbhakarna shook off the monkeys climbed on his body like a harsh elephant shakes off its rider.” and develops toward “Enraged by the loud sound of the twang of the bow, the bull among Rakshasas unable to bear rushed towards Raghava.”

This passage begins with “Thereafter Rama spoke to Kumbhakarna, whose hands were like Vasuki's body, coming towards him to attack on the battlefield like a cloud chased by wind.” and develops toward “Then Kumbhakarna with his club covered with blood and frightening even the celestials scattered the monkeys.”

This passage begins with “Thereupon taking up the weapon of Wind God, Rama discharged on the night ranger and severed his right arm with the mace. His arm torn off, Kumbhakarna made uproarious noise.” and develops toward “Rama crushed the night ranger to pieces with his arrow encrusted with diamonds and gold, glowing like the Sun 's rays and fire which resembled…”

This passage begins with “Discharged from Rama's arm, the terrific weapon went just as the Lord of god's weapon, which was like Indra's thunderbolt burning like fire, shining by its brilliance and went in all…” and develops toward “Many Vanaras, with their faces resembling full blown lotuses joyfully laughed and worshipped Rama for getting rid of an enemy of terrific strength.”

chapters.name: Sarga 68
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Seeing Kumbhakarna slain by the glorious Raghava, the Rakshasas informed Ravana, the king of Rakshasas.” and develops toward “"I who depended on you like my right arm did not fear Devatas or Danavas and I am destroyed now."”

This passage begins with “"You, who crushed the pride of Devas and Danavas are equal to timespirit and death at dissolution and being a hero, how did you die in the hands of Raghava?"” and develops toward “"Righteous and prosperous Vibheeshana has been expelled by me earlier. By that action, I have obtained this grief."”

chapters.name: Sarga 69
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Hearing the lamentations of the evilminded Ravana, Trisira spoke to him.” and develops toward “All sons of Ravana possessed stupendous strength, all were very famous, and never got defeated in war be it Gandharvas, Kinnaras or even gods will…”

This passage begins with “All were experts in the use of weapons, all were heroes and skilled in warfare, knowledgeable in scriptures, so also all were blessed with boons.” and develops toward “Trisira seated in the best of chariots, with his three heads adorned with crowns shone like the golden mountain, Himalayas with its three peaks.”

This passage begins with “Then Atikaya the very bright son of Rakshasa king, foremost of all archers mounted on the best of chariots.” and develops toward “Those heroes determined to defeat the enemy even if it meant death and went thinking for the combat.”

This passage begins with “The great Rakshasas who were fierce in combat went roaring shouting loudly and discharging arrows.” and develops toward “The Vanaras of dreadful valour and unparalleled might waged with trees, rock and mountain when obstructed by the Rakshasas।।”

This passage begins with “The Rakshasas and Vanaras enthusiastic in the battlefield roared like lion. The Vanaras pounded the Rakshasas with rocks.” and develops toward “The Rakshasas killed by Vanaras, their shields and weapons broken, blood flowed from their limbs just as sap exudes from broken trees.”

This passage begins with “In the battlefield a chariot with another chariot, an elephant with another elephant, a horse with another horse struck one another.” and develops toward “Mighty Naranthaka, the destroyer of humans, ranged over all sides in all directions in the battlefield, like the wind in the rainy season, taking a…”

This passage begins with “Mighty Naranthaka, the destroyer of humans, ranged over all sides in all directions in the battlefield, like the wind in the rainy season, taking a burning spear and crushing everything.” and develops toward “Angada, the best of the Vanaras, adorned with arm lets resembled masses of rocks and shone like a mountain with metal streaks.”

This passage begins with “Brilliant Angada, unarmed only with nails and teeth (to fight) advanced towards Naranthaka and spoke these words.” and develops toward “Naranthaka, the foremost of the heroes, having been killed in combat, the Devatas and gods of the three worlds experienced great joy.”

chapters.name: Sarga 70
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Seeing the bulls among Rakshasas Naranthaka, Devanthaka, Trisira and Mahodara a scion of Paulastya and Mahodara, killed the Rakshasas cried aloud.” and develops toward “Eminent and highly energetic son of Vali did not feel perturbed when three of the foremost Rakshasas attacked him together.”

This passage begins with “Angada, who was endowed with high speed, and exceedingly difficult to encounter, jumped up the huge elephant of Mahodara attacked him.” and develops toward “Then the son of wind god, Hanuman observing Devanthaka coming towards him, got up and hit him on the forehead with his thunderbolt like fist.”

This passage begins with “When the heroic and strong son of the wind god hit on the head of Devanthaka, the Rakshasas trembled by the sound of the great monkey 's blow.” and develops toward “Stupendous Trisira tore with his whetted arrows the rain of trees through the sky and roared.”

This passage begins with “Then Hanuman also jumped up in anger and tore the horse off Trisira just as the lion would tear the lordly elephant with nails.” and develops toward “The heads of Trisira, an enemy of Indra, resembling mountains with incongruous eyes glowing like Vyshvanara's eyes, fell into wilderness like stars…”

This passage begins with “Trisira, the enemy of gods, endowed with the prowess of Indra got killed by Hanuman. The earth shook, the monkeys roared and the Rakshasas from all sides took to their heels.” and develops toward “Like a tree whose roots have been severed, the Rakshasa fell on the ground. Then seizing forcibly, the dreadful mace that resembled the rod of Lord…”

This passage begins with “For a short while, Mahaparsva, the enemy of gods remained as if life had gone off up and struck the son of waters (Rshaba).” and develops toward “Strength gone, life ceased, eyes crushed, his own Rakshasas fallen on the ground, the Rakshasa army fled.”

chapters.name: Sarga 71
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Highly energetic Atikaya, who was like a mountain in form, had huge body, obtained boons from Brahma, subdued the pride of Devas and Danavas, was equal to Indra in valour. Seeing his own…” and develops toward “"Who is he, the yelloweyed, resembling a mountain, seated in the broad chariot drawn by a thousand horses?"”

This passage begins with “"Who is he in the midst, with sharp tridents and shining spears, like Maheswara surrounded by beings?"” and develops toward “"He who shines like the full moon between two Punarvasu stars, his auspicious eyes and countenance shines with both earrings."”

This passage begins with “"Oh! Vibheeshana, a mighty armed one! Show me that foremost of Rakshasas, whom all Vanaras feared and fled in all directions."” and develops toward “"Oh, best of men! Quickly make efforts to put an end to him before he puts down Vanara army."”

This passage begins with “Thereafter, mighty Atikaya entered the monkey army, twanged his bow again and again and made a loud noise.” and develops toward “The twang of Lakshmana filled the mountains, earth, ocean, and all directions striking terrible fear in the night ranger.”

This passage begins with “Then the extraordinarily energetic and powerful son of Rakshasa king was in turn frightened at the sound of Saumithri's bow and was astonished.” and develops toward “"You are seated in the chariot with all kinds of weapons collected. By that alone you do not become capable. Show your capacity."”

This passage begins with “"Then I will strike down his head with sharp arrows and throw him just as ripened palm fruit drops in the process of time."” and develops toward “The heroic Lakshmana fully stretching the arrow bending to curvature struck Atikaya, the foremost of Rakshasas on his forehead.”

This passage begins with “That arrow stuck on the forehead of the terrific Rakshasa looked like a serpent on a mountain.” and develops toward “Then Lakshmana by concentration of his mind fixed on fire god which presided over the arrow, that great selffixed in his bow glowed.”

This passage begins with “Highly energetic Atikaya took a serpentlike arrow with gold feathers and charged it by the concentration of his mind on the power of Sun god.” and develops toward “Lakshmana of great fame, subduer of enemy looking at the arrows becoming useless mighty hero released thousand arrows.”

This passage begins with “Though covered with arrows showered on him, mighty Rakshasa Atikaya had an impenetrable shield in the battlefield and could not feel the torment.” and develops toward “As Atikaya was looking at the arrow, Lakshmana struck many arrows forcibly with sharp arrows possessing the speed of Garuda. The arrows reached…”

This passage begins with “Perceiving the glowing arrow coming, like the spirit of death, Atikaya struck it with unceasing effort with javelins, spears, maces, axes, tridents and even arrows.” and develops toward “Enemy who was difficult to approach and of terrible might being killed, the Vanaras were extremely delighted. Their faces looked like lotuses in…”

chapters.name: Sarga 72
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On hearing that Atikaya was killed by highly energetic Lakshmana, the king was overtaken by emotion and spoke as follows.” and develops toward “"Wherever the troops are posted here and there be it exit, or entrance should be checked again and again and not to go out."”

This passage begins with “"Surrounded by the army on all sides, whoever it may be, keep watching the movement of Vanaras."” and develops toward “Ravana, the king of Rakshasas, enjoined the Rakshasas and himself bearing the thorn of wrath, entered the abode desperately.”

chapters.name: Sarga 73
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then the Rakshasas who survived the killings, hurriedly reported to the Rakshasa king, Ravana about the killing of Devanthaka, Trisira and Atikaya.” and develops toward “Many Rakshasas of terrific valour, endowed with extraordinary strength, happily, wielding excellent bows, taking barbed missiles, hammers, whetted…”

This passage begins with “Many Rakshasas of terrific valour, endowed with extraordinary strength, happily, wielding excellent bows, taking barbed missiles, hammers, whetted axes, maces, Bhusandi mallets, Ayasthis,…” and develops toward “There he strewed into the fire, lances as leaves, dark coloured live go at clasped by its neck and consigned.”

This passage begins with “From the fire set ablaze by the of fearing of fire sticks into it was free from smoke burst into flames indicating victory.” and develops toward “Mighty and energetic son of Ravana, Indrajith became angry and began to attack the Vanaras.”

This passage begins with “Filled with anger, Indrajith alone with nine, five, seven arrows tore the Vanaras into pieces.” and develops toward “Then he struck Jambavan with ten, Neela with thirty, and Sugriva, Rshaba and Angada. In the same way Dwivida with sharp and terrific arrows that were…”

This passage begins with “Highly enraged like the fire at the time of universal destruction, he tormented many chiefs of Vanaras and others.” and develops toward “Hit by the Rakshasa king, the bulls among Vanaras gliding towards one another roared in discordant tones and fell down.”

This passage begins with “Some Vanaras looking up to the sky eyes pierced with arrows held one another and fell on the ground.” and develops toward “"This Indrajith is desisting from comb at on seeing us. As the Vanaras have lost pride and happiness and fallen into unconscious state, he would…”

This passage begins with “Seeing the two princes severed and fallen down struck by the net of arrows of Indrajith, he roared happily in the battle.” and develops toward “Thereafter Vanara army withdrew from the battle along with Rama and Lakshmana. Indrajith for his part moved to the abode protected by Ravana's arms,…”

chapters.name: Sarga 74
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The two brothers (Rama and Lakshmana) become unconscious in the forefront of the battle the monkey leaders lose senses. Sugriva, Neela, Angada and Jambavantha did not know What to do.” and develops toward “By the creator's missile Indrajith struck down sixtyseven crores of strong Vanaras between the fifth and last part of the day.”

This passage begins with “Vibheeshana and Hanuman seeing the ocean of Vanara army and the glowing arrows on them, looked for Jambavantha.” and develops toward “Thereafter, Hanuman the son of the wind god coming close to the aged Jambavan clasped his feet in accordance with tradition and greeted him.”

This passage begins with “Then again, Jambavan whose senses were affected, on hearing the statement of Hanuman, the foremost of Vanaras, felt as though he was born again.” and develops toward “Standing on the summit of the foremost of the mountains, hero Hanuman pressing his feet seemed like a second mountain”

This passage begins with “Trodden by the feet of Hanuman that mountain pressed forcibly by himself could not bear his strength.” and develops toward “Maruti, scorcher of enemies, endowed with terrific valour offered prayers to Sri Rama and made up his mind to embark on the stupendous task for…”

This passage begins with “Raising his tail which resembled a serpent, bending his back, and ears, opening his mouth wide expounded Hanuman sprang up at terrific speed.” and develops toward “He saw Vajralayam (where Brahma gave Indra the thunderbolt), the abode of Kubera shining like the Sun, the spot where the Sun was bound, Brahma's…”

This passage begins with “He saw Kailas, the rocks of Himalayas, that way on the Rshaba the foremost Lord Siva, the golden mountain, and the great mountain on which grew, glowing medicinal herbs.” and develops toward “The great Hanuman along with the mountain descended in the midst of the Vanara army, and greeted the army head bent down and embraced Vibheeshana…”

This passage begins with “The two sons of the ruler of men inhaling the fragrance of the herbs became free from wounds. Other Vanara heroes also got restored.” and develops toward “From the beginning of the war in Lanka till the end without exception, all the Rakshasas were killed in war by the Vanara leaders then and there were…”

chapters.name: Sarga 75
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Sugriva, the king of Vanaras called Hanuman to make him know the task ahead and said these words.” and develops toward “With fire all over, rugs made of hair of animals, whisks made of fur of animals, tiger skins, musk, wonderful pearls, and gems in laid dwellings of…”

This passage begins with “With fire all over, rugs made of hair of animals, whisks made of fur of animals, tiger skins, musk, wonderful pearls, and gems in laid dwellings of many kinds, and clusters of war weapons…” and develops toward “The lovely women sleeping in burnt mansion tops screamed loudly in desperation saying 'Alas! Oh Oh!' and removing their ornaments.”

This passage begins with “Like peaks of mountains struck by thunderbolt the mansions also fell, caught in fire.” and develops toward “Then Rama drew the best of bow, that created fear among the Rakshasas by its tumultuous sound.”

This passage begins with “Then, great Rama stretching his bow in great fury shone as though the divine Lord Siva was stretching his bow with imprints of Vedas.” and develops toward “Yupaksha, Sonithaksha, Prajangha, Kampana set out with both the sons of Kumbhakarna.”

This passage begins with “Roaring like a lion, he ordered all the Rakshasas of extraordinary strength to proceed instantly.” and develops toward “The great army of Rakshasas rushed towards the hostile army just as moths flew up towards flame.”

This passage begins with “Their Clubs and Asanis brandished by rubbing with their arms, the great army of Rakshasas appeared very bright.” and develops toward “In that fight the Rakshasas killed the Vanaras at the rate of ten to seven and the Vanaras struck down Rakshasas at the rate of ten to seven.”

chapters.name: Sarga 76
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While the fierce fight brought destruction of heroes in the ongoing combat, Angada eager to fight, assailed gallant Kampana.” and develops toward “Sonitha adorned with golden armlets taking hold of iron mace rushed behind Angada.”

This passage begins with “Then mighty Yupaksha, and Prajaghna for his part in fury assailed mighty Angada's chariot.” and develops toward “Seeing the pestle shaped sword fallen on the ground, mighty Prajaghna clenched his fist, which was as hard as the thunderbolt.”

This passage begins with “Sonitaksha of extraordinary energy hit at the forehead of highly valiant Angada. For a while Angada was unable to move.” and develops toward “Kumbha quickly restored the confidence of the troops. Then energetic Kumbha seeing the eminent heroes of Rakshasa army struck down, he found a…”

This passage begins with “Kumbha quickly restored the confidence of the troops. Then energetic Kumbha seeing the eminent heroes of Rakshasa army struck down, he found a favourable opportunity and drew up an…” and develops toward “Kumbha, a valiant hero pierced Angada continuously with several sharp and pointed arrows decked with gold.”

This passage begins with “Vali's son was not shaken by the strike, showered rain of rocks and trees on his (Kumbha) forehead.” and develops toward “Jambavan, Sushena, Vegadarsi and other heroic Vanaras, full of anger also rushed to assail Kumbhakarna's son.”

This passage begins with “Perceiving that great army of Vanara leaders are advancing, Kumbha obstructed them with a volley of arrows just like a mountain obstructing the path of torrent.” and develops toward “"You are equal to Prahlada, Bali, Indra, Kubera and Varuna in might. You alone have taken over like your father."”

This passage begins with “"Oh! mightyarmed, Kumbha! wielding pike in hand, you are a tamer of enemies, you alone are the one that gods are not able to overcome, a pre eminent one who has gained over senses, highly…” and develops toward “Then Sugriva lifted Kumbha and threw him into the ocean quickly, to show him the bottom.”

This passage begins with “By Kumbha falling into the ocean, it got swollen to the form of Vindhya and Mandara mountain and water flowed all over.” and develops toward “Struck in the chest by Sugriva's fist, Kumbha fell down and shone like the Sun god over powered by Rudra।।”

chapters.name: Sarga 77
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Seeing his brother struck down by Sugriva, Nikumbha gazed at him as if to burn him in wrath.” and develops toward “As soon as the iron club touched the broad chest (of Hanuman), it split into a hundred splinters, in the sky and crumbled.”

This passage begins with “The great Vanara did not move and remained steady like a mountain in an earthquake with the iron club touching him.” and develops toward “And thereafter as Nikumbha killed by windgod's son was crying aloud in the battlefield, a violent and fierce war took place between the highly…”

chapters.name: Sarga 78
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On hearing that Nikumbha and Kumbha had been killed, Ravana was very furious and burning in anger.” and develops toward “"Going there now, I will terminate the great Vanara army with these pikes just as drywood is burnt by fire."”

This passage begins with “On hearing Maka raksha's words all the mighty Rakshasas came with different kinds of weapons.” and develops toward “Seeing those portents incidentally and not taking into account those Rakshasas went where Rama and Lakshmana were.”

chapters.name: Sarga 79
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “On seeing Maka raksha all the Vanara chiefs returned, jumped, and stood ready enthusiastically to fight.” and develops toward “"O Evil minded Raghava! at that time, I did not see you in D and aka forest. My limbs are burning in anger."”

This passage begins with “"Rama! To my good fortune it became possible for me to see you here. Just as a hungry lion waits for an animal, I am waiting for you."” and develops toward “Then the sound released by the friction of the bowstring and the hand (of the two heroes) filled the sky like thunder of clouds.”

This passage begins with “Devas, Danavas, Ghand arvas, Kinnaras and huge serpents all of them stood in space to look at the wonderful evil deed.” and develops toward “Seeing the unwearied action of Raghava in destroying the pike, the beings in the sky praised him saying," well done, well done."”

This passage begins with “The night ranger Makaraksha seeing his pike destroyed lifting up his fist said this to Kakuthsa, "Wait, wait."” and develops toward “Seeing Maka raksha fall, those Rakshasas became scared of Rama's arrow and ran straight to Lanka.”

chapters.name: Sarga 80
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ravana, who was always victorious in warhearing that Maka raksha had been killed, became highly enraged and grinding his teeth in anger was engrossed in thinking. He commanded his son…” and develops toward “Drawn by horses, fitted with sharp mighty bows, the chariot was shining exquisitely and looked charming.”

This passage begins with “The chariot was glittering incarnated with gold covered with images offull moon, crescent moon inscriptions and decorated with images of antelopes.” and develops toward “Though the mighty Rama and Lakshmana were covering the sky with their arrows resembling the sun they could not touch Indrajith.”

This passage begins with “The glorious Indrajith obscured the sky by making it dark through smoke and fog and made the surrounding directions invisible.” and develops toward “Then, monkeys struck and killed, lifelessin hundreds fell on the ground.”

This passage begins with “Then Lakshmana also, enraged, spoke to his brother, saying," I will kill all the Rakshasas using the weapon of Brahma."” and develops toward “"If he enters into earth, heaven or the underground world or the subterranean world or remains hidden, my weapon will consume him, and he will fall…”

chapters.name: Sarga 81
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Knowing the highsouled Raghava's mind, Indrajith departed from there and entered the city.” and develops toward “Great Vanara, Hanuman, gazing at miserable, joyless, ascetic woman seated in the chariot, worried, why she is in the chariot. He spoke to the best of…”

This passage begins with “Great Vanara, Hanuman, gazing at miserable, joyless, ascetic woman seated in the chariot, worried, why she is in the chariot. He spoke to the best of Vanara leaders and rushed towards the…” and develops toward “Rakshasa army troops of mighty prowess, terribly angry, were advancing towards wood dwellers (Vanara troops) and were intercepted by the Vanara…”

This passage begins with “Indrajith shattered the army of monkeys with a thousand arrows. Hanuman the foremost Vanara replied to them as follows.” and develops toward “The sound of his roar was heard by the Vanaras waiting for war standing not far from there, which was difficult for Indrajith to access.”

chapters.name: Sarga 82

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The bulls among Vanaras heard the roar which was like thunder and looking at Indrajith fled in all directions.” and develops toward “As the rock fell on the battlefield the Rakshasa troops got alarmed and were violently crushed to death.”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Seeing enraged wise Rama's countenance, all beings on earth were shaken in fear.” and develops toward “In the meantime, the evil self Ravana who is called so, as he makes others cry, in intense anger making loud noise, stroking an unassailable weapon…”

This passage begins with “In the battlefield surrounded by Rakshasa heroes, enraged valiant Ravana took up the trident holding it in the middle.” and develops toward “The arrows released from Rama's bow fell on the great trident of Ravana and burnt like moths falling in fire.”

This passage begins with “Rama was overcome with fury on seeing his arrows being crushed to powder in the sky on touching the trident and reduced to ashes.” and develops toward “Pierced all over the limbs with arrows, blood flowing from the limbs, he (Rakshasa king) appeared like an Ashoka tree in the gathering.”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Ravana, who was proud of his skill in warfare, became very angry when he was pained by Kakuthsa's arrows.” and develops toward “"Having abducted Vaidehi living in the forest by force, when she was desperate and separated from me, do you think that you are a hero?”

This passage begins with “"Timid fellow! You having laid hands on another woman think you are a hero? You, who have done such an action, think that you are a hero?"” and develops toward “The strength, valiance, enthusiasm, and the strength of missiles of Rama, who was desirous of the destruction of the enemy, doubled.”

This passage begins with “All missiles appeared before Rama because of his richness in selfknowledge. In his excessive energy and joy the prince became more skillful with his hands.” and develops toward “Observing his (Ravana's) condition, the charioteer quietly, slowly withdrew his chariot from the battlefield.”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Enraged out of delusion, by force of destiny, Ravana's eyes turned red in anger and spoke to his charioteer.” and develops toward “Wishing your welfare, for your fame and to protect you with a friendly and pleasant feeling I did that which is good for you. You are unpleasant…”

This passage begins with “"O King! I who wish your welfare ought not to be blamed like an unworthy and pettyminded person by mistake."” and develops toward “Ravana was fully satisfied with the words of the charioteer. He praised the charioteer in many ways and spoke like this desiring to wage war.”

This passage begins with “"Quickly drive the chariot towards Raghava. As a policy, Ravana would never turn back on the battlefield without putting an end to the enemy."” and develops toward “Fully satisfied with Charioteer's words, Ravana bestowed him with an excellent auspicious ornament of the hand. sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then seeing Sri Rama standing and thinking of the war, duly prepared, Rishi Agastya came with Devatas to witness the war with Ravana and spoke as follows.” and develops toward “"You have the golden cosmic egg in the womb, you are cool, you are the purifier, the ruler of all, bringer of day, who Bears the fire of dissolution…”

This passage begins with “"You are the Lord of the sky, dispeller of darkness, the master of the three VedasRig, Yajur and Sama, the sender of rain, the friend of water and the courser of the path of Vindhya swiftly.” and develops toward “"He is all vedas, as well as all the sacrifices as also the fruits of all sacrifices. He is the supreme controller of all actions in all worlds.”

This passage begins with “"O Raghava! Anyone praying to him will surely not experience grief in straits, in difficulties, in the woods and in times of fear.” and develops toward “Sipping water three times and looking at Aditya, the valiant Rama getting purified, with devotion chanted the supreme with delight. This passage begins with “"You are a driver of great Indra's chariot. You are not required to be told. Wishing to fight with rapt attention, I will remind you now and then not instruct you", said Rama to Matali."” and develops toward “Meteors and huge fire dropped there indicating Ravana's end. Seeing them, Rakshasas were sad.”

This passage begins with “Wherever Ravana went, the earth shook. Many Rakshasas felt their hands clasped.” and develops toward “Sri Rama was very delighted over the appearance of good signs of his own victory and thought 'Ravana is destroyed by me'.”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then there ensued a fierce duel between Rama and Ravana that was fearsome to the whole world.” and develops toward “Glorious Rama aiming at the flag post of Ravana, released an arrow that tore the post and went into the ground.”

This passage begins with “Ravana's flag post fell down, struck by Rama. Mighty Ravana, observing his post being struck, became intolerant, and burning in anger, laughing, rained showers of arrows at Rama out of fury.” and develops toward “Seeing the arrows in the sky released by Rama, even Ravana released arrows up to the sky. With the arrows released by both of them, the space became…”

This passage begins with “The arrows released by both Rama and Ravana were without result. None failed to reach the target. None failed to complete the target. The arrows were colliding with one another and fell on…” and develops toward “Both became furious in that way and the best duel took place for a moment that was fierce and horipulating.”

This passage begins with “Rama was furious for tormenting Matali like that, but not for hurting him and in fury discharged a network of arrows on him (Ravana) and diverted him.” and develops toward “Gandharvas and Apsaras watching the combination of Rama and Ravana fighting an unimaginable war said that sky is its own analogue for sky, sea is its…”

This passage begins with “The Devas, Danavas, Yakshasas also the fiends Demon, Nagas and Rakshasas watched the great war that continued for seven days and nights.” and develops toward “The fight between Rama and Ravana continued all day and night, without stopping for a moment.”

This passage begins with “The great arrow, was a dispeller of fear of enemies to Ikshvaku kings, which takes away the glory of enemies, delighting to oneself, chanting vedas as per tradition, charging with mystic…” and develops toward “Seeing Ravana killed, the Vanaras holding trees went roaming on all sides, feeling triumphant.”

This passage begins with “"Rakshasas who followed as tails, humps and horns, Ravana the conquerors of enemies, like elephants in pride, animality as ears and eyes, the bull in the form of Rakshasa Ravana was killed…” and develops toward “"He maintained sacrificial fire and practised austerities. For Vanaras, Rakshasas and you too, this destruction in this war has come by divine grace."”

Sita, who is tolerant as the goddess of earth, prosperous, goddess of fortune, who is fond of her…”

"Convey to Vaidehi that me and Lakshman and also Sugriva are doing well, and that Ravana has been killed, O Best among the eloquent."

'Vibheeshana, who is very happy, is eager and favouring to see you."” and develops toward “"Now that the enemy is killed, Rama is happy, which means as if I have attained the rulership of gods."”

This passage begins with “Hearing the words of Mythili, daughter of Janaka, Hanuman spoke these auspicious words.” and develops toward “"These Rakshasas who have wronged, who are of dreadful appearance, I wish to hit them with fists, with feet spreading long arms, strike with blows of…”

This passage begins with “"Devi! you are the righteous wife of Rama and earned fame. Give me the right message to Rama in return. I will go."” and develops toward “Hanuman reached Raghava with extraordinary speed assuring Sita, who shone like Lakshmi, goddess of prosperity, and had spoken like that.”

This passage begins with “Noble Mythili (on hearing Vibheeshana), who looked upon her husband as God, said in reply, 'Alright'.” and develops toward “Seeing them excited to know and dispersing, Raghava stopped them out of kindness.”

This passage begins with “Exhibiting anger with his gaze at the uproar, Rama addressed the highly intelligent Vibheeshana resenting their action.” and develops toward “Sita, a gentle lady, who considered her husband as God only wondered with a mixed feeling of joy and affection and looked at the gentle face of Rama.”

You are endowed with beauty and having you in his own house for so long and seeing you, how Ravana could have resisted or detained you."”

Had you given me up immediately on receiving your message from Vanara I would have given up my life in the presence of Hanuman there itself."”

This passage begins with “"O Life! If you had a doubt this exertion to you is wasteful. This fruitless hard work was perhaps not required on your part nor your friends."” and develops toward “Mythili greeted the gods and brahmanas with joined palms and went near fire and again joined her palms in greeting and spoke as follows.”

This passage begins with “"If it is true that my heart has never slid from Raghava at all, may the fire god protect me always and so also the world witness."” and develops toward “All the Devas, Gandharvas and Danavas of the three worlds who witnessed her falling, extolled her as if goddess was falling from heaven into hell.”

This passage begins with “"You are the divine self, glorious Narayana himself, wielder of discus. You are having a single tusk and a divine boar, knower of past and present."” and develops toward “"Rama, all gods constitute your heart, Devi Saraswathi is your tongue, the gods created by Brahma are the hair on your limbs."”

This passage begins with “"When you close your eyelids, it becomes night and when you open it becomes day. Your perfection in action are the Vedas. This passage begins with “"Rama separated from you, even the suras and sages honouring me in heaven does not give me happiness. It is true and I promise."” and develops toward “O slayer of foes, you have accomplished a praiseworthy deed and won fame; now, established in your kingdom with your brothers, enjoy a long life.”
