sections.summary: This passage begins with “There upon Raghava who was quick in exhibiting his prowess started thinking with his friends looking at Ayodhya (from Pushpaka).” and develops toward “"Gentle Hanuman! tell him about my coming with Sugriva the king of Vanaras, and king of Rakshasas, Vibheeshana."”

This passage begins with “"Tell him that Rama , after conquering hosts of enemies, attaining unsurpassed fame, accomplished his purpose, has returned with mighty friends."” and develops toward “"Tomorrow that is the fifth lunar day, after spending the night at sage Bharadwaja's hermitage Raghava will see you."”

This passage begins with “Highly energetic Hanuman, was thrilled, overcome with happiness (that he was able to communicate to Guha) and unmindful of the fatigue, being endowed with speed and swift movement, went up.” and develops toward “"That Kakuthsa for whom you are worrying and living as he was at Dandaka forest with bark tree fabric, and matted hair sent me to tell you about his…”

This passage begins with “"O God! I will tell you some pleasant news. Give up that terrible grief. You will be reunited with your brother this moment."” and develops toward “"O Gentle Hanuman! You have come here with great compassion. Are you God or a human being? For the sweet tidings brought by you to me, I will bestow…”

chapters.name: Sarga 129
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “"I am hearing the good news of my Lord returning from the forest, who was living in the great forest for many years."” and develops toward “As they advanced through the dense forest, the mighty Viradha appeared before them, roaring with a tremendous noise.”

This passage begins with “"That Viradha uplifted his arms and bent his head low screamed like an elephant. The three dug up a pit and threw him into it."” and develops toward “"Rama, wielder of bow, chased the deer and followed it chasing and attacked with his arrow."”

This passage begins with “"When gentle Raghava went after the deer and Lakshmana also went from there, the ten headed Ravana entered the hermitage."” and develops toward “"Even before they met, they had a happy feeling at heart towards each other. Earlier Sugriva was banished by his enraged brother (Vali). On mutual…”

This passage begins with “"Even before they met, they had a happy feeling at heart towards each other. Earlier Sugriva was banished by his enraged brother (Vali). On mutual talk and discussion, they both developed…” and develops toward “"Rama made war efforts to win the war for the destruction of Lanka just as fire burns at the dissolution of the worlds and to conquer Ravana."”

This passage begins with “"Then Nala constructed the bridge, and the monkey heroes crossed the bridge."” and develops toward “"He reached the bank of Ganga and is staying at the hermitage of sage. Tomorrow at the time when Pushya star is in union with the moon you will be…”

chapters.name: Sarga 130
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Hearing the delightful news, Bharata of truthful prowess, the destroyer of enemies, was very happy and ordered Shatrughna.” and develops toward “Seated on top of thousands of well decorated elephants in rut, and on the best of horses, people went (to receive Rama). Female elephants had golden…”

This passage begins with “Chiefs on horses, champions in thousands went surrounded by thousands of foot soldiers carrying javelins, spears, and nooses.” and develops toward “And then Bharata having spoken that way, Hanuman understood the meaning of his words and spoke thus.”

This passage begins with “"By the grace of Bharadwaja, all the trees have fruits shedding sweet juice, and in bloom, the Vanaras are very delighted. The forest rangers are making terrific sounds. The tormentor of…” and develops toward “Then Bharata offered salutations with joined palms to Sri Rama seated in the peak of Pushpaka like saluting Sun from the mountain Meru.”

This passage begins with “Then that excellent Pushpaka which had pictures of swans on it came down to the ground with Rama's permission.” and develops toward “Rama reached his mother who was pale and emaciated in grief and offered salutations clasping her feet in obedience and was delighted in his mind.”

This passage begins with “Then he greeted Sumitra, and illustrious Kaikeyi also and then all the mothers and the priests who had come.” and develops toward “That aerial car duly permitted by Rama rose up to go to Kubera and set out in the northern direction and flew.”
That wonderful Pushpaka received from Rakshasa impelled by Rama's order speedily went to Kubera.

chapters.name: Sarga 131
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Bharata, the enhancer of Kaikeyi's joy, of unfailing valour, with folded palms on head submitted to his elder brother.” and develops toward “Hearing Bharata's words, the conqueror of enemies, Rama said 'Be it so'. Then Bharata took the auspicious seat.”

This passage begins with “Then at the command of Shatrughna's words, experts with hand work (barbers) who had soft skills quickly surrounded Raghava.” and develops toward “Ashoka, Vijaya, Siddhartha, collected together and started consulting with each other for the growth of Rama and the City.”

This passage begins with “For the success of the great soul Rama's consecration, whatever was auspicious, all that ought to be done as he deserves.” and develops toward “Surrounded by ministers, brahmins and common people, glorious Rama shone brightly like the moon surrounded by stars.”

This passage begins with “He went forward surrounded by bands, trumpets, wooden cymbals and swastikas and very happily singing auspicious festive songs.” and develops toward “Highly brilliant Sugriva said to Bharata, "Let envoys be sent for the consecration of Rama."”

This passage begins with “Sugriva instantly gave four golden pots decorated well to the four Vanara leaders.” and develops toward “Eight sages, Vasishta, Vama Deva, Jabali, Kasyapa, Katyayana, Suyajna, Gautama and Vijaya consecrated Sri Rama, the tiger among men, with pure…”

This passage begins with “Eight sages, Vasishta, Vama Deva, Jabali, Kasyapa, Katyayana, Suyajna, Gautama and Vijaya consecrated Sri Rama, the tiger among men, with pure fragrant waters as the Vasus consecrated the…” and develops toward “The Gandharvas from among the gods sang and Apsara groups danced for Rama's consecration.”

This passage begins with “At the time of celebration of Rama's coronation, earth became green with crops, trees bore fruits and filled with flowers which spread fragrance.” and develops toward “Then Sri Rama, the tormentor of enemies, the ruler of the earth looking at Mainda, Dwivida and Neela presented articles according to their desires.…”

This passage begins with “Then Sri Rama, the tormentor of enemies, the ruler of the earth looking at Mainda, Dwivida and Neela presented articles according to their desires. For the aged among the Vanaras and the…” and develops toward “Raghava in the ten thousand years of his rule performed hundred horse sacrifices with excellent horses and gave donations liberally.”

This passage begins with “Sri Rama whose arms reached unto knees, endowed with broadchest and stupendous glory, ruled this earth with Lakshmana as his follower.” and develops toward “He who reads, listens to this first epic, a noble text composed by Valmiki will be blessed, attain fame, enjoy long life and victory in this world…”

This passage begins with “On hearing about Rama's consecration, in this world, one who is seeking sons will get sons, one who is desirous of wealth will attain wealth, kings will overcome enemies and conquer earth.” and develops toward “Rama is said to be ever pleased with him who listens or reads the Ramayana daily. Foremost of Raghus, the mighty armed Rama is eternal Lord Vishnu…”

This passage begins with “This way Ramayana is the ancient narration. Read it daily with trust and devotion. You be auspicious and let the strength of Vishnu grow in you.” and develops toward “Listening to this great auspicious epic will result in the growth of the family, excellent women, augmentation of wealth and grains and comfort on…”

chapters.name: Sarga 1
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “After Rama had attained his kingdom and the destruction of the Rakshasas was accomplished, all the sages came to congratulate Raghava.” and develops toward “Having heard that those sages, radiant like the morning sun, had arrived, Rama then replied to the doorkeeper, instructing him to admit them…”

This passage begins with “Having seen those arrived sages, Rama rose to greet them with folded hands, honored them with water for their feet and other offerings, and respectfully presented a cow.” and develops toward “O Rama, it is by good fortune that you have killed Kumbha and Nikumbha, the two sons of Kumbhakarna who were of terrible appearance, in battle.”

This passage begins with “This shloka continues the list of formidable demons, including Yuddhonmatta and Matta who were like Kala and Yama, along with Yajnakopa and the powerful demon named Dhumraksha, whose defeat…” and develops toward “This shloka lists several powerful demons: Mahodara, Prahasta, Virupaksha, Matta, Unmatta, Devantaka, and Narantaka, all of whom are formidable.”

This passage begins with “The speaker questions why they are praising Indrajit, having overlooked other greatly valorous warriors.” and develops toward “Rama further inquires how that Rakshasa (Ravana) became superior to his father, how he conquered Indra in a great battle, and how he obtained boons,…”

chapters.name: Sarga 2
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard those words of the great-souled Rama, the greatly effulgent sage Agastya then spoke this to Rama.” and develops toward “Then, the greatly effulgent great sage, being angered, declared, "Whoever comes into my sight will conceive a child."”

This passage begins with “But all of them, having heard the words of that great soul, were frightened by the brahmin's curse and no longer approached that place.” and develops toward “King Trasabindu, having understood the curse from the great self-realized sage, took his daughter and went to Pulastya, to whom he spoke these words.”

This passage begins with “O revered great sage, please accept this daughter of mine, adorned with her own virtues, as an offering that she herself is ready to make.” and develops toward “Sage Vishrava became learned in scriptures, impartial, devoted to vows and observances, and indeed, like his father, engaged in severe austerities.…”

chapters.name: Sarga 3
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, Vishrava, the son of Pulastya and foremost among sages, soon became engaged in penance just like his father.” and develops toward “At the completion of each thousand years, he adopted various methods of austerity, such as subsisting only on water, then only on air, and then…”

This passage begins with “Thus, those thousands of years passed as if they were a single year, and then the greatly effulgent Brahma, pleased, came with Indra and the hosts of gods.” and develops toward “Ravana further specified that the dwelling place should be one where no living being would suffer any harm, and upon hearing this request from his…”

This passage begins with “The righteous and excellent one spoke, saying, "Listen! On the shore of the southern ocean, there is a mountain named Trikuta."” and develops toward “Praised by hosts of gods and Gandharvas, and with his abode adorned by the dances of Apsaras, that lord of wealth, Kubera, approached his father,…”

chapters.name: Sarga 4
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard the words spoken by Agastya, Rama was filled with astonishment, wondering how the Rakshasas could have originated in Lanka in ancient times.” and develops toward “Among those beings, some said, "We will protect," while others said, "We will eat"; then, having heard these responses from the hungry and the…”

This passage begins with “To those who said, "We will protect," he declared, "You shall be Rakshasas," and to those who said, "We will eat," he declared, "You shall indeed be Yakshas."” and develops toward “Then, that female demon, having gone to the Mandara mountain, gave birth to an offspring shining like a thundercloud, just as Ganga gave birth to the…”

This passage begins with “Having abandoned her offspring, she, desiring union with Vidyutkesha, then enjoyed herself with her husband, completely forgetting her own son.” and develops toward “Then, Sukesha, proud of the boon and having obtained glory from the presence of Lord Shiva, roamed everywhere, great and of great intellect, just as…”

chapters.name: Sarga 5
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having seen Sukesha, the righteous demon who had obtained a boon, a Gandharva named Gramani, whose splendor was like that of Vishvavasu, (approached him).” and develops toward “Then, the powerful, four-faced Brahma, seated on his excellent aerial chariot, addressed the sons of Sukesha, declaring, "I am here to grant boons."”

This passage begins with “Realizing that Brahma, the bestower of boons, was present, surrounded by Indra and hosts of gods, all of them spoke with folded hands, trembling like trees.” and develops toward “On the middle peak of that mountain, which resembles a cloud, is difficult for even birds to reach, and is carved on all four sides as if by a chisel.”

This passage begins with “This place is thirty yojanas wide and a hundred yojanas long, surrounded by golden walls and adorned with golden archways.” and develops toward “He begot in her the following offspring: Vajramushti, Virupaksha, and Durmukha, who were Rākṣasas.”

This passage begins with “O Rama, from Sundari were born sons named Suptaghna, Yajñakopa, Matta, and Unmatta, and also a beautiful daughter named Anala.” and develops toward “These beings wander the world like the wind, are difficult to approach, possess valor equal to death in battles, are exceedingly proud due to the…”

chapters.name: Sarga 6
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Tormented by them, the gods and the sages whose wealth was penance, distressed by fear, went to Maheśvara, the god of gods, for refuge.” and develops toward “Then, indeed, those afflicted by the fear of night-wanderers (Rakshasas) acclaimed Lord Shiva with cries of victory and approached Lord Vishnu.”

This passage begins with “Having bowed down and greatly honored the god bearing the conch and discus, they spoke agitatedly concerning the sons of Sukesha.” and develops toward “It is said that the gods and sages, having met Shankara, spoke these words, desiring our destruction.”

This passage begins with “O god, the sons of Sukesha, who are exceedingly arrogant due to the power of boons and possess terrible forms, harass us at every step.” and develops toward “All these Asuras and Danavas, though endowed with great strength and power, are never heard to have remained undefeated once they entered battle.”

This passage begins with “All of them had performed hundreds of sacrifices, were skilled in various illusions, proficient in all weapons, and struck terror into their enemies.” and develops toward “They also advanced with crocodiles, tortoises, fish, birds resembling Garuḍa, lions, tigers, boars, antelopes, and yaks.”

This passage begins with “All the Rākṣasas, proud of their strength and enemies of the gods, abandoned Lanka and set out for the world of the gods to fight.” and develops toward “All those night-wandering demons indeed took refuge in Malyavan, just as gods resort to Brahma, and as one would take refuge in an unmoving mountain.”

This passage begins with “That army of the demon lords, resounding like the crashing of great clouds, went to the world of gods, which was under Mali's sway, with a desire for victory.” and develops toward “Then, the night-wandering Rakshasas, surrounding Madhava, pierced him by thousands with their excellent weapons, which were smeared with blood and…”

chapters.name: Sarga 7
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Those roaring demon-clouds, like actual clouds showering rain upon a mountain, indeed showered a rain of arrows upon the mountain-like Narayana (Vishnu/Rama).” and develops toward “The horses were unable to stand still, the elephants lost their ichor and strength, and warriors, weakened by the conch's sound, fell from their…”

This passage begins with “Arrows released from the Śārṅga bow, with points like thunderbolts, tore through those Rākṣasas and, with their well-feathered shafts, penetrated the earth.” and develops toward “The routed rakshasa army, terrified by Narayana's arrows and greatly bewildered by the sound of his conch, fled towards Lanka.”

This passage begins with “When the rakshasa army was routed and struck by Narayana's arrows, Sumali, in battle, obstructed Hari with a shower of arrows.” and develops toward “The wielder of the conch, discus, and mace turned Mali away and then struck down Mali's crown, banner, bow, and horses.”

This passage begins with “But Mali, the foremost among night-roaming demons, having lost his chariot, took up his mace and, mace in hand, sprang forward like a lion from a mountain peak.” and develops toward “The Rakshasas had their lotus-like faces severed by the discus, their chests crushed by maces, their necks destroyed by ploughs, and their heads…”

This passage begins with “Some Rakshasas were cut down by swords, others were wounded by arrows, and all of them swiftly fell from the sky into the ocean's waters.” and develops toward “The falling Rakshasas, who resembled dark blue clouds and wore dangling jewel necklaces and earrings, were continuously seen falling like blue…”

chapters.name: Sarga 8
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When his army was being struck from behind by Padmanabha, Malvan then turned back, like the ocean reaching its shore.” and develops toward “That spear, released from Govinda's hand, flew towards the demon as if thrown by Skanda, desiring to strike, just as Indra's thunderbolt strikes the…”

This passage begins with “That spear fell upon the broad chest of the king of demons, which was shining with many necklaces, just as a thunderbolt strikes a mountain peak.” and develops toward “The Rākṣasas of the Pulastya lineage whom you killed, O Rama, include Sumālī, Mālyavān, Mālī, and their other leaders.”

This passage begins with “O highly fortunate one, all of them were more powerful than Rāvaṇa.” and develops toward “For a long time, that powerful Rākṣasa Sumālī, tormented by fear of Viṣṇu, wandered in Rasātala accompanied by his sons and grandsons, while the lord…”

chapters.name: Sarga 9
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, after some time, a demon named Sumali wandered throughout the entire mortal world, having come from Rasatala.” and develops toward “O daughter, without a doubt, your sons will be like this lord of wealth (Kubera), equal to the sun in splendor.”

This passage begins with “Having heard that instruction, the maiden, out of respect for her father, approached the place where Vishravas was performing penance and stood there.” and develops toward “O venerable one, O speaker of Brahman, I do not desire such extremely ill-behaved sons from you; please show me your grace.”

This passage begins with “But Vishrava, the foremost among sages, thus spoken to by the maiden, spoke again to Kaikasi, just as the full moon speaks to Rohini.” and develops toward “Then, drums of the gods sounded in the sky, and a voice from the heavens exclaimed, "Excellent! Excellent!"”

This passage begins with “Then, those two, Kumbhakarna and Dashagriva, grew up in that great forest, becoming exceedingly powerful and a cause of distress to the world.” and develops toward “That demon of fierce prowess, along with his younger brothers, then performed incomparable penance there, and he also pleased the lord Brahma, who,…”

chapters.name: Sarga 10
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then Rama asked the sage, "O Brahmana, how did your mighty brothers perform penance in the forest, and what kind of penance was it?"” and develops toward “Then, in the tenth thousand-year period, as Dashagriva (Ravana) was about to cut off his tenth head, Lord Brahma arrived there.”

This passage begins with “The grandfather (Brahma), greatly pleased, appeared there with the gods and addressed Dashagriva (Ravana), saying, "O Dashagriva, I am pleased with you."” and develops toward “O Rakshasa, those (heads) will be yours again just as before, and here I grant you, O gentle one, another formidable boon.”

This passage begins with “O Rakshasa, whatever form you desire in your mind will be yours according to your will, without a doubt, by virtue of my boon.” and develops toward “Having thus spoken, when Prajapati was about to grant a boon to Kumbhakarna, all the gods, with folded hands, spoke to him.”

This passage begins with “"A boon should not be granted by you to Kumbhakarna at this time, for you indeed know how this evil-minded one terrorizes the worlds."” and develops toward “The evil-minded Kumbhakarna, now distressed, pondered, "What kind of words have I uttered from my mouth today?"”

This passage begins with “I believe I was deluded by the gods who had come then; thus, all the brothers, having obtained boons and possessing blazing splendor, went to the Shlesmataka forest and resided there…” and develops toward “I believe I was deluded by the gods who had come then; thus, all the brothers, having obtained boons and possessing blazing splendor, went to the…”

chapters.name: Sarga 11
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Sumali, having learned that these night-wanderers had obtained boons, rose up from Rasatala with his followers, abandoning all fear.” and develops toward “Then, after some time, while Ravana was residing there, the night-wanderer (Prahasta) addressed Ravana, who had spoken such words.”

This passage begins with “Prahasta then spoke these humble and reasonable words.” and develops toward “"O King, O gentle one, O sinless one, this city of Lanka, belonging to the great-souled Rakshasas, was established by you, but this is not proper for…”

This passage begins with “O unequalled in valor, if you would indeed give it to us today, my satisfaction would be complete, and my duty would also be fulfilled.” and develops toward “Kubera further stated, "Let the city of Lanka, formerly inhabited by Rakshasas, be given to him; please tell me, O revered one, what I should do in…”

This passage begins with “Thus addressed, the Brahmarshi Vishrava, the foremost among sages, spoke to Kubera, who stood with folded hands, saying, "Listen, O son, to my words."” and develops toward “That city of Lanka is now empty, as Kubera has abandoned it and departed; therefore, enter that city with us and govern according to your own duty.”

This passage begins with “Thus spoken to by Prahasta, the mighty Dashagriva entered the city of Lanka with his brothers, army, and followers.” and develops toward “But then Kubera, out of respect for his father's words, established a city on the moon-bright mountain (Kailasa), adorned with well-decorated…”

chapters.name: Sarga 12
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The lord of Rakshasas, Ravana, having been crowned and accompanied by his two brothers, then thought about giving his Rakshasi sister in marriage.” and develops toward “Indeed, a daughter always keeps two families in a state of uncertainty; also, two sons were born to me from this wife.”

This passage begins with “O dear one, Mayavi was the first, and Dundubhi came after him; thus, everything has been truthfully told to you who asked.” and develops toward “Vibhishana obtained as his wife Sarama, the righteous daughter of the great-souled Gandharva king Shailusha.”

This passage begins with “She was born on the bank of Lake Manasa, and at that time, during the rainy season, Lake Manasa was overflowing.” and develops toward “Ravana's son was causing great joy to his mother and father. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये श्रीमदुत्तरकाण्डे द्वादशः सर्गः ।। 12 ।।…”

chapters.name: Sarga 13
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, after some time, an intense, embodied sleep, sent forth by the Lord of the worlds, arose there for Kumbhakarna.” and develops toward “Then, Kubera, wishing to demonstrate brotherly affection and for Daśagrīva's welfare, sent a messenger to Lanka.”

This passage begins with “Having arrived in the city of Lanka, the messenger met Vibhīṣaṇa, who honored him properly and inquired about the purpose of his visit.” and develops toward “By the divine power of the goddess, my left eye was burnt, and it became yellowish-brown, like a flame obscured by dust.”

This passage begins with “Then, I went to another expansive slope of that mountain and silently observed a great vow for eight hundred years.” and develops toward “This protector of wealth (Kubera) is not speaking what is beneficial for me; rather, this fool is indeed making me hear about his friendship with…”

This passage begins with “O messenger, what you have spoken is not to be forgiven by me, though I have endured his (Kubera's) actions for so long.” and develops toward “Having thus performed auspicious rites and mounted his chariot, Ravana, desirous of conquering the three worlds, went to where Kubera, the lord of…”

chapters.name: Sarga 14
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, accompanied by six powerful ministers, including Mahodara, Prahasta, Maricha, Shuka, and Sarana, he proceeded.” and develops toward “There, Dashanana, being struck down as if breathless, was obstructed by the continuous onslaught of weapons, much like clouds pouring down streams of…”

This passage begins with “Even though struck by the weapons of the Yakshas, he felt no pain, just like a mountain remains unaffected when drenched by hundreds of streams of rain from clouds.” and develops toward “Then, he entered the archway, which was the boundary of the doorkeepers, adorned with gold and sprinkled with sapphire dust.”

This passage begins with “O King, the doorkeeper known as Suryabhanu obstructed that Dashagriva, the night-wanderer, as he was entering.” and develops toward “Then, all of them fled, having witnessed the Rakshasa's prowess, and, afflicted by fear, exhausted, with pale faces, and having abandoned their…”

chapters.name: Sarga 15
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, the lord of wealth, having seen those thousands of terrified chief Yakshas, spoke to the great Yaksha Manichara.” and develops toward “Having seen Dhūmrākṣa struck, fallen, and drenched in blood, Daśānana rushed towards Maṇibhadra in battle.”

This passage begins with “The foremost among Yakshas, Maṇibhadra, struck that angry Daśānana, who was rushing towards him, with three spears.” and develops toward “Indeed, the fruit of sin is sorrow, which one must experience oneself in this world; therefore, only a fool would commit sin for the purpose of…”

This passage begins with “Indeed, the intellect of an evil-minded person does not arise by their own will, for destiny directs everything, and one struck by fate is destroyed, ultimately receiving the fruit…” and develops toward “The lord of wealth, Kubera, fell down like an Ashoka tree whose roots had been cut.”

This passage begins with “Then, surrounded by the treasures like Padma and others, Kubera was revived by them and brought to the Nandana forest.” and develops toward “Having achieved that great victory by his own prowess, the mighty Rakshasa, adorned with a spotless crown and necklace, shone brightly while seated…”

chapters.name: Sarga 16
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “O Rama, having conquered his brother Kubera, the lord of Rakshasas then went to that great thicket of reeds, the birthplace of Kartikeya.” and develops toward “With eyes reddened by rage, he descended from the Pushpaka chariot, exclaiming, "Who is this Shankara?" and approached the base of the mountain.”

This passage begins with “There, not far from Shiva, he saw Nandi standing, firmly holding a shining trident, appearing like a second Shankara himself.” and develops toward “By what power does Shiva constantly play like a king, not recognizing the imminent danger that should be known?”

This passage begins with “Having thus spoken, O Rama, (Ravana) spread his arms on the mountain and quickly began to lift it, causing that mountain to tremble.” and develops toward “Then, O Rama, the pleased Lord Mahadeva, situated on the mountain peak, released Ravana's arms and spoke to the ten-headed one.”

This passage begins with “O ten-headed one, I am pleased with your valor and prowess, demonstrated by the very terrible cry you released while being crushed by the mountain.” and develops toward “Then, O Rama, Ravana roamed about on the earth's surface, harassing very mighty Kshatriyas here and there.”

This passage begins with “Some powerful and brave Kshatriyas, unyielding in battle, who disobeyed his command, perished along with their retinue.” and develops toward “Others, knowing the Rakshasa to be unconquerable and being considered wise, declared to the strength-proud Rakshasa, "We are conquered.". इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 17
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, O King, the mighty-armed Ravana, wandering on the earth, reached the Himalayan forest and roamed there.” and develops toward “My father indeed desired Vishnu, the lord of gods and the lord of the three worlds, to be his son-in-law, and therefore he would not give me to…”

This passage begins with “But upon hearing that my father desired to give me to Vishnu, the king of Daityas named Dambhu, who was proud of his strength, became enraged.” and develops toward “Ravana asks Vedavati who this Vishnu is, whom she speaks of, and what he possesses in terms of valor, penance, enjoyment, and strength.”

This passage begins with “Ravana declares to Vedavati that the one she desires is not equal to him.” and develops toward “She emerged again from a lotus, radiant like a lotus, and from that lotus, she was again taken by that demon as before.”

This passage begins with “Having seized the maiden, who shone like the heart of a lotus, the demon went to his own house, and Ravana then showed her to his minister.” and develops toward “She was born in the family of the great-souled Janaka, the king of Mithila, and because she emerged from the furrow, she is known by humans as Sita.…”

chapters.name: Sarga 18
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “After Vedavati entered the fire, Ravana ascended his Pushpaka chariot and wandered across the earth.” and develops toward “Marutta continues, "An act associated with unrighteousness is not praiseworthy among people; having performed such a wicked deed, you boast about…”

This passage begins with “Marutta questions, "How did you, who formerly practiced only righteousness, obtain a boon? Indeed, I have never heard you speak such words as you now utter."” and develops toward “Indra, the lord of gods, thus granted a boon to the peacock, saying that the thousand eyes would appear on its tail-feathers, and it would experience…”

This passage begins with “O king of men, it is said that formerly the feathers of peacocks were blue, and having obtained a boon from the lord of gods, all peacocks became (as they are now, with the characteristic…” and develops toward “After granting boons to them during that sacrificial festival, the gods, along with the king, returned to their respective abodes. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 19
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “After conquering Marutta, Daśānana, the lord of Rākṣasas, being desirous of battle, then proceeded to the cities of other kings.” and develops toward “O best among men, that army consisted of ten thousand elephants, a hundred thousand horses, many thousands of chariots, and foot soldiers.”

This passage begins with “O expert in warfare, that army, covering the earth with its foot soldiers and chariots, marched out for battle, and then a very great battle commenced.” and develops toward “Laughing loudly, the Rakshasa (Ravana) then said to that Ikshvaku king, "What result have you now obtained by fighting against me?"”

This passage begins with “O king of men, there is no one in the three worlds who can challenge me; I suspect you are too engrossed in pleasures to know my strength.” and develops toward “Then, O king of kings, that king went to the abode of heaven, and when that king had ascended to heaven, the demon departed. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 20
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, the lord of demons, terrifying mortals on earth, met Narada, the foremost among sages, in that dense region.” and develops toward “O mighty-armed lord of Rakshasas, just behold the human being, who is so foolish and whose strange nature or destiny is not truly known.”

This passage begins with “At times, joyful people engage in music and dance, while at other times, distressed individuals weep with faces and eyes streaming with tears.” and develops toward “Indeed, O revered one, I, desirous of battle, have vowed in my anger, "I shall conquer the four guardians of the world, O lord."”

This passage begins with “Therefore, I have now indeed set out towards the city of the king of the dead, and I shall unite with death the one who causes distress to living beings.” and develops toward “He himself wished to witness the conflict between Yama and the Rakshasa. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये श्रीमदुत्तरकाण्डे विंशः सर्गः…”

chapters.name: Sarga 21
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having thus pondered, Narada, moving swiftly, went towards Yama's abode to narrate the events as they had transpired.” and develops toward “He saw embodied beings being killed and tormented, crying out with a great cry and intent on intense wailing.”

This passage begins with “He saw them being devoured by worms and by fierce dogs.” and develops toward “When the dead were being released by the mighty Rakshasa, the guardians of the dead became very enraged and rushed towards the lord of Rakshasas.”

This passage begins with “Then, a great tumultuous roar arose from all directions, made by the brave warriors of Yama who were charging forward.” and develops toward “That extremely terrible shower of trees, stones, and weapons fell upon Yama's armies on the surface of the earth.”

This passage begins with “But having pierced through all those weapons and repelled that missile, Yama's soldiers, in hundreds of thousands, struck that single terrible demon.” and develops toward “Then, the demon of terrible prowess, Ravana, along with his attendants, let out a very great roar, as if shaking the earth. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 22
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard that great roar, Lord Yama considered the enemy victorious and his own army destroyed.” and develops toward “But Ravana, upon seeing that chariot which struck terror into the world, was neither disturbed nor did fear enter his heart.”

This passage begins with “Yama, greatly enraged, approached Ravana and hurled spears and javelins, piercing Ravana's vital spots.” and develops toward “Then, Death, even more enraged, spoke to Yama, saying, "Release me in battle so that I may kill this sinful demon, for by nature, this demon should…”

This passage begins with “The glorious Hiranyakashipu, Namuchi, Shambara, Visandhi, Dhumaketu, and Bali Vairochana were among them.” and develops toward “That great weapon, surrounded by flames as if burning the demon, trembled when touched by the powerful Yama.”

This passage begins with “Then all those on the battlefield, terrified by that, fled, and all the gods were agitated upon seeing Yama with his mace raised.” and develops toward “What indeed can I do now, having come to battle, if this one (Ravana) cannot be killed by me because he is protected by a boon?”

This passage begins with “Having said, "Therefore, I disappear from the sight of this demon," he vanished right there with his chariot and horses.” and develops toward “And Vaivasvata (Yama), delighted, went to heaven along with the gods led by Brahma, as did the great sage Narada. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये…”

chapters.name: Sarga 23
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, having conquered Yama, the chief among the gods, Ravana, who boasted of his prowess in battle, indeed saw his own companions.” and develops toward “Indeed, this Ravana cannot be conquered in battle by gods and asuras, nor can you (Nivatakavachas) be led to destruction even by gods and Danavas.”

This passage begins with “Friendship with you Rakshasas is agreeable, and there is no doubt that all interests of friends are undivided.” and develops toward “Ravana, having circumambulated the exceedingly wonderful Surabhi, whom people in the world call by that name, then proceeded.”

This passage begins with “He then entered a very dreadful place, guarded by various forces, and there he saw Varuna's excellent abode, which was filled with hundreds of streams, resembled an autumn cloud, and was…” and develops toward “Then, the enraged hero Mahodara, seeing his king humiliated, abandoned all fear of death and, desirous of battle, looked towards the enemy.”

This passage begins with “In that fierce battle, those who moved at will and resembled the wind were indeed struck by Mahodara with his mace and fell to the earth.” and develops toward “Then, the Rakshasa Ravana, having uttered great roars, struck down the followers of Varuna (Devas) with showers of various weapons, just like a cloud…”

This passage begins with “Then, all of them, defeated and fallen to the ground, were quickly brought back into their homes by their own men from the battlefield.” and develops toward “Having arrived by a certain path, the demon Ravana returned by that very path, flying through the sky towards Lanka. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 24
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While returning, the exceedingly wicked Ravana, greatly delighted, abducted maidens of kings, sages, gods, and gandharvas along his path.” and develops toward “Some, greatly distressed, wondered if Rāvaṇa would kill them, and thus, remembering their mothers, fathers, husbands, and brothers, all the women…”

This passage begins with “They lamented, "How will my son fare without me? How will my mother and brother, immersed in an ocean of sorrow, endure?"” and develops toward “In the meantime, that terrible Rākṣasī, Rāvaṇa's sister, who could assume any form at will, suddenly fell on the ground.”

This passage begins with “Ravana, having raised his sister up and consoled her, asked her what she quickly wished to tell him.” and develops toward “Your brother will be the lord of fourteen thousand very powerful Rakshasas, commanding them in expeditions and bestowing gifts.”

This passage begins with “There, your cousin and brother Khara, the lordly night-wanderer, will always execute your commands.” and develops toward “Khara established a kingdom free from enemies there, and Shurpanakha also resided in the Dandaka forest. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये…”

chapters.name: Sarga 25
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having given that terrible forest to Khara and having consoled his sister, Dashagriva became delighted and more at ease.” and develops toward “O King, he also obtained two inexhaustible quivers with arrows, a bow that is very difficult to conquer, and a powerful weapon that destroys enemies…”

This passage begins with “Having obtained all these boons, O Dashanana, your son, now that the sacrifice is concluded, and I myself, stand here eager to behold you.” and develops toward “She, the daughter born of Anala, is our mother's sister's daughter, and thus by dharma, she is indeed our sister.”

This passage begins with “O King, she was abducted by the powerful demon Madhu while my son was engaged in a sacrifice and I was dwelling in penance underwater.” and develops toward “All the Rakshasas set forth, mounted on donkeys, camels, splendid horses, porpoises, and great serpents, completely filling the sky.”

This passage begins with “Hundreds of Daityas, who had long-standing enmity with the gods, saw Ravana going and indeed followed him from behind.” and develops toward “Therefore, O Rakshasa, you should go with your relatives to help him, for it is proper to provide assistance to an affectionate one who seeks it.”

This passage begins with “Having heard her words, Madhu replied, "So be it."” and develops toward “Then, the lord of Rakshasas, Ravana, who resembled Mahendra, reached Mount Kailasa, the abode of Kubera, and encamped his army there.”

chapters.name: Sarga 26
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “There, the powerful Ravana, along with his army, chose to reside as the sun was setting.” and develops toward “The night had set in, enhanced by the melodious music, the profusion of flowers, the cool mountain breeze, and the rising of the moon.”

This passage begins with “Ravana, despite his great might, had fallen under the sway of desire and repeatedly sighed deeply as he gazed at the moon.” and develops toward “Who today will ascend your broad hips, which resemble a golden disc, are adorned with golden chains, and are like heaven itself?”

This passage begins with “Who is that man superior to me today, be it Indra, Vishnu, or the Ashvins, whom you, O timid one, would go to, leaving me behind, for that would not be proper?” and develops toward “Thus, my affection is indeed for him alone, and not for anyone else.”

This passage begins with “O King, destroyer of enemies, by that truth, you ought to release me.” and develops toward “O god, this Dashagriva arrived here to go to heaven, and with the help of his army, he spent this night.”

This passage begins with “O destroyer of enemies, I was seen by him while coming to your presence, and I was seized and asked by that Rakshasa, "Whose are you?"” and develops toward “But having heard that terrifying, hair-raising curse, Dashagriva no longer desired sexual intimacy with unwilling women.”

This passage begins with “All the chaste women who had been abducted by him attained great joy upon hearing that delightful curse uttered by Nalakubara. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये…” and develops toward “All the chaste women who had been abducted by him attained great joy upon hearing that delightful curse uttered by Nalakubara. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 27
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “After crossing the Kailasa mountain, that greatly powerful ten-necked demon Ravana reached the world of Indra.” and develops toward “Indra further states that all these three worlds, with their moving and non-moving beings, were created by the Lord, and at the end of an age, all…”

This passage begins with “Indra implores the Lord of gods to reveal truly how He, even with the aid of His discus, intends to fight Ravana.” and develops toward “Those mighty heroes, engaged in battle, looked at each other and advanced joyfully, intent only on the fight.”

This passage begins with “Then, seeing that immense and inexhaustible great army at the forefront of the battle, agitation arose among the armies of the gods.” and develops toward “Then a battle took place between the gods and the demons, who were enraged and renowned for never retreating from combat.”

This passage begins with “Then all those demons, using dreadful and various weapons, struck down the gods arrayed in battle by hundreds of thousands.” and develops toward “That mace, resembling a falling meteor, shone as it descended upon him, like a great thunderbolt released by Indra roaring down upon a mountain.”

This passage begins with “Then, neither his bone, nor head, nor flesh was seen, as the mace had reduced the slain Sumali to ashes on the battlefield.” and develops toward “Having seen Sumali killed in battle, all those Rakshasas fled together from all sides, crying out to each other, and being driven away by Vasu, they…”

chapters.name: Sarga 28
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having seen Sumalin killed and reduced to ashes by Vasu, and observing his own army fleeing and tormented by the gods, (Meghanada reacted).” and develops toward “Indeed, that powerful son of Ravana, filled with rage and with wide-open eyes, showered Indra's son Jayanta with a volley of arrows.”

This passage begins with “Then, greatly enraged, Ravana's son hurled thousands of various sharp-edged weapons upon the armies of the gods.” and develops toward “That divine, very formidable, and indeed ready great chariot, of immense speed, was then brought forth by Matali.”

This passage begins with “Then, very powerful clouds, bearing lightning and swift as the wind, roared with mighty sounds in front of that chariot.” and develops toward “Then, the night-wanderer Kumbhakarna, having encountered and fought with the extremely terrible Rudras in battle, was continuously wounded by their…”

This passage begins with “Kumbhakarna, with his body covered in weapons and oozing blood, appeared like a rain-bearing cloud accompanied by the roar of thunder and lightning.” and develops toward “And similarly, the mighty-armed Dashagriva stood firm and covered Indra with showers of arrows released from his bow.”

This passage begins with “As those two fought with showers of arrows all around, nothing could be discerned then, for everything was indeed covered by darkness. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये…” and develops toward “As those two fought with showers of arrows all around, nothing could be discerned then, for everything was indeed covered by darkness. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 29
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, with the darkness having arisen, all those gods and demons, intoxicated with their strength, fought, slaying each other.” and develops toward “Then, Indra, the lord of gods, having understood his (Ravana's) determination, spoke these words to the gods who were also present in the…”

This passage begins with “O gods, listen to my words, which seem good to me: this demon Dashagriva should be captured alive.” and develops toward “Indrajit, disregarding all other gods, rushed only towards Indra, but the greatly effulgent Mahendra could not see the enemy's son.”

This passage begins with “Even though Indrajit was without armor and being struck there by the exceedingly valiant gods, he did not suffer any harm.” and develops toward “"Indeed, this one who is the lord of the army of gods and of the three worlds has been captured by divine power, and thus the gods have been rendered…”

This passage begins with “Enjoy the three worlds to your heart's content, having subdued the enemy with your might; why exert yourself in vain here, for today's battle is indeed fruitless.” and develops toward “Then, the valiant son of Ravana, Indrajit, having captured the lord of the gods along with his vehicles and surrounded by his army, returned to his…”

chapters.name: Sarga 30
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When the exceedingly powerful Mahendra was indeed conquered by Ravana's son, the gods then went to Lanka, making Prajapati their leader.” and develops toward “Having heard the imperishable words spoken by his grandfather, Lord Brahma, Indrajit (Meghanada then responded).”

This passage begins with “Then the mighty Meghanada spoke to Brahma, who was present there, saying, "Let it be heard, or may there be success in releasing Indra."” and develops toward “Then, I created a woman named Ahalya, endowed with qualities of beauty, for "Hala" here means ugliness or deformity, and "Halya" is that which arises…”

This passage begins with “Because no ugliness exists in her, she is renowned as Ahalya, and O Shakra, I thus established her name as Ahalya.” and develops toward “The sage then told you that whoever becomes the king of gods will not remain permanent, as this curse has been uttered by him.”

This passage begins with “Having rebuked his wife, the great ascetic then said, "O ill-behaved one, disappear from the vicinity of my hermitage!"” and develops toward “You must quickly perform a Vaishnava sacrifice with great concentration, and purified by that sacrifice, you will then ascend to heaven.”

This passage begins with “O king of gods, your son did not perish in the great battle; he was taken and kept safe by Aryaka in the great ocean.” and develops toward “O Rama, thus was born Ravana, a tormentor of the world, by whom, along with his son, Shakra, the lord of gods, was conquered in battle. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 31
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, the greatly effulgent Rama, filled with wonder, again spoke words to Agastya, the best among sages, bowing down to him.” and develops toward “Thus addressed by Ravana, those very wise ministers informed the lord of Rakshasas about the king's absence.”

This passage begins with “Having heard from the citizens that Arjuna had departed, Ravana, the son of Vishravas, also departed and came to the Vindhya mountain, which resembled the Himalayas.” and develops toward “Ravana, having entered the Narmada as if embracing a beloved woman, then sat on her beautiful sandbank, which was frequented by various sages.”

This passage begins with “The foremost of rakshasas, Ravana, then sat with his ministers who were seated nearby, mistakenly declaring the Narmada to be the Ganga.” and develops toward “Then, those mighty Rākṣasas, having bathed in the Narmada and emerged from the water, brought flowers for Ravana's offering.”

This passage begins with “On the charming sandbank of the Narmada, which shone like a white cloud, the Rākṣasas swiftly created a mountain of flowers in a moment.” and develops toward “Then, having worshipped Shiva, who removes the distress of the virtuous, is the supreme boon, grants boons, and is adorned with the moon's rays,…”

chapters.name: Sarga 32
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Not far from the place on the Narmada's sandbank where the very fierce lord of Rakshasas (Ravana) was performing his flower offering, another event was unfolding.” and develops toward “Those two heroes, the brothers Shuka and Sarana, ordered by Ravana, set out facing west, having ascended into the sky.”

This passage begins with “Having gone only half a Yojana, those two night-wanderers saw a man playing in the water with a woman.” and develops toward “The king of Rākṣasas, Ravana, there saw that King Arjuna, the king of men, surrounded by women, just like an elephant surrounded by female elephants.”

This passage begins with “He, the king of Rākṣasas, arrogant with his power, had his eyes red with anger.” and develops toward “Ravana's ministers, Prahasta, Shuka, and Sarana, enraged, were indeed destroying Kartavirya's army with their fierce might.”

This passage begins with “That deed of Ravana and his ministers was indeed reported to Arjuna, who was then engaged in sport, by the gatekeepers.” and develops toward “Having seen Prahasta fallen, Maricha, Shuka, Sarana, along with Mahodara and Dhumraksha, indeed fled from the battlefield.”

This passage begins with “After his ministers had retreated and Prahasta was killed, Ravana quickly rushed towards Kartavirya Arjuna, the best among kings.” and develops toward “Then, the enraged Arjuna, with all his might, hurled that mace at Ravana's broad chest, between his breasts.”

This passage begins with “That mace, striking Ravana's chest which was protected by boons, split into two and fell to the ground as if it were weak, despite its full force.” and develops toward “Then, after frightening the Rakshasas, Kartavirya Arjuna seized Ravana and entered the city, surrounded by his friends.”

This passage begins with “Then, Arjuna, resembling Indra, entered that city, being showered with heaps of flowers and unbroken rice grains by Brahmins and citizens, just as the thousand-eyed Indra entered after…” and develops toward “Then, Arjuna, resembling Indra, entered that city, being showered with heaps of flowers and unbroken rice grains by Brahmins and citizens, just as…”

chapters.name: Sarga 33
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The capture of Ravana was as futile as trying to grasp the wind, and Pulastya heard the gods speaking of this in heaven.” and develops toward “O Brahmana, this kingdom, these sons, these wives, and we ourselves are at your disposal; please command us what we should do or what task is to be…”

This passage begins with “Having inquired about the welfare of that king, Kartavirya Arjuna, regarding his dharma, sacred fires, and sons, sage Pulastya then spoke to him.” and develops toward “Then, that king of the flesh-eaters, having obtained a truce from Sahasrabahu, again caused the slaughter of kings and roamed all over the earth with…”

chapters.name: Sarga 34
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Indeed, Ravana, the lord of Rakshasas, having been released by Arjuna and treated in such a manner, roamed all over the earth undeterred.” and develops toward “There, Ravana saw Valin, who resembled a golden mountain with a face like the rising sun, intently engaged in his twilight prayers.”

This passage begins with “Then, Ravana, dark like collyrium, descended from the Pushpaka chariot and quickly approached Valin with silent steps, intending to seize him.” and develops toward “Vali, being followed by those Rakshasas in the middle of the sky, shone like the sun in the sky being followed by masses of clouds.”

This passage begins with “Those chief Rakshasas, unable to reach Vali due to his immense speed, became exhausted and stopped.” and develops toward “Ravana addressed Bali, saying, "O king of monkeys, resembling Mahendra, I am Ravana, the king of Rakshasas, who arrived here desirous of battle, and…”

This passage begins with “Ravana exclaimed, "Oh, what strength, what valor, and what profundity, by which I was seized like an animal and carried around all four oceans!"” and develops toward “O Rama, Vali possessed supreme and unequalled strength, yet even he was completely destroyed by you, just as a moth is consumed by fire. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 35
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then Rama, with folded hands and full of humility, asked the sage (Agastya) in the hermitage of the southern direction, speaking these meaningful words.” and develops toward “I believe Hanuman did not know his own strength, because he saw Sugriva, who was dear to him as life, suffering.”

This passage begins with “O revered one, worshipped by gods, please tell me all about the great-minded Hanuman in detail and truly.” and develops toward “The child, facing the morning sun, soared through the middle of the sky like an embodied morning sun, desirous of seizing the sun itself.”

This passage begins with “Indeed, a very great wonder arose among the gods, demons, and yakshas as this child Hanumān soared through the sky.” and develops toward “Having heard Rahu's words, Vasava (Indra), filled with agitation, sprang up, abandoning his seat and still wearing his golden garland.”

This passage begins with “Then (Indra's elephant) appeared, resembling the peak of Kailasa, four-tusked, with ichor flowing, adorned with ornaments, tall, and making a loud sound with its golden bells.” and develops toward “When the child, distressed by the thunderbolt's strike, fell, the wind god Vayu became angry at Indra, foreseeing harm to all creatures.”

This passage begins with “The lord Vayu, having withdrawn his circulation from among all creatures, took his child and entered a cave.” and develops toward “The bodiless Vayu moves within and protects all bodies, for indeed, a body without Vayu becomes equal to a mere log of wood.”

This passage begins with “Vayu is the life-breath, Vayu is happiness, and Vayu is this entire world, for the world finds no happiness when completely abandoned by Vayu.” and develops toward “Then, the four-faced Brahma, seeing the son, who shone with the radiance of the sun, fire, and gold, placed on Vayu's lap, then showed compassion…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, Vayu, distressed by the injury to his son, having seen the grandfather Brahma, picked up that child and stood up in front of the creator.” and develops toward “I will grant him a supreme and wonderful boon: from this moment forward, he will be invulnerable to my thunderbolt.”

This passage begins with “There, the revered Sun-god, dispeller of darkness, then declared, "I bestow upon him a hundredth part of my own radiance."” and develops toward “Hanuman will possess the ability to assume any form, move anywhere at will, be the foremost among leapers, have unhindered movement, and achieve…”

This passage begins with “In battle, Hanuman will perform awe-inspiring deeds that will lead to the destruction of Ravana and be pleasing to Rama.” and develops toward “Then, this Hanuman, having lost his splendor and vigor by the power of the great sages' words, attained a gentle state and wandered only in those…”

This passage begins with “Then, there was a king named Ṛkṣarajas, the father of Vali and Sugriva, who was the king of all monkeys and shone with the radiance of the sun.” and develops toward “Indeed, he rivals the preceptor of the gods (Brihaspati) in all branches of knowledge and in the practice of austerities, and this knower of the nine…”

This passage begins with “Who indeed will be able to stand before Hanuman, who is like the ocean wishing to overflow, like fire wishing to burn the worlds, or like Yama at the end of an eon?” and develops toward “Therefore, you should constantly come here assembled together; and Agastya and other sages of firm vows, having heard that (request)...”

This passage begins with “...and having said "So be it" to him, they began to depart.” and develops toward “Then, when the sun had set, having dismissed the kings and monkeys, the best among men, Rama, performed his evening prayers according to ritual, and…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When Rama, whose soul was known for righteousness, had been consecrated, the first night, which increased the joy of the citizens, passed.” and develops toward “Thousands of humble men, with folded hands, approached the risen great-souled Rama, offering him water in pure vessels.”

This passage begins with “Having purified himself with ablutions and offered oblations to the sacred fire at the appropriate time, he quickly went to the holy temple, which was traditionally frequented by the…” and develops toward “To those who were seated, various very sweet stories imbued with righteousness were narrated by great souls who were learned in the Puranas.…”

This passage begins with “Yudhājit then said to Rama regarding his departure, "So be it; let the jewels and wealth indeed be inexhaustible in you alone."” and develops toward “In that matter, I was merely an instrument; it was by your valor that Ravana, along with his retinue, sons, ministers, and relatives, was killed in…”

This passage begins with “The great-souled Rama, filled with joy, received all those gifts and then gave them to King Sugriva, who had accomplished great deeds.” and develops toward “Rama declared that he, a dweller of the forest, was rescued from distress by them, and King Sugriva was blessed to have them, the best among friends.”

This passage begins with “Having spoken thus, Rama, the best among men, gave them ornaments and very valuable diamonds as was fitting, and also embraced them.” and develops toward “While they were experiencing supreme joy in the beautiful city of Ikshvaku, time passed happily for them due to Rama's delightful actions. इत्यार्षे…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While those bears, monkeys, and Rakshasas were thus dwelling, the greatly effulgent Rama then spoke these words to Sugriva.” and develops toward “Having heard Rama's words, the bears, monkeys, and rakshasas repeatedly praised him, exclaiming "Excellent! Excellent!"”

This passage begins with “O mighty-armed Rama, your intellect and valor are truly wonderful, and your supreme sweetness is eternal, like that of the Self-born Brahma.” and develops toward “With that great necklace fastened on his chest, Hanuman shone like a magnificent golden mountain whose peak is adorned by the moon.”

This passage begins with “Having heard these words from Rama, those mighty monkeys repeatedly rose, bowed their heads at his feet, and then departed.” and develops toward “Then, those Rakshasas, bears, and monkeys, having bowed down to Rama, the enhancer of the Raghu dynasty, returned to their respective abodes with…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “And the mighty-armed Rama, having dismissed the bears, monkeys, and Rakshasas, was very happy and rejoiced greatly along with his brothers.” and develops toward “If it is so, then welcome to you, O best of aerial cars, Pushpaka; due to the favor of Kubera, no impropriety shall befall us.”

This passage begins with “Then, the mighty-armed Rama worshipped the Pushpaka chariot with parched grains, flowers, and fragrant incense.” and develops toward “Having heard these very sweet words spoken by Bharata, Rama, the best of kings, became filled with joy. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये…”

This passage begins with “The area was embellished with diverse enclosures and rock surfaces, and within that forest region, there were spots that shimmered like lapis lazuli gems.” and develops toward “Rama, filled with joy, thus shone with splendor like Vasishtha seated with Arundhati, with Sita resembling a celestial nymph.”

This passage begins with “Rama, like a god, delighted Sita day after day, and thus Sita and Raghava sported together for a long time.” and develops toward “After speaking thus to Sita, Janaka's daughter, Rama, surrounded by his friends, went out to the middle apartment. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

This passage begins with “O King, listen to how the citizens speak of good and bad things in the squares, markets, streets, forests, and gardens.” and develops toward “Having heard the words uttered by all, Rama, the destroyer of enemies, then dismissed his friends. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये…”

This passage begins with “"You, quickly usher the princes into my presence, for my life is in them, and these are my dear vital breaths."” and develops toward “As Rama spoke thus, all the kings, though very attentive, became anxious in their minds, wondering what the king would say next. इत्यार्षे…”

This passage begins with “Infamy is condemned by the gods, while fame is honored in all worlds, and indeed, all great-souled individuals undertake their endeavors for the sake of renown.” and develops toward “Having spoken thus, Rama, the righteous one, with his face covered in tears, entered, surrounded by his brothers.”

This passage begins with “With his heart engulfed by sorrow, he sighed like an elephant. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये श्रीमदुत्तरकाण्डे पञ्चचत्वारिंशः सर्गः ।। 45 ।। Thus ends the forty-fifth sarga…” and develops toward “With his heart engulfed by sorrow, he sighed like an elephant. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये श्रीमदुत्तरकाण्डे पञ्चचत्वारिंशः सर्गः…”

This passage begins with “Then Sita said to Lakshmana, the augmenter of prosperity, "O delight of the Raghus, I indeed see many inauspicious signs."” and develops toward “Observing the situation, the distressed Lakshmana wept loudly with a great sound.”

This passage begins with “But Sita, a knower of dharma and greatly agitated upon seeing Lakshmana distressed, asked him, "Why are you weeping like this?"” and develops toward “Desiring to cross the Ganga, Lakshmana boarded the auspicious boat and then attentively ferried Sita across the Ganga. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

This passage begins with “Lakshmana informs Sita that she, though faultless in his presence, has been abandoned by the king who fears public scandal, and this command must be accepted by her without question.” and develops toward “O goddess, by adopting the state of a devoted wife and always keeping Rama in your heart, your supreme welfare will be achieved. इत्यार्षे…”

This passage begins with “O hero, I have been abandoned by you, who fears ill-repute among people; whatever blame arises from public criticism, I shall endure it, for you are indeed my ultimate refuge.” and develops toward “Lakshmana, having repeatedly turned back to see Sita struggling helplessly on the other bank, then departed.”

This passage begins with “As Sita, agitated, repeatedly looked at the chariot and Lakshmana moving far away, sorrow overwhelmed her.” and develops toward “She, glorious and renowned, bowed down by the immense sorrow and finding no protector, cried loudly in the forest filled with peacock calls, being…”

chapters.name: Sarga 49
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Seeing Sita crying there, the hermit boys ran to where the revered Valmiki, of keen intellect, resided.” and develops toward “Valmiki further stated, "You, as you were coming, were known to me through my meditation on dharma, and indeed, all the reasons for your arrival have…”

This passage begins with “O highly fortunate one, your true nature is indeed known to me, and everything that exists in the three worlds is also known to me.” and develops toward “And having repeatedly entrusted Vaidehi, the greatly renowned and great ascetic Valmiki, surrounded by his disciples, returned to his own hermitage.…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Indeed, having seen Maithili Sita led into the hermitage, Lakshmana, with a distressed mind, experienced terrible sorrow.” and develops toward “The great and righteous Rama will indeed abandon you, Maithili, and both Shatrughna and Bharata, after a long period of time.”

This passage begins with “O Lakshmana, this statement, which Durvasa indeed spoke to the king and which the king then spoke to you all, should not be revealed to you or even to Bharata.” and develops toward “Having heard his profound and significant words, Lakshmana then spoke to the charioteer, saying, "Tell me the truth.". इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

This passage begins with “Seeing the demons protected by her, the enraged lord of gods (Vishnu) cut off the head of Bhrigu's wife with his sharp-edged discus.” and develops toward “Two sons will indeed be born to Rama through Sita, but they will be born elsewhere and not in Ayodhya; this is the truth, without a doubt.”

This passage begins with “Then, Rama will anoint Sita's two sons (as kings).” and develops toward “Then, as the charioteer and Lakshmana were conversing on the path, the sun set, and they stayed in Keshini for the night. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having stayed that night in Keshini, Lakshmana, the delight of the Raghus, rose again in the morning and then departed.” and develops toward “Therefore, one should not have excessive attachment to sons, wives, friends, or wealth, for separation from them is indeed inevitable.”

This passage begins with “O Kakutstha, you are surely capable of controlling your own self and indeed all worlds by your mind, so how much more easily can you control your own sorrow?” and develops toward “O gentle Lakshmana, relief has come, and my sorrow has been dispelled; I have been comforted by your very pleasing words. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard that exceedingly wonderful speech of Lakshmana, Rama became very pleased and indeed spoke these words.” and develops toward “Then that Brahmana called her by her own name, saying, "Come, Shabala!" and the cow indeed heard his voice.”

This passage begins with “Having recognized the voice of that hungry Brahmana, the cow indeed followed him from behind as he, radiant like fire, walked away.” and develops toward “In this manner, that king experiences that very terrible curse.”

This passage begins with “Indeed, the crowding of petitioners leads to harm for kings; therefore, let the petitioners quickly approach me for an audience.” and develops toward “Indeed, the fruit of a well-done work is not lost, O king; therefore, O son of Sumitra, go and wait for any person who has business. इत्यार्षे…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard Rama's speech, Lakshmana, who knew the highest truth, spoke words with folded hands to Rama, who was radiant with splendor.” and develops toward “Let fragrant flowers always be placed in them, surrounding the area for me to a distance of one and a half Yojanas.”

This passage begins with “Having made this arrangement, the king then instructed his son, "O son, ever devoted to righteousness, protect your subjects according to the duty of a Kshatriya."” and develops toward “Having thus just entered that well adorned with great jewels, the great-souled king then fulfilled the curse that had indeed been uttered with anger…”

chapters.name: Sarga 55
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “I have told you this detailed account of Nṛga's curse; if you have a desire to listen, then hear another story here.” and develops toward “King Nimi, the lord of men, however, gathered those brahmins and performed a sacrifice near his own city, on the side of the Himalayas, undertaking…”
