chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard Rama's words, Lakshmana, the destroyer of enemy heroes, spoke with folded hands to the radiant Rama.” and develops toward “At that very time, Mitra also performed the role of Varuna, accompanied by the Ocean of Milk and being worshipped by the best of gods.”

This passage begins with “At that very time, Urvashi, the foremost Apsara, arrived at that place by chance, accompanied by her friends.” and develops toward “Due to this misdeed, you, sullied by my wrath, will reside in the human world for some time.”

This passage begins with “Go today to Pururavas, the royal sage, son of Budha and king of Kashi, O ill-minded one, for he will be your husband.” and develops toward “That Urvashi, with beautiful teeth, eyes, and eyebrows, then went to the earth due to that curse, resided there for many years, and returned to…”

chapters.name: Sarga 57
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard that divine-like and amazing story, Lakshmana was greatly pleased and spoke these words to Rama.” and develops toward “Then, when the sacrifice was concluded, Bhrigu said: O king, I am pleased with you, and I shall restore your consciousness.”

This passage begins with “And all the gods, being very pleased, thus spoke to Nimi's spirit: "O royal sage, choose a boon; where should your mind be fixed?"” and develops toward “Thus, the ancestor of Janaka, the king of Videha, was the greatly effulgent Mithi, from whom this region became known as Maithila. इत्यार्षे…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While Rama was speaking thus, Lakshmana, the destroyer of enemy heroes, replied to the great-souled Rama, who was shining as if blazing with splendor.” and develops toward “Yadu questioned his mother, Devayani, born in the lineage of the divine Bhrigu whose deeds are unblemished, asking why she endures such deep-seated…”

This passage begins with “Yadu proposed to his mother, "O goddess, let us both enter the fire together, while the king enjoys himself with the Daitya's daughter for many nights."” and develops toward “Having thus spoken and comforted his daughter, the highly renowned Brahmarshi Chyavana, the son of Bhrigu, returned to his own abode.”

This passage begins with “Having thus spoken, the foremost Brahmin Shukra, after consoling his daughter Devayani and cursing Yayati, the son of Nahusha, departed again with a splendor like the sun. इत्यार्षे…” and develops toward “Having thus spoken, the foremost Brahmin Shukra, after consoling his daughter Devayani and cursing Yayati, the son of Nahusha, departed again with a…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard that Ushanas was angry, the distressed son of Nahusha, Yayati, then, having immediately obtained extreme old age, spoke these words to Yadu.” and develops toward “O mighty-armed one, I am pleased by your acceptance of my command, and I will affectionately anoint you as the king of men.”

This passage begins with “Having thus spoken to his son Puru, King Yayati, the son of Nahusha, then spoke enraged words to Devayani's son.” and develops toward “As Rāma, whose face was like the moon, spoke thus, the sky at that time became very sparse with stars, and the eastern direction shone, reddened by…”

chapters.name: Sarga 60
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “As Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa thus conversed, the spring night arrived, which was neither cold nor hot.” and develops toward “Having seen the sages seated there, Rama, the conqueror of enemy cities, reverently and with folded hands, spoke these words.”

This passage begins with “He asked what the purpose of their visit was and what he could do for them attentively, declaring himself to be at the command of the great sages, ready to fulfill all their desires easily.” and develops toward “But you, out of respect for Brahmins, made this vow without considering the difficulty, and therefore you are indeed the one who will accomplish it,…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When the sages spoke thus, Rama replied, "O sages, tell me what the task is; first of all, let your fear depart."” and develops toward “O Lord, may this excellent spear always remain with my lineage, for you are indeed the lord of the gods.”

This passage begins with “To Madhu, who was speaking thus, the greatly effulgent god Shiva, the lord of all beings, replied, "This will not be so."” and develops toward “Having heard that Ravana, along with his army and vehicles, has been killed, we know no other king on earth as a protector, and therefore, O dear…”

This passage begins with “O Rama of undiminished valor, you are capable of removing this cause of fear that has arisen and been reported, so please fulfill that wish. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये…” and develops toward “O Rama of undiminished valor, you are capable of removing this cause of fear that has arisen and been reported, so please fulfill that wish.…”

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having quickly sent forth all those arrangements completely, Rama, Bharata's elder brother, then released the black-spotted horse, which was endowed with auspicious marks, for the sacrifice.” and develops toward “Before a word could even emerge from the lips of the supplicants, it was already seen to be given by the monkeys and rakshasas.”

This passage begins with “In that excellent sacrifice of the king, which was filled with happy and well-nourished people, there was no one who was dirty, poor, or emaciated.” and develops toward “Such a sacrifice of the lion among kings, endowed with all good qualities, continued for more than a year without diminishing. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While that supremely wonderful sacrifice was thus going on, Valmiki, the foremost among sages, quickly arrived with his disciples.” and develops toward “Do not show even a little greed for wealth, for what use is wealth to those who live in hermitages and subsist on fruits and roots?”

This passage begins with “If Rama, the descendant of Kakutstha, asks, "Whose boys are you two?", then tell the king, "We are the disciples of Valmiki."” and develops toward “Then, those two princes, having fixed those wonderful words spoken by the sage in their hearts, passed the night with great eagerness, just as the…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When the night dawned, after bathing and performing their fire oblations, the two boys sang the entire Ramayana as previously narrated by the sage Valmiki in that assembly.” and develops toward “All of them, from all sides, said this to each other: "Both are similar to Rama, as if one image has arisen from another."”

This passage begins with “If they did not have matted hair and were not wearing bark garments, we would not perceive any difference between the two singers and Rama.” and develops toward “The sons of the sage then spoke words to Rama, who was questioning them.”

This passage begins with “The revered Valmiki, the author, has arrived at this sacrificial assembly, and it is by him that this entire story has been revealed to you.” and develops toward “He heard that song, which was endowed with rhythm and tempo, contained cantos, was rich in notes and words, and harmonized with string instruments…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Rama thus heard that supremely auspicious song for many days, accompanied by sages, kings, and monkeys.” and develops toward “Then, greatly delighted after hearing the words of the great soul Valmiki, Rama addressed the sages and kings assembled there.”

This passage begins with “O revered sages with your disciples, and O kings with your followers, let all who desire witness Sita's oath.” and develops toward “Having thus deliberated well and made the decision for the oath on the morrow, that great-souled king, the lion among kings and one of great power,…”

chapters.name: Sarga 96
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When that night had dawned, King Rama, the greatly effulgent one, having gone to the sacrificial enclosure, summoned all the sages.” and develops toward “Upon seeing Sita following Valmiki, appearing like a Veda following Brahma, a great acclamation of "excellent" arose from the crowd.”

This passage begins with “Then, a tumultuous clamor arose from all those whose minds were agitated by the vast sorrow born of their distress.” and develops toward “Therefore, O son of the best among men, this Sita of pure nature, whom you, whose mind was defiled by public scandal, abandoned even though she was…”

chapters.name: Sarga 97
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Indeed, when spoken to thus by Valmiki, Rama, having seen Sita of excellent complexion in the midst of the people, replied with folded hands.” and develops toward “Then, the auspicious, sacred, divinely fragrant, and delightful wind, the best among gods (Vayu), delighted that multitude of people from all sides.”

This passage begins with “People gathered from all kingdoms beheld that wonderful and inconceivable sight, just as they would have in the Krita Yuga.” and develops toward “All those sages and kings, who were like tigers among men, present in the sacrificial enclosure, did not cease their astonishment.”

This passage begins with “All stationary and moving beings in the sky and on the earth, along with the huge-bodied demons and the lords of serpents in the netherworld, were present.” and develops toward “Upon witnessing Sita's entry, there was a great gathering of beings, and for a moment, the entire world seemed exceedingly bewildered. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 98
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When Vaidehi entered the netherworld, all the monkeys and sages present before Rama cried out "Excellent! Excellent!"” and develops toward “O virtuous Rama, you should not grieve; O destroyer of enemies, remember your ancient divine nature and the sacred incantation.”

This passage begins with “O mighty-armed and unconquerable one, I would indeed remind you of your unsurpassed divine nature; at this very moment, remember your divine birth as Vishnu.” and develops toward “And those great-souled, greatly powerful sages from Brahma's world, who were desirous to hear the Uttara Kanda and the future events concerning Rama,…”

This passage begins with “Then, the most effulgent Rama, having heard the auspicious words spoken by Brahma, the god of gods, said this to Valmiki.” and develops toward “Having made this decision and accepted Kusha and Lava, Rama dismissed the crowd and returned to his hermitage, where that night passed as he grieved…”

chapters.name: Sarga 99
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When the night dawned, Rama assembled the great sages and instructed his sons, Kusha and Lava, to sing the Ramayana fearlessly.” and develops toward “Day by day, the bears, monkeys, and rakshasas, abiding by Rama's command, continued to please King Rama.”

This passage begins with “The rain-god rained at the proper time, there was an abundance of food, the directions were clear, and the city and its districts were filled with happy and well-nourished people.” and develops toward “Thus, many thousands of years passed happily for him who was always increasing various kinds of righteousness through sacrifices. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 100
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, after some time, King Yudhājit of Kekaya sent his own preceptor, Gārgya, the son of Angiras, a Brahmarshi of immeasurable splendor, to the great-souled Rama.” and develops toward “O mighty-armed Rama, descendant of Kakutstha, having conquered those Gandharvas, establish your own well-governed and auspicious city in that…”

This passage begins with “No one else has access to that exceedingly beautiful region; O mighty-armed one, may this proposal please you, for I would never speak anything harmful to you.” and develops toward “Many thousands of lions, tigers, boars, and various birds marched in front of the army.”

This passage begins with “After staying on the road for one and a half months, the army, healthy and filled with joyful and well-nourished people, arrived in Kekaya. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये…” and develops toward “After staying on the road for one and a half months, the army, healthy and filled with joyful and well-nourished people, arrived in Kekaya. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard that Commander Bharata had arrived, the King of Kekaya, accompanied by Yudhajit and Gargya, attained great joy.” and develops toward “These cities were filled with heaps of wealth and jewels, adorned with forests, and their extensive qualities caused mutual rivalry and competition.”

This passage begins with “Both cities were very beautiful in appearance, characterized by blameless transactions, full of gardens and conveyances, and had well-divided inner markets.” and develops toward “Bharata reported the full account of the excellent slaying of the Gandharvas and the establishment of the region, and upon hearing this, Rama was…”

chapters.name: Sarga 102
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having heard that, Rama, along with his brothers, felt great joy and then spoke amazing words to Lakshmana.” and develops toward “Lakshmana, the son of Sumitra, followed Angada, and Bharata became Chandraketu's close supporter.”

This passage begins with “Lakshmana, having resided for a year in Angada's territory until his unconquerable son was established, then returned to Ayodhya.” and develops toward “Having spent their time with fulfilled desires, endowed with prosperity, and well-established in the city of dharma, the three brothers shone like…”

chapters.name: Sarga 103
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, after some time, while Rama was abiding devoted to righteousness, Time, in the form of an ascetic, approached the royal gate.” and develops toward “Urged by the lion among kings, the sage spoke these words: "This secret must be spoken if you indeed desire what is beneficial."”

This passage begins with “Whoever listens or looks will be punishable by death by you, if you truly desire to heed the words of the chief of sages.” and develops toward “Rama urged the sage to speak without hesitation whatever message he desired or was intent upon, for a similar thought was also present in Rama's…”

chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “The sage then began, asking Rama to listen, and revealed that he had been sent by the divine grandfather Brahma for a specific purpose.” and develops toward “Formerly, you yourself fixed the duration of your dwelling (on earth) for ten thousand and ten hundred years.”

This passage begins with “O best among men, you, the mind-born son, have completed your lifespan among humans here, and now it is time for you to return to my proximity.” and develops toward “You are indeed understood and your time has arrived, there is no doubt in my mind about that; for I must indeed act in all duties, obedient to the…”

chapters.name: Sarga 105
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “While those two were thus conversing, the revered sage Durvasa, desiring an audience with Rama, approached the royal gate.” and develops toward “Having heard those words spoken by Rama, the best of sages, Durvasa, replied to the lord, saying, "O lover of righteousness, listen."”

This passage begins with “O sinless one, today marks the completion of my thousand-year penance, and so I desire whatever food is ready to break my fast.” and develops toward “Then, the greatly renowned Rama, having intellectually ascertained the words of Kala and deciding that "this is not (to be avoided)", remained…”

chapters.name: Sarga 106
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Seeing Rama with his face downcast and distressed, like the moon submerged in sorrow, Lakshmana, with a firm resolve, spoke gentle words to him.” and develops toward “Having heard the words full of righteousness and purpose from those assembled in the midst of the council, Rama spoke to Lakshmana.”

This passage begins with “O son of Sumitra, I shall abandon you, lest there be a transgression of dharma, for abandonment or killing is prescribed, and for the virtuous, both are considered equal.” and develops toward “Then, having seen the fourth part of Vishnu (Lakshmana) arrive, all the best of gods, along with the great sages, were greatly delighted and…”

chapters.name: Sarga 107
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having sent Lakshmana away, Rama, filled with sorrow and grief, spoke these words to his chief priest, ministers, and citizens.” and develops toward “Then all the subjects spoke to Rama, saying, "We will follow you, wherever you, Rama, will go."”

This passage begins with “O Rama, if you truly have unsurpassed affection and love for your citizens, then let us all, with our children and wives, accompany you on your path.” and develops toward “After consecrating his heroic sons and establishing them in their cities, he then dispatched messengers to the great-souled Shatrughna. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 108
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Those messengers, urged by Rama's command and swift in their journey, quickly set forth for Mathura and did not make any halts on the way.” and develops toward “He saw the great-souled one, shining like fire, clad in fine silken garments, accompanied by immortal sages.”

This passage begins with “Then, having saluted Rama, the knower of Dharma, he spoke with folded hands and controlled senses, contemplating only righteousness.” and develops toward “O lord of men, I have come after consecrating the heroic Angada; O king, know me to be resolute in following you.”

This passage begins with “Having heard his words, Rama, the best among those who delight, then spoke to the king of monkeys, reflecting upon their friendship.” and develops toward “As long as your purifying story spreads throughout the world, I shall remain on earth, obeying your command.”

This passage begins with “Having thus spoken to the aged Jambavan, the son of Brahma, and also to Mainda and Dvivida, making a total of five including Jambavan.” and develops toward “Having thus spoken to all those bears and monkeys, Rama, the descendant of Kakutstha, told them, "Certainly, go with me as you desire.". इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 109
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “When the night had dawned, the broad-chested, highly renowned, lotus-eyed Rama then spoke to his chief priest.” and develops toward “All those great-souled individuals, having assembled with their sacred fires, sons, and wives, followed the great-minded Rama.”

This passage begins with “All the ministers and groups of servants, along with their sons, animals, relatives, and followers, joyfully followed Rama.” and develops toward “Not even the slightest breathing creature was seen in Ayodhya, as all beings, even those of lower species, were devoted followers of Rama. इत्यार्षे…”

chapters.name: Sarga 110
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Having traveled one and a half yojanas, Rama saw the sacred-watered Sarayu river, which flowed westward.” and develops toward “Having heard and understood the words of his grandfather Brahma regarding Visalakshi (Sita), the great-minded Rama, along with his body and younger…”

This passage begins with “Then, the gods, including the Sadhyas, the Maruts, Indra, and those led by Agni, worshipped the deity who had become one with Vishnu.” and develops toward “Whoever living being, greatly delighted, plunged into the water, abandoned their human body and ascended a celestial chariot.”

This passage begins with “And hundreds of animals, having attained the water of Sarayu, went to heaven with radiant bodies, becoming like shining gods with divine forms.” and develops toward “Thereafter, the preceptor of the worlds, Rama, having established all those who had gathered in heaven, went to the great heaven along with the…”

chapters.name: Sarga 111
chapters.summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “This is the complete narrative, including the Uttara Kanda, revered by Brahma, known as the Ramayana, and composed as the foremost work by Valmiki.” and develops toward “In this world, whoever has listened to the Ramayana is considered to have visited holy places like Prayaga, rivers like Ganga, forests like Naimisha,…”

This passage begins with “One who donates a load of gold in Kurukshetra during a solar eclipse and one who listens to the Ramayana in this world are both considered equal in merit.” and develops toward “Whoever listens to even a quarter of a verse or a single word of the Ramayana with devotion attains the abode of Brahma and is always worshipped by…”

This passage begins with “Thus is this ancient narrative; may welfare be to you; proclaim it confidently, and may the strength of Vishnu increase. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे श्रीमद्वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये…” and develops toward “Thus is this ancient narrative; may welfare be to you; proclaim it confidently, and may the strength of Vishnu increase. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे…”
